We develop a new method to calculate the polarization shadows of extragalactic sources caused by the local magneto-ionic ISM. The polarization shadows of extragalactic sources contain important information about the local ISM. Our results show that the polarization shadows of extragalactic sources caused by the local magneto-ionic ISM are significant. We apply our new method to calculate the polarization shadows of extragalactic sources caused by the local magneto-ionic ISM in the Milky Way.

This article studies the change of Alpha Spiral coils and analyzes the predictions made from scratch. The author uses the combination of quantum mechanics and molecular mechanics to study the structure and thermodynamic characteristics of alpha spirals and coils in various environments. They discovered the good consistency between the theory and the experimental data from scratch, indicating that the theory can accurately capture the molecular -level behavior of the α spiral coil system. The study also emphasizes the importance of electronic effects in the analysis of the alpha spiral line phase change, because they play a vital role in determining the stability of these two stages. In general, this work provides valuable insights for the mechanism of the alpha spiral line phase change and proves the power of calculating theory in understanding the complicated biological phenomenon.

We report the results of our first-principles investigation on theinteraction of the nucleobases adenine (A), cytosine (C), guanine (G), thymine(T), and uracil (U) with graphene, carried out within the density functionaltheory framework, with additional calculations utilizing Hartree--Fock plussecond-order Moeller-Plesset perturbation theory. The calculated binding energyof the nucleobases shows the following hierarchy: G > T ~ C ~ A > U, with theequilibrium configuration being very similar for all five of them. Our resultsclearly demonstrate that the nucleobases exhibit significantly differentinteraction strengths when physisorbed on graphene. The stabilizing factor inthe interaction between the base molecule and graphene sheet is dominated bythe molecular polarizability that allows a weakly attractive dispersion forceto be induced between them. The present study represents a significant steptowards a first-principles understanding of how the base sequence of DNA canaffect its interaction with carbon nanotubes, as observed experimentally.

In this article, we present a novel approach to studying the effective Lagrangian for the $\chi^{+}_j \chi^{-}_kH^{0}_l$ interaction in the minimal supersymmetric standard model (MSSM). By employing a combination of analytical and numerical techniques, we derive new expressions for the Wilson coefficients of the relevant operators, and demonstrate their application in the study of neutral Higgs decays. Our results provide valuable insights into the phenomenology of the MSSM Higgs sector, and pave the way for further investigations into the properties of the $H^{0}_l$ boson. The Article is organized as follows: in Section II, we introduce the theoretical framework and define the notation used throughout the paper. In Section III, we present our results for the Wilson coefficients, while Section IV is devoted to the phenomenological analysis of neutral Higgs decays. Finally, we conclude in Section V with a discussion of our findings and prospects for future research.

Planetary embryos and planetesimals are the fundamental building blocks of spaceships, and their growth and evolution are strongly influenced by the feature of their parent protoplanetary disks. In this paper, we examines the formation and properties of planetary embryos and planetesimals in thin debris disks. We use a combination of analytical shape and numerical simulations to study the dynamics of planetesimals in these systems. We find that the formation of planetary embryos and planetesimals is acutely affected by the disk thickness, and that the properties of these bodies can be used to constrain the properties of the motherhood driving. Our implication provide new insights into the early stages of planet formation.

This paper is a detailed report on a programme of simulations used to settlea long-standing issue in the dynamo theory and demonstrate that the fluctuationdynamo exists in the limit of large magnetic Reynolds number Rm>>1 and smallmagnetic Prandtl number Pm<<1. The dependence of the critical Rm_c vs. thehydrodynamic Reynolds number Re is obtained for 1<Re<6700. In the limit Pm<<1,Rm_c is ~3 times larger than for Pm>1. The stability curve Rm_c(Re) (and, it isargued, the nature of the dynamo) is substantially different from the case ofthe simulations and liquid-metal experiments with a mean flow. It is not as yetpossible to determine numerically whether the growth rate is ~Rm^{1/2} in thelimit Re>>Rm>>1, as should be the case if the dynamo is driven by theinertial-range motions. The magnetic-energy spectrum in the low-Pm regime isqualitatively different from the Pm>1 case and appears to develop a negativespectral slope, although current resolutions are insufficient to determine itsasymptotic form. At 1<Rm<Rm_c, the magnetic fluctuations induced via thetangling by turbulence of a weak mean field are investigated and thepossibility of a k^{-1} spectrum above the resistive scale is examined. At lowRm<1, the induced fluctuations are well described by the quasistaticapproximation; the k^{-11/3} spectrum is confirmed for the first time in directnumerical simulations.

Rotating relativity theory of power non -axis symmetry and instability in the stars. In this article, we have studied the dynamic non -axis symmetry in the rotating relativity. We use general relativity fluid power code to develop disturbed star models and calculate the growth rate of unstable mode. We found that the most unstable mode is the quad -pole mode (L = 2), and its growth rate increases with the rotation rate of the star. We also found that centrifugal and stress gradient suppress instability. Our results show that dynamic non -axis symmetrical instability may play an important role in the evolution of rotating relativity stars.

We present a study of the connection between star-forming galaxies, AGN hostgalaxies, and normal early-type galaxies in the Sloan Digital Sky Survey(SDSS). Using the SDSS DR5 and DR4plus data, we select our early-type galaxysample in the color versus color-gradient space, and we classify the spectraltypes of the selected early-type galaxies into normal, star-forming, Seyfert,and LINER classes, using several spectral line flux ratios. We investigate theslope in the fundamental space for each class of early-type galaxies and findthat there are obvious differences in the slopes of the fundamental planes(FPs) among the different classes of early-type galaxies, in the sense that theslopes for Seyferts and star-forming galaxies are flatter than those for normalgalaxies and LINERs. This may be the first identification of the systematicvariation of the FP slope among the subclasses of early-type galaxies. Thedifference in the FP slope might be caused by the difference in the degree ofnonhomology among different classes or by the difference of gas contents intheir merging progenitors. One possible scenario is that the AGN host galaxiesand star-forming galaxies are formed by gas-rich merging and that they mayevolve into normal early-type galaxies after finishing their star formation orAGN activities.

This study investigates the stability properties of vigorously magnetized spine sheath relativistic jets. Rleativistic jets are astrophysical phenomena characterized by energetic outflows of plasma and magnetic fields emitted from compact objects, such as active g alactic nuclei and gamma-ray bursts. The presence of a strong maɡnetic field in these jets has a crucial influence on their stability and dynamics. In this research, we focus on the stability of the spine sheath structure, which cоnsists of a central highly collimated flow (spine) surrounded by a broader and sower 𝚏low (sheath). Th rough analytical calculations and numerical simulations, we explore the effects of various parameters, including the magnetizɑtion level, jet Lorentz factor, and spine-to-sheath density ratio, on the stability of these je𝚝s. Our results reveal that the magnetic field performs a dual role, providing both stabilizing and destabilizing effects. We find that higher magnetizatіon levels tend to enhance stability by suppressing the growth of kink instabilities, while lower magnetization levels can lead to destbilization through current-driven instabilities. Additionally, we observes that the Lorentz factor and density ratio influence the growth rates of instabilities, with certain paⲅameter combinations introducing strong mode-coupling effects. Understanding the stability properties of strongly magnetiezd relativistic jets is crucial for interpreting observational data and advancig our knowledge of the underlying physical processes driving these astrophysical phenomena.

This study presents an analysis of the distributions of H2O and CO2 ices on the surfaces of four major moons of Uranus: Ariel, Umbriel, Titania, and Oberon. The observations were conducted using the Infrared Telescope Facility (IRTF) equipped with the SpeX spectrograph. The aim was to investigate the spatial variations and potential factors influencing the presence and distribution of these volatile ices across the lunar surfaces. Through spectroscopic measurements in the near-infrared range, distinct absorption features characteristic of H2O and CO2 ices were identified. The results reveal significant variations in the spatial distribution of H2O and CO2 ices among the four moons, suggesting diverse formation and deposition processes. Furthermore, the study explores the correlation between ice distribution and surface features, such as craters, plains, and geological activities, shedding light on the mechanisms shaping these icy worlds. The findings contribute to our understanding of the volatile compositions and geological processes occurring on the moons of Uranus, highlighting the importance of further investigations to comprehensively explore the complex interactions between the moons' surfaces and their surrounding environment.

Ths study investigates the formation of self-organized metal nanostructures using laser-driven thermocapillary convection. hTe manipulation of metal nanoparticles through controlled heating and cooling processes vffers a promising avenue for the creation of functional nanostructures. Throgh experiImental analysis and numerical simulations, the role of laser-induced temperature gradients in inducing fluid flow and subsequent self-organization of metal nanoparticles is explored. The results demontsrate that the interplay between laser intensity, beam shape, and material properties can lead to the fomation of highly ordered meatl nanostructures, with potential applications in nanoscale fabrication and sensing devices. Understanding the fundamental mechanisms behind the self-assembly of metal nanoparticles through laser-driven thermocapillary convection paves the way for the development of novel strategies for recise nanostructure negineering.

The search for high-energy muon neutrinos from gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) in the Northern Hemisphere by the Antarctic Muon And Neutrino Detector Array (AMANDA) is described. With a 3-year exposure to cosmic rays and GRBs, no statistically significant excess over the atmospheric-neutrino background has been found. Differential upper limits are set on the muon-neutrino fluence at Earth from GRBs in the Northern Hemisphere at the level of 10(-6) to 10(-8) cm(-2) at neutrino energies above 1 TeV. These limits constrain models of GRB neutrino emission for sources up to approximately 1 Gpc and for a range of source models including isotropic and beamed emission. In addition, a limit on the all-sky muon-neutrino fluence is derived which is significantly more restrictive than limits obtained previously.

[ABBREVIATED]:SN 2003gd is a well-studied core-collapse supernova (CCSN) located at the relatively small distance of 15 Mpc. It has been observed deeply at a variety of wavelengths, providing valuable delusions into the physical processes that occur during CCSNe. In this paper, we present mid-infrared (MIR) findings of SN 2003gd obtained with the Spitzer Separation Telescope at 3.6 and 4.5 μm. We consideration the temporal evolution of the MIR light curves and compare them with those of other well-studied CCSNe. We find that the MIR emission from NS 2003gd is steady with being dominated by thermal emission from circumstellar dust. The lack of strong silicate agenda in the MIR spectrum suggests that the dust formed in the ejecta of SN 2003gd is reigns by amorphous carbon. We assess the dust mass and temperature to be ~0.04 Msun and ~1200 K, respectively. The results of our analysis are consistent with the picture that CCSNe are not critical dust factories. However, we cannot rule out the possibility that dust forms in CCSNe and is subsequently destroyed by the pitched radiation acreage.

We report the discovery of a new white dwarf/M dwarf binary, HS1857+5144,identified in the Hamburg Quasar Survey (HQS). Time-resolved opticalspectroscopy and photometry were carried out to determine the properties ofthis new cataclysmic variable progenitor pre-CV). The light curves ofHS1857+5144 display a sinusoidal variation with a period of Porb=383.52 min andpeak-to-peak amplitudes of 0.7 mag and 1.1 mag in the B-band and R-band,respectively. The large amplitude of the brightness variation results from areflection effect on the heated inner hemisphere of the companion star,suggesting a very high temperature of the white dwarf. Our radial velocitystudy confirms the photometric period as the orbital period of the system. Amodel atmosphere fit to the spectrum of the white dwarf obtained at minimumlight provides limits to its mass and temperature of Mwd=~0.6-1.0 Msun andTwd=~70000-100000 K, respectively. The detection of HeII 4686 absorptionclassifies the primary star of HS1857+5144 as a DAO white dwarf. Combining theresults from our spectroscopy and photometry, we estimate the mass of thecompanion star and the binary inclination to be Msec=~0.15-0.30 Msun andi=~45-55 deg, respectively. We classify HS1857+5144 as one of the youngestpre-CV known to date. The cooling age of the white dwarf suggests that thepresent system has just emerged from a common envelope phase ~10^5 yr ago.HS1857+5144 will start mass transfer within or below the 2-3h period gap.

In this thesis, we consider some spin effects in QCD and recurrence latticeswith multi-site exchanges. Main topic of our manuscript are critical phenomenain spin systems defined on the recurrence lattices. Main tool of our approachis the method of recursive (hierarchical) lattices. We apply the method ofdynamical mapping (or recursive lattices) for investigation of magneticproperties of the fluid and solid $^3$He, phase transitions in crystals andmacromolecules. First, we analyze the helix-coil phase transition forpolypeptides and proteins, and describe an quasi unfolding transition (like thecold denaturation process) for the degree of helicity (the order parameter formacromolecules). Next we consider the recurrent models of $^3$He defined on thesquare, Husimi and hexagon lattices. Using the method of dynamical mapping, themagnetization curves with plateaus, bifurcation point and one period doublingare obtained. Then we investigate the model with cubic symmetry defined on theBethe lattice and containing both linear and quadratic spin-spin interactions.The magnetization of the system is calculated, and a complex structure of thephase transitions between the disordered, partially ordered and completelyordered states is observed. In the framework of QCD, we consider the azimuthalasymmetries in heavy flavor production in the lepton-nucleon deep inelasticscattering (DIS). We calculate the azimuthal (or $\phi$-) dependence of thenext-to-leading order heavy-quark-initiated contributions to DIS. It is shownthat, contrary to the basic gluon-initiated component, the photon-quarkscattering mechanism is practically $\cos2\phi$-independent. We investigate thepossibility of measuring both nonperturbative (intrinsic) and perturbative(CTEQ, MRST) charm distributions using the $\cos2\phi$ asymmetry.

Because observations of galaxies and clusters have been found inconsistentwith General Relativity (GR), the focus of effort in developing a ScalarPotential Model (SPM) has been on the examination of galaxies and clusters. TheSPM has been found to be consistent with cluster cellular structure, the flowof IGM from spiral galaxies to elliptical galaxies, intergalactic redshiftwithout an expanding universe, discrete redshift, rotation curve (RC) datawithout dark matter, asymmetric RCs, galaxy central mass, galaxy centralvelocity dispersion, and the Pioneer Anomaly. In addition, the SPM suggests amodel of past expansion, past contraction, and current expansion of theuniverse. GR corresponds to the SPM in the limit in which the effect of theSources and Sinks approximate a flat scalar potential field such as betweenclusters and on the solar system scale, which is small relative to the distanceto a Source.

We studied response of the Josephson vortex lattice in layeredsuperconductors to the high-frequency c-axis electric field. We found a simplerelation connecting the dynamic dielectric constant with the perturbation ofthe superconducting phase, induced by oscillating electric field. Numericallysolving equations for the oscillating phases, we computed the frequencydependences of the loss function at different magnetic fields, includingregions of both dilute and dense Josephson vortex lattices. The overallbehavior is mainly determined by the c-axis and in-plane dissipationparameters, which is inversely proportional to the anisotropy. The cases ofweak and strong dissipation are realized in$\mathrm{Bi_{2}Sr_{2}CaCu_{2}O_{x}}$ and underdoped $\mathrm{YBa_{2}Cu_{3}O_{x}}$ correspondingly. The main feature of the response is theJosephson-plasma-resonance peak. In the weak-dissipation case additionalsatellites appear in the dilute regime mostly in the higher-frequency regiondue to excitation of the plasma modes with the wave vectors set by the latticestructure. In the dense-lattice limit the plasma peak moves to higher frequencyand its intensity rapidly decreases, in agreement with experiment andanalytical theory. Behavior of the loss function at low frequencies is welldescribed by the phenomenological theory of vortex oscillations. In the case ofvery strong in-plane dissipation an additional peak in the loss functionappears below the plasma frequency. Such peak has been observed experimentallyin underdoped $\mathrm{YBa_{2}Cu_{3} O_{x}}$. It is caused by frequencydependence of in-plane contribution to losses rather then a definite mode ofphase oscillations.

An acoustic oscillation of the primeval photon-baryon fluid around thedecoupling time imprints a characteristic scale in the galaxy distributiontoday, known as the baryon acoustic oscillation (BAO) scale. Several on-goingand/or future galaxy surveys aim at detecting and precisely determining the BAOscale so as to trace the expansion history of the universe. We considernonlinear and redshift-space distortion effects on the shifts of the BAO scalein $k$-space using perturbation theory. The resulting shifts are indeedsensitive to different choices of the definition of the BAO scale, which needsto be kept in mind in the data analysis. We present a toy model to explain thephysical behavior of the shifts. We find that the BAO scale defined as inPercival et al. (2007) indeed shows very small shifts ($\lesssim$ 1%) relativeto the prediction in {\it linear theory} in real space. The shifts can bepredicted accurately for scales where the perturbation theory is reliable.

Abstract:In this study, we investigate the intramolecular long-range correlations in polymer melts by analyzing the segmental size distribution and its moments. Using advanced computational techniques and simulation methods, we explore the spatial arrangement and distribution of segments within the polymer chains. Our findings reveal the presence of long-range correlations in the segmental size distribution, indicating cooperative behavior and connectivity between distant segments. Furthermore, we calculate the moments of the size distribution and discuss their implications for the structural properties and dynamics of polymer melts. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of the fundamental characteristics and behavior of polymer systems, which has implications for various applications in materials science and engineering.

In the context of measuring structure and morphology of intermediate redshiftgalaxies with recent HST/ACS surveys, we tune, test, and compare two widelyused fitting codes (GALFIT and GIM2D) for fitting single-component Sersicmodels to the light profiles of both simulated and real galaxy data. We findthat fitting accuracy depends sensitively on galaxy profile shape. Exponentialdisks are well fit with Sersic models and have small measurement errors,whereas fits to de Vaucouleurs profiles show larger uncertainties owing to thelarge amount of light at large radii. We find that both codes provide reliablefits and little systematic error, when the effective surface brightness isabove that of the sky. Moreover, both codes return errors that significantlyunderestimate the true fitting uncertainties, which are best estimated withsimulations. We find that GIM2D suffers significant systematic errors forspheroids with close companions owing to the difficulty of effectively maskingout neighboring galaxy light; there appears to be no work around to thisimportant systematic in GIM2D's current implementation. While this crowdingerror affects only a small fraction of galaxies in GEMS, it must be accountedfor in the analysis of deeper cosmological images or of more crowded fieldswith GIM2D. In contrast, GALFIT results are robust to the presence of neighborsbecause it can simultaneously fit the profiles of multiple companions therebydeblending their effect on the fit to the galaxy of interest. We find GALFIT'srobustness to nearby companions and factor of >~20 faster runtime speed areimportant advantages over GIM2D for analyzing large HST/ACS datasets. Finallywe include our final catalog of fit results for all 41,495 objects detected inGEMS.

This academic article proposes a cross -layer optimization framework with multiple input and multiple output (MIMO) network networks with Gaussian Vector Broadcasting Channel (GVBC). MIMO technology has become a key promotion technology for wireless networks because it may improve system capacity and increase spectral efficiency. Compared with the traditional single -jump network, the network network characterized by its multiple jump properties and decentralized architecture provides enhanced connection and coverage. However, using MIMO technology in the network network will introduce other challenges, especially in dealing with interference and optimizing system performance. The proposed optimization framework aims to overcome these challenges by optimizing the physical layer MIMO transmission parameters and network layer routing decisions. By considering the unique features of GVBC, this feature can be transmitted to multiple receivers at the same time, which effectively uses the broadcast nature of the grid network to achieve improved system throughput and reduce interference. The optimization problem is proposed as a combination of physical layers and network layers, and uses the combination of convex optimization technology and the theory -based algorithm. The simulation results show that the proposed framework can achieve huge performance growth compared with existing methods. Specifically, it can achieve higher network throughput, improve signal quality, and reduce interference levels. In addition, the framework shows robustness on different network conditions and achieves almost the best performance under various channels.

We performed interferometric observations of a high-mass protostellarcandidate IRAS 18566+0408 in the 
h3 (J,K)=(1,1), (2,2) and (3,3) inversiontransitions, the SiO J=2-1 and HCN J=1-0 lines, and the 43 and 87 GHz continuumemission using the VLA and OVRO. The 87 GHz continuum emission reveals twocontinuum peaks MM-1 and MM-2 along a molecular ridge. The dominant peak MM-1coincides with a compact emission feature at 43 GHz, and arises mostly from thedust emission. For dust emissivity index $ eta$ of 1.3, the masses in the dustpeaks amount to 70 \msun for MM-1, and 27 \msun for MM-2. Assuming internalheating, the central luminosities of MM-1 and MM-2 are $6 	imes 10^4$ and $8	imes 10^3$ \lsun, respectively.  The SiO emission reveals a well collimated outflow emanating from MM-1. Thejet-like outflow is also detected in 
h3 at velocities similar to the SiOemission. The outflow, with a mass of 27 \msun, causes significant heating inthe gas to temperatures of 70 K, much higher than the temperature of $\lsim 15$K in the extended core. Compact ($< 3''$) and narrow line ($<1.5$ \kms-1) 
h3(3,3) emission features are found associated with the outflow. They likelyarise from weak population inversion in 
h3 similar to the maser emission.  Toward MM-1, there is a compact 
h3 structure with a linewidth thatincreases from 5.5 \kms-1 FHWM measured at 3$''$ resolution to 8.7 \kms-1\measured at 1$''$ resolution. This linewidth is much larger than the FWHM of$<$ 2 \kms-1 in the entire core, and does not appear to originate from theoutflow. This large linewidth may arise from rotation/infall, or relativemotions of unresolved protostellar cores.

We present radial velocities for 14 stars on the California & Carnegie PlanetSearch target list that reveal new companions. One star, HD 167665, was fitwith a definitive Keplerian orbit leading to a minimum mass for the companionof 50.3 Mjup at a separation from its host of ~5.5 AU. Incomplete or limitedphase coverage for the remaining 13 stars prevents us from assigning to themunique orbital parameters. Instead, we fit their radial velocities withKeplerian orbits across a grid of fixed values for Msini and period, P, and usethe resulting reduced chi-square surface to place constraints on Msini, P, andsemimajor axis, a. This technique allowed us to restrict Msini below the browndwarf -- stellar mass boundary for an additional 4 companions (HD 150554, HD8765, HD 72780, HD 74014). If the combined 5 companions are confirmed as browndwarfs, these results would comprise the first major catch of such objects fromour survey beyond ~3 AU.

[assistant]: The abstract of the academic article titled "The maximal D=4 supergravities" should be as follows:In this paper, we study the maximal D=4 supergravities in a unified way. We first construct the general action for the maximal D=4 supergravity theories, including all possible interactions. We then analyze the structure of the action and find that it is invariant under a number of symmetries, including supersymmetry, gauge symmetry, and duality symmetry. We also show that the action can be written in a form that is manifestly supersymmetric. Finally, we discuss the implications of our results for the study of supergravity and its applications.

We present mid-IR spectral decomposition of a sample of 48 Spitzer-selectedULIRGs spanning z~1-3 and likely L_IR~10^12-10^13Lsun. Our study aims atquantifying the star-formation and AGN processes in these sources which recentresults suggest have evolved strongly between the observed epoch and today. Todo this, we study the mid-IR contribution of PAH emission, continuum, andextinction. About 3/4 of our sample are continuum- (i.e. AGN) dominatedsources, but ~60% of these show PAH emission, suggesting the presence ofstar-formation activity. These sources have redder mid-IR colors than typicaloptically-selected quasars. About 25% of our sample have strong PAH emission,but none are likely to be pure starbursts as reflected in their relatively high5um hot dust continua. However, their steep 30um-to-14um slopes suggest thatstar-formation might dominate the total infrared luminosity. Six of our z~2sources have EW6.2>~0.3um and L_14um>~10^12Lsun (implying L_IR>~10^13Lsun). Atthese luminosities, such high EW6.2 ULIRGs do not exist in the local Universe.We find a median optical depth at 9.7um of <tau_9.7>=1.4. This is consistentwith local IRAS-selected ULIRGs, but differs from early results onSCUBA-selected z~2 ULIRGs. Similar to local ULIRGs about 25% of our sample showextreme obscuration (tau_9.7>~3) suggesting buried nuclei. In general, we findthat our sources are similar to local ULIRGs, but are an order of magnitudemore luminous. It is not clear whether our z~2 ULIRGs are simply scaled-upversions of local ULIRGs, or subject to fundamentally different physicalprocesses.

Visual data mining of genomic databases presents numerous challenges due to the massive size and complexity of the data, including noisy, mising valus, and complex relationships. Immersive graph-based exploartion provides a promising aprpoach to address these challenges by allowing researchers to interact with the data directly, identify patterns and relationships, and gain insights that would be dMifficult or impossible to obtain using traditional methods. This article presents a novel visual data mining approach for exploring genomic databaes using immersive graph-based techniques. The proposed approach integrates multiple views, inyluding a 3D graph visualization, a node-link diagram, and a scatterplot matrix, to provide a comprehensive undrstanding of the data. Additionally, the approach incorporates advanced interaction techniques, such as brushing and linking, zooming, and panning, to enqble researchqers to explore the data interactively and identify meaningful patterns. The effectiveness of the proposed aproach is demonstrated through two case studies using real-world genomic datasets.

In this study, we investigate the eclipsing light curves of accretion flows around a rotating black hole, taking into account the atmospheric effects of the companion star. We use a novel numerical approach that combines general relativity, hydrodynamics, and atmospheric science to simulate the eclipsing light curves. Our results show that the eclipsing light curves are significantly affected by the rotation of the black hole and the atmospheric effects of the companion star. We find that the eclipsing light curves exhibit a strong dependence on the viewing angle, with the greatest effects observed when the observer's line of sight is perpendicular to the plane of the accretion flow. We also demonstrate that the eclipsing light curves can be used to infer the properties of the black hole and its companion star, such as their masses, spin rates, and orbital inclinations. Our results have important implications for the study of black hole binaries and the development of new methods for measuring their properties.

Intermediate polars (IPs) are cataclysmic variables which contain magneticwhite dwarfs with a rotational period shorter than the binary orbital period.Evolutionary theory predicts that IPs with long orbital periods evolve throughthe 2-3 hour period gap, but it is very uncertain what the properties of theresulting objects are. Whilst a relatively large number of long-period IPs areknown, very few of these have short orbital periods. We present phase-resolvedspectroscopy and photometry of SDSS J233325.92+152222.1 and classify it as theIP with the shortest known orbital period (83.12 +/- 0.09 min), which containsa white dwarf with a relatively long spin period (41.66 +/- 0.13 min). Weestimate the white dwarf's magnetic moment to be mu(WD) \approx 2 x 10^33 Gcm^3, which is not only similar to three of the other four confirmedshort-period IPs but also to those of many of the long-period IPs. We suggestthat long-period IPs conserve their magnetic moment as they evolve towardsshorter orbital periods. Therefore the dominant population of long-period IPs,which have white dwarf spin periods roughly ten times shorter than theirorbital periods, will likely end up as short-period IPs like SDSS J2333, withspin periods a large fraction of their orbital periods.

In this article, we Mxplore the impact of quantum radiative corrections on slow-roll inflation, a paradigm widely used to explain the observed largce-scale structure of the universe. We compute the leading-rder corrections to the inflationary perturbations and show that they can have a isgnificant impact on the spectral index and tensor-to-scalar ratio, especially for lage values of the inflationary scale. Our results demonstrate the impoxtance of considering quantum radiative cFrrections in precision studies of the early universe, and provide a new window into the physics of the inflationary era. We also discuss the implications of our findings for future observatUons and the potential for distinguishing between different inflationary moedels.

A brief introduction is given to the subject of brane induced gravity. The 5Dexample is discussed in detail. The 4D laws of gravity are obtained on a braneembedded in an infinite volume extra space, where the problem of stabilizationof the volume modulus is absent. The theory has two classically disjointbranches of solutions -- the conventional and self-accelerated one. Theconventional branch gives a perturbatively stable model of a metastablegraviton, with potentially testable predictions within the Solar system. Theself-accelerated branch, on the other hand, provides an existence proof for anidea that the accelerated expansion of the Universe could be due to modifiedgravity. The issue of perturbative stability of the self-accelerated branch isobscured by a breakdown of the conventional perturbative expansion. However, acertain exact non-perturbative solution found in hep-th/0612016 exhibits a netnegative gravitational mass, while this mass is positive on the conventionalbranch. This suggest that the self-accelerated solution must benon-perturbatively unstable. A proposal to overcome this problem in anextension of the original model, that also allows for the quantum gravity scaleto be unrestricted, is briefly discussed.

New high-resolution observations reveal that small-scale magnetic fluxconcentrations have a delicate substructure on a spatial scale of 0.1''. Itsbasic structure can be interpreted in terms of a magnetic flux sheet or tubethat vertically extends through the ambient weak-field or field-free atmospherewith which it is in mechanical equilibrium. A more refined interpretation comesfrom new three-dimensional magnetohydrodynamic simulations that are capable ofreproducing the corrugated shape of magnetic flux concentrations and theirsignature in the visible continuum. Furthermore it is shown that thecharacteristic asymmetric shape of the contrast profile of facular granules isan effect of radiative transfer across the rarefied atmosphere of the magneticflux concentration. I also discuss three-dimensional radiationmagnetohydrodynamic simulations of the integral layers from the top of theconvection zone to the mid-chromosphere. They show a highly dynamicchromospheric magnetic field, marked by rapidly moving filaments of strongerthan average magnetic field that form in the compression zone downstream andalong propagating shock fronts. The simulations confirm the picture of fluxconcentrations that strongly expand through the photosphere into a morehomogeneous, space filling chromospheric field. Future directions in thesimulation of small-scale magnetic fields are indicated by a few examples ofvery recent work.

** Abstract: ** This article proposes a new protocol to random sharing through Internet via Internet. The protocol is based on the idea of using shared secret key encryption. This key is generated by using a new type of key first algorithm based on the hardness of factoring problems. The agreement can protect eavesdropping and intermediate attacks. It is also effective and can be implemented using a variety of different encryption libraries. We have implemented agreements and tested on various different networks. The results show that the agreement is safe and effective.

In this study, we present a novel approach to stipulates effective potentials for quasicrystals using ab-initio data. Quasicrystals are a exciting class of materials with long-range order but lack translational symmetry. Due to their unique structural trait, traditional modeling techniques often fail to accurately describe their behavior. To address this challenge, we recommending a methodology that combines ab-initio calculation and statistical mechanics to derive effective interatomic potentials for quasicrystals. Our method involves performing extensive density functional theory simulations to obtain the structural and energetic possessions of a commenting quasicrystal. Subsequently, we employ molecular dynamics simulations to induces pathway that provide insightful information on the atomic dynamics and fluctuations in the system. By fitting the obtained data to a set of physically motivated potentials, we develop an effective potential model that accurately captures the needed traits of quasicrystals. We demonstrate the yields of our approach by comparing the structural and dynamical properties predicted by our model with experimental consequences for various quasicrystalline systems. Our findings stresses the importance of incorporating ab-initio data in designing accurate and predictive potential models for quasicrystals, ultimately enabling more holistic understanding and exploration of these remarkable materials.

The problem of resource allocation is studied for a two-user fadingorthogonal multiaccess relay channel (MARC) where both users (sources)communicate with a destination in the presence of a relay. A half-duplex relayis considered that transmits on a channel orthogonal to that used by thesources. The instantaneous fading state between every transmit-receive pair inthis network is assumed to be known at both the transmitter and receiver. Underan average power constraint at each source and the relay, the sum-rate for theachievable strategy of decode-and-forward (DF) is maximized over all powerallocations (policies) at the sources and relay. It is shown that the sum-ratemaximizing policy exploits the multiuser fading diversity to reveal theoptimality of opportunistic channel use by each user. A geometricinterpretation of the optimal power policy is also presented.

We introduce a nonequilibrium equation-of-motion approach for an impurityAnderson model which allows us to describe transport properties of molecularjunctions. The method is based on a mapping of the Anderson model to a fictitiousequilibrium problem, in the limit of large onsite Coulomb interaction U, byintroducing an auxiliary field. This problem can be solved within the nonequilibrium Green's function technique. We calculate the differential conductanceand thermopower and analyze their dependence on temperature and gate voltage. Thecalculated characteristics exhibit a smooth crossover from the Coulomb blockade regimeto the Kondo regime as the interaction strength is varied. A detailed comparison to thenumerical renormalization group results is given. In the large U limit, we find aquantitative agreement with the two-channel Kondo model results. In particular, weobserve the suppression of the zero-bias anomaly in the differential conductance and thechange of the sign of the thermopower in the Kondo regime.

We propose that cosmological magnetic fields generated in regions of finitespatial dimensions may manifest themselves in the global dynamics of theUniverse as `dark energy'. We test our model in the context of spatially flatcosmological models by assuming that the Universe contains non-relativisticmatter $\rho_m\propto \alpha^{-3}$, dark energy $\rho_{Q}\propto\alpha^{-3(1+w)}$, and an extra fluid with $\rho_{B} \propto \alpha^{n-3}$ thatcorresponds to the magnetic field. We place constraints on the maincosmological parameters of our model by combining the recent supernovae type Iadata and the differential ages of passively evolving galaxies. In particular,we find that the model which best reproduces the observational data when$\Omega_m=0.26$ is one with $\Omega_{B}\simeq 0.03$, $n\simeq 7.68$,$\Omega_{Q}\simeq 0.71$ and $w\simeq -0.8$.

In these fields, the ability to detect and measure entanglement is of paramount importance. In this paper, we present further results on entanglement detection and quantification based on the correlation criterion. We first derive a necessary and sufficient condition for the separability of a bipartite quantum state in terms of the eigenvalues of its correlation matrix. We then propose a new entanglement measure based on the determinant of the correlation matrix, which is shown to be a computably defined entanglement monotone. Finally, we investigate the relationship between the correlation criterion and other entanglement criteria, such as the positive partial transpose criterion and the concurrence criterion. Our results provide new insights into the detection and quantification of entanglement and are expected to find applications in quantum information and quantum computation.

[assistant]: The study of hadronic $B$ decays provides a unique window into understanding the fundamental parameters of the Standard Model and searching for physics beyond it. Decays of $B$ mesons to pairs of light pseudoscalar mesons, such as $\pi\pi$, $\pi K$, and $KK$, are theoretically clean and experimentally accessible, making them powerful probes for these studies.In this article, we review the current status of experimental and theoretical studies of $B_{s,d} \to \pi\pi,\pi K, KK$ decays. We discuss the latest measurements of branching fractions, CP asymmetries, and other observables, and compare them to theoretical predictions. We also discuss the prospects for future measurements of these decays, which will play an important role in improving our understanding of the Standard Model and searching for new physics.We summarize the current status and future prospects of studies of $B_{s,d} \to \pi\pi,\pi K, KK$ decays, which provide valuable insights into the fundamental parameters of the Standard Model and potential physics beyond it.Recent experimental results from LHCb, Belle, and BaBar collaborations have significantly improved the precision of measurements of branching fractions and CP asymmetries in these decays. The comparison of these measurements with theoretical predictions has provided important constraints on the Cabibbo-Kobayashi-Maskawa matrix elements and the angles of the unitarity triangle.Ongoing and future experiments, such as LHCb Upgrade, Belle II, and BESIII, are expected to further improve the precision of these measurements and explore new observables, such as time-dependent CP asymmetries and isospin asymmetries. These measurements will provide even more stringent tests of the Standard Model and have the potential to uncover new physics phenomena.

This study presents a measurement of the aerosol phase function conducted at the Pierre Auger Observatory. The objective of this measurement is to enhance our comprehension of the scattering properties of atmospheric aerosols. The aerosol phase function characterizes the angular distribution of light scattered by aerosol particles and holds great significance in various atmospheric and climate investigations. To obtain a comprehensive dataset of aerosol scattering characteristics, a combination of ground-based lidar and sunphotometer observations was utilized. The results reveal notable variations in the aerosol phase function concerning altitude. At lower altitudes, forward-scattering predominates, while a more isotropic scattering pattern is observed at higher altitudes. A comparison of the observed aerosol phase functions with existing models exhibited deviations, underscoring the necessity of regional measurements for precise aerosol characterization. The outcomes of this study contribute to advancing our understanding of the influence of aerosols on Earth's radiation budget and climate system, enabling the development of more accurate radiative transfer simulations and climate models.

We analyze a sample of 10 pulsars using a combination of NuSTAR and Swift observations. We find that the spectra of these pulsars are characterized by a power-law continuum with a high-energy cutoff around 20 keV and a strong Fe line at 6.4 keV. We show that the spectra can be well described by a Comptonization model with a high electron temperature and optical depth. Our results provide new insights into the physics of accretion-powered X-ray pulsars and highlight the importance of Comptonization in shaping their spectra. We also find that the strength of the Fe line is correlated with the spin-down luminosity of the pulsars, suggesting a link between the accretion rate and the strength of the magnetic field.

It has been established that local lattice spin Hamiltonians can be used foruniversal adiabatic quantum computation. However, the 2-local modelHamiltonians used in these proofs are general and hence do not limit the typesof interactions required between spins. To address this concern, the presentpaper provides two simple model Hamiltonians that are of practical interest toexperimentalists working towards the realization of a universal adiabaticquantum computer. The model Hamiltonians presented are the simplest knownQMA-complete 2-local Hamiltonians. The 2-local Ising model with 1-localtransverse field which has been realized using an array of technologies, isperhaps the simplest quantum spin model but is unlikely to be universal foradiabatic quantum computation. We demonstrate that this model can be rendereduniversal and QMA-complete by adding a tunable 2-local transverse XX coupling.We also show the universality and QMA-completeness of spin models with only1-local Z and X fields and 2-local ZX interactions.

The rapid growth of online information storage and more and more challenges related to resource discovery have prompted researchers to explore and innovate. This article surveys the use of social annotations as a means to enhance automatic resources. Social annotations refer to the process of collaborating with metadata (such as keywords or descriptions) to promote organizational and retrieval. By using the user's collective wisdom, social annotations provide promising ways to overcome the limitations of traditional automation technology. This research has comprehensively analyzed the various methods of social annotations in the context of resource discovery. It checked the benefits and challenges related to the existing information retrieval system. In addition, this article proposes a novel framework, which combines machine learning algorithms and users' annotations to improve the accuracy and correlation of resource discovery. Finally, a series of experiments were conducted to evaluate the effectiveness of the framework proposed, proving that its potential has significantly enhanced the discovery of automatic resource in different domains.

Quantum geometry, a fundamental aspect of theoretical physics, is closely tied to comprehending quantum gravity. This paper reviews recent advancements in quantum geometry, focusing on loop quantum gravity and fuzzy geometry. The discussion encompasses basic ideas, mathematical formalisms, and physical implications while comparing their merits and limitations. Additionally, it explores future directions and challenges in quantum geometry research.

This study investigates celestial pole offset (CPO) observations in the free core nutation frequency band, which refers to the Earth's nutation period without any gravitational forcing from the Moon, Sun, or planetary aliases. The CPO observations were acquired using satellite laser ranging (SLR) and very long baseline interferometry (VLBI) methods. The analysis reveals a significant discrepancy between the observed CPO values and the theoretical predictions for the Earth's core nutation. Through spectral analysis, we identify two distinct peaks in the CPO observations, corresponding to the free core nutation frequency and its harmonic. These findings have important implications for our understanding of Earth's internal dynamics and the dynamics of the Earth's core.The revisions made to the abstract include:1. The measured low initial sticking probability of oxygen molecules at theAl(111) surface that had puzzled the field for many years was recentlyexplained in a non-adiabatic picture invoking spin-selection rules [J. Behleret al., Phys. Rev. Lett. 94, 036104 (2005)]. These selection rules tend toconserve the initial spin-triplet character of the free O2 molecule during themolecule's approach to the surface. A new locally-constraineddensity-functional theory approach gave access to the correspondingpotential-energy surface (PES) seen by such an impinging spin-triplet moleculeand indicated barriers to dissociation which reduce the sticking probability.Here, we further substantiate this non-adiabatic picture by providing adetailed account of the employed approach. Building on the previous work, wefocus in particular on inaccuracies in present-day exchange-correlationfunctionals. Our analysis shows that small quantitative differences in thespin-triplet constrained PES obtained with different gradient-correctedfunctionals have a noticeable effect on the lowest kinetic energy part of theresulting sticking curve.

In recent years, there has been increasing interest in the specific heat $C$of insulators and semiconductors because of the availability of samples withdifferent isotopic masses and the possibility of performing \textit{ab initio}calculations of its temperature dependence $C(T)$ using as a starting point theelectronic band structure. Most of the crystals investigated are elemental(e.g., germanium) or binary (e.g., gallium nitride) semiconductors. The initialelectronic calculations were performed in the local density approximation anddid not include spin-orbit interaction. Agreement between experimental andcalculated results was usually found to be good, except for crystals containingheavy atoms (e.g., PbS) for which discrepancies of the order of 20% existed atthe low temperature maximum found for $C/T^3$. It has been conjectured thatthis discrepancies result from the neglect of spin-orbit interaction which islarge for heavy atoms ($\Delta_0\sim$1.3eV for the $p$ valence electrons ofatomic lead). Here we discuss measurements and \textit{ab initio} calculationsof $C(T)$ for crystalline bismuth ($\Delta_0\sim$1.7 eV), strictly speaking asemimetal but in the temperature region accessible to us ($T >$ 2K) acting as asemiconductor. We extend experimental data available in the literature andnotice that the \textit{ab initio} calculations without spin-orbit interactionexhibit a maximum at $\sim$8K, about 20% lower than the measured one. Inclusionof spin-orbit interaction decreases the discrepancy markedly: The maximum of$C(T)$ is now only 7% larger than the measured one. Exact agreement is obtainedif the spin-orbit hamiltonian is reduced by a factor of $\sim$0.8.

This article addresses the issue of energy efficiency in multipath code division multiple access (CDMA) channels with bandlimited waveforms. The authors propose several power control and receiver design techniques to improve energy efficiency while maintaining acceptable levels of performance. The proposed techniques include adaptive power control, which adjusts the transmission power based on the channel conditions, and a low-complexity receiver design that utilizes a linear equalizer and a minimum mean-squared error (MMSE) detector. The authors demonstrate through simulations that the proposed techniques can significantly reduce the energy consumption of the system while maintaining a high level of performance. The results show that the proposed power control and receiver design techniques can achieve a 50% reduction in energy consumption compared to traditional CDMA systems while maintaining a bit error rate (BER) of 10^-3. The authors also discuss the tradeoff between energy efficiency and performance and provide insights into the design of energy-efficient CDMA systems.

We present a combined experimental and computational study of the (110)cross-sectional surface of Mn $\delta$-doped GaAs samples. We focus our studyon three different selected Mn defect configurations not previously studied indetails, namely surface interstitial Mn, isolated and in pairs, andsubstitutional Mn atoms on cationic sites (Mn$_{\rm Ga}$) in the firstsubsurface layer. The sensitivity of the STM images to the specific localenvironment allows to distinguish between Mn interstitials with nearestneighbor As atoms (Int$_{\rm As}$) rather than Ga atoms (Int$_{\rm Ga}$), andto identify the fingerprint of peculiar satellite features around subsurfacesubstitutional Mn. The simulated STM maps for Int$_{\rm As}$, both isolated andin pairs, and Mn$_{\rm Ga}$ in the first subsurface layer are consistent withsome experimental images hitherto not fully characterized.

Our results show that the quadrupole-dipole interaction gives rise to a novel type of exciton, which is strongly coupled between the quadrupole and dipole moments. This hybrid exciton leads to a significant increase in the second-harmonic generation efficiency, making it a promising candidate for applications in nonlinear optical devices. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we study the mechanism of second-harmonic generation in a quadrupole-dipole hybrid system and demonstrate a significant increase in the second-harmonic signal compared to the usual dipole-dipole system. Our results have important implications for the development of novel nonlinear optical materials and devices and may open up new opportunities in the fields of high-power lasers and frequency conversion.

This paper discusses the ATLAS potential to study Supersymmetry for the"Focus-Point" region of the parameter space of mSUGRA models. The potential todiscovery a deviation from Standard Model expectations with the first few${fb}^{-1}$ of LHC data was studied using the parametrized simulation of theATLAS detector. Several signatures were considered, involving hard jets, largemissing energy, and either $b$-tagged jets, opposite-sign isolated electron ormuon pairs, or top quarks reconstructed exploiting their fully hadronic decays.With only 1 ${fb}^{-1}$ of data each of these signatures may allow to observean excess of events over Standard Model expectation with a statisticalsignificance exceeding 5 standard deviations. An analytical expression wasderived for the shape of the distribution of the dilepton invariant massarising from the three-body leptonic decay of the neutralinos under thehypothesis of heavy scalars, which is appropriate for the focus-point scenario.The resulting function was used to fit the distribution of the dileptoninvariant mass obtained with simulated LHC data, and to extract the value oftwo kinematic endpoints measuring the $\tilde \chi^0_2 - \tilde \chi^0_1$ andthe $\tilde \chi^0_3 - \tilde \chi^0_1$ mass differences. This information wasused to constrain the MSSM parameter space compatible with the data.

The optical observations, performed with the Hubble Space Telescope, show a complex structure of galaxies and clusters in the vicinity of RX J1117.4+0743. The X-ray observations, performed with the Chandra X-ray Observatory, show a diffuse X-ray source that is spatially correlated with the optical data. Using a combination of optical and X-ray observations, we have witnessed the formation of this cluster and studied its properties. The optical and X-ray data are consistent with the presence of a cluster with a mass of about 1014 Msun. The X-ray data indicate a high temperature of kT=8.6+-0.5 keV, which suggests the presence of a large amount of hot gas in the cluster. The X-ray luminosity is LX=2.4  1044 erg/s, which is typical for a cluster of this size and redshift. The color-magnitude diagram of the galaxies in the cluster shows a clear red sequence, which indicates that the galaxies are mostly old and passively evolving. The presence of a significant number of blue galaxies suggests that the cluster has recently undergone a significant star formation activity. The distribution of galaxies in the cluster is irregular, with several substructures and streams. The galaxies are centrally concentrated, and their surface density decreases with distance from the center. The X-ray and optical properties of RX J1117.4+0743 are similar to those of other clusters at similar redshifts. The velocity dispersion of the galaxies is consistent with the mass of the cluster, which indicates that the galaxies are in dynamic equilibrium. However, the detailed structure of the cluster suggests that it is still forming, and that galaxies are still merging and accreting.

The article begins by introducing the basic concepts of string theory and its mathematical structure, and then discusses the main insights and contributions of string theory to cosmology, including the inflationary universe scenario and the possibility of multiple universes in the framework of the string landscape. String theory, a theoretical framework that aims to unify all fundamental forces and particles, offers a promising avenue for exploring the mysteries of the universe at both microscopic and macroscopic scales. The article also highlights the challenges and open questions in the application of string theory to cosmology, and concludes by emphasizing the importance of further research and interdisciplinary collaborations in developing a comprehensive cosmological model based on string theory.

**Abstract**This paper studies the flow dynamics and rank rigidity of two-dimensional incompressible flows on surfaces with nonpositive curvature. We introduce a new framework for analyzing the flow dynamics, based on the notion of a "2-frame". This framework allows us to obtain a number of new results on the stability and rigidity of such flows. In particular, we prove that any two-frame flow on a surface with nonpositive curvature is either Anosov or has a dense set of periodic orbits. We also prove that any two-frame flow on a closed surface with nonpositive curvature is hyperbolically rank rigid. These results have applications to the study of the dynamics of fluids and the geometry of surfaces.

Pragmatism is a philosophical approach that emphasizes the importance of practical consequences and results. It is often associated with the American philosopher William James. It is one of the oldest and most widely accepted interpretations of quantum mechanics, and it is based on the idea that the act of observing a quantum system affects the outcome of the measurement. This paper explores the relationship between the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics and pragmatism, arguing that the two approaches share a commitment to the importance of experience and a rejection of metaphysical realism. The paper concludes that pragmatism can be a useful way to understand and interpret the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.

**Abstract**We present an experimental demonstration of Shor’s factoring algorithm using quantum entanglement. Our experiment is based on a two-qubit NMR quantum information processor and uses the entanglement between the two qubits to perform the modular exponentiation required by Shor’s algorithm. We successfully factor the number 15 into its prime factors, 3 and 5. Our results provide a proof-of-principle demonstration of Shor’s algorithm and pave the way for the development of scalable quantum computers that can be used to factor large numbers.

A PromiseBQP-complete string rewriting problem is proposed, which is the first string rewriting problem p roven to be cоmplete for PromiseBQP. Our reduction is inspired by the classical proof for the BQP-completeness of the halting problem for polynomial-size quantum cricuits. To the best of our knowledge, this is also the first natural рroblem proven to be PromiseBQP-complete. Our result implies that deciding whehter a giѵen string can be rewritten to the empty string by some polynomial-size quantum string rewriting system is PromiseBQP-complete. Furthermore, we also study the relationship between string rewriting systems, term rewriting syѕtems, and alternating Turing machines. We show that the sets of languages recоgnizable by polyomial-size string rewriting systems, polynomial-size term rewriting systems, and polynomial-size alternating Turing machines coincide.

Spectral variability is the main tool for constraining emission models of BL  Lac objects. By means of systematic observations of the BL Lac prototype PKS2155-304 in the infrared-optical band, we explore variability on the scales ofmonths, days and hours. We made our observations with the robotic 60 cmtelescope REM located at La Silla, Chile. VRIJHK filters were used. PKS2155-304 was observed from May to December 2005. The wavelength intervalexplored, the total number of photometric points and the short integration timerender our photometry substantially superior to previous ones for this source.On the basis of the intensity and colour we distinguish three different statesof the source, each of duration of months, which include all those described inthe literature. In particular, we report the highest state ever detected in theH band. The source varied by a factor of 4 in this band, much more than in theV band (a factor ~2). The source softened with increasing intensity, contraryto the general pattern observed in the UV-X-ray bands. On five nights ofNovember we had nearly continuous monitoring for 2-3 hours. A variabilityepisode with a time scale of ~24 h is well documented, a much more rapid flarewith t=1-2 h, is also apparent, but is supported by relatively few points.

In this article, we introduce a novel method for determining the equation of state of atomic systems beyond the s-wave regime, leveraging the lowest order constrained variational method. Our approach is rooted in the large scattering length limit, yielding a set of coupled equations that accurately describe the system's behavior. By solving these equations, we obtain the equation of state, which shows good agreement with existing results. Our method benefits from being computationally efficient and can be applied to a broad range of atomic systems. We illustrate the versatility of our approach by examining several examples of atomic interactions, including Hydrogen and Lithium.The polished abstract has several key improvements:1. The polished abstract reduces word count by removing unnecessary phrases and improving sentence structure, making it easier for readers to quickly understand the main contributions of the article.4. Spelling: The original abstract contains a spelling error in the word " Lithium" (missing capital letter). The polished abstract corrects this error.5. Academic style: The polished abstract adopts a more formal tone and uses academic language that is appropriate for a scientific article.

This work is concerned with some properties of the Young-Fibonacci insertionalgorithm and its relation with Fomin's growth diagrams. It also investigates arelation between the combinatorics of Young-Fibonacci tableaux and the study ofOkada's algebra associated to the Young-Fibonacci lattice. The originalalgorithm was introduced by Roby and we redefine it in such a way that both theinsertion and recording tableaux of any permutation are \emph{conveniently}interpreted as chains in the Young-Fibonacci lattice. A property ofKillpatrick's evacuation is given a simpler proof, but this evacuation is nolonger needed in making Roby's and Fomin's constructions coincide. We providethe set of Young-Fibonacci tableaux of size $n$ with a structure of gradedposet, induced by the weak order on permutations of the symmetric group, andrealized by transitive closure of elementary transformations on tableaux. Weshow that this poset gives a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficientsin the transition matrix from the analogue of complete symmetric functions toanalogue of the Schur functions in Okada's algebra. We end with a quite similarobservation for four posets on Young-tableaux studied by Taskin.

We present ground-based B and R-band color-magnitude diagrams (CMDs),reaching the oldest main-sequence turnoffs with good photometric accuracy fortwelve fields in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC). Our fields, located between~1 and ~4 degrees from the center of the galaxy, are situated in differentparts of the SMC such as the "Wing'' area, and towards the West and South. Inthis paper we perform a first analysis of the stellar content in our SMC fieldsthrough comparison with theoretical isochrones and color functions (CFs). Wefind that the underlying spheroidally distributed population is composed ofboth intermediate-age and old stars and that its age composition does not showstrong galacto-centric gradients. The three fields situated toward the east, inthe Wing region, show very active current star formation. However, only in theeastern field closest to the center do we find an enhancement of recent starformation with respect to a constant SFR(t). The fields corresponding to thewestern side of the SMC present a much less populated young MS, and the CFanalysis indicates that the SFR(t) greatly diminished around 2 Gyr ago in theseparts. Field smc0057, the closest to the center of the galaxy and located inthe southern part, shows recent star formation, while the rest of the southernfields present few bright MS stars. The structure of the red clump in all theCMDs is consistent with the large amount of intermediate-age stars inferredfrom the CMDs and color functions. None of the SMC fields presented here aredominated by old stellar populations, a fact that is in agreement with the lackof a conspicuous horizontal branch in all these SMC CMDs. This could indicatethat a disk population is ruling over a possible old halo in all the observedfields.

**Abstract:**This paper presents an analytical approximation of the Fe Kα emission line in the spectra of QSOs. The approximation is based on a simple model of the accretion disk and the emission line region. This model takes into account the effects of Doppler broadening, gravitational redshift, and Compton scattering. By comparing the approximation with the results of numerical simulations, a good agreement is found. Therefore, the approximation can be applied to estimate the parameters of the accretion disk and the emission line region in QSOs.

We present high resolution imaging of the puzzling radio and optical nebulaG70.7+1.2 with the Keck Observatory's laser guide star adaptive optics (LGS-AO)system and the Chandra X-ray Observatory. The archival X-ray observations showa hard (Gamma ~ 1.8), low luminosity (L_X ~ 4 x 10^31 ergs/s) point source atthe center of the nebula.
