The results show that the van der Waals forces between the microparticle and the SWCNT are highly dependent on the distance between them and the size of the microparticle. The interaction between the two entities is distance-dependent, and the magnitude of the interaction increases with decreasing distance. We use theoretical modeling and molecular dynamics simulations to explore the nature and strength of the interaction forces at the nanoscale. This study contributes to a deeper understanding of the fundamental forces involved in the interaction between microparticles and SWCNTs, which may have important implications for various applications, such as the synthesis of nanomaterials and the fabrication of nanodevices.

This study aimed to develop a novel approach for preventing pressure ulcers in paraplegic patients through linguistic electrostimulation. Pressure ulcers are a common problem in paraplegic patients, and current prevention methods have limited effectiveness. The proposed approach uses electrostimulation of the skin and underlying tissues to improve blood flow and reduce pressure. A pilot study was conducted with promising results, showing a significant reduction in pressure ulcer incidence. The study demonstrates the potential of linguistic electrostimulation as a safe and effective method for preventing pressure ulcers in paraplegic patients. Further research is needed to fully explore the benefits and limitations of this approach.

One of the most notorious non-Fermi liquid properties of both archetypalheavy-fermion systems [1-4] and the high-Tc copper oxide superconductors [5] isan electrical resistivity that evolves linearly with temperature, T. In theheavy-fermion superconductor CeCoIn5 [5], this linear behaviour was one of thefirst indications of the presence of a zero-temperature instability, or quantumcritical point. Here, we report the observation of a unique control parameterof T-linear scattering in CeCoIn5, found through systematic chemicalsubstitutions of both magnetic and non-magnetic rare-earth, R, ions into the Cesub-lattice. We find that the evolution of inelastic scattering in Ce1-xRxCoIn5is strongly dependent on the f-electron configuration of the R ion, whereas twoother key properties -- Cooper-pair breaking and Kondo-lattice coherence -- arenot. Thus, T-linear resistivity in CeCoIn5 is intimately related to the natureof incoherent scattering centers in the Kondo lattice, which provides insightinto the anomalous scattering rate synonymous with quantum criticality [7].

We describe an efficient approximation algorithm for evaluating theground-state energy of the classical Ising Hamiltonian with linear terms on anarbitrary planar graph. The running time of the algorithm grows linearly withthe number of spins and exponentially with 1/epsilon, where epsilon is theworst-case relative error. This result contrasts the well known fact that exactcomputation of the ground-state energy for the two-dimensional Ising spin glassmodel is NP-hard. We also present a classical approximation algorithm for theLocal Hamiltonian Problem or Quantum Ising Spin Glass problem on a planar graphwith bounded degree which is known to be a QMA-complete problem. Using adifferent technique we find a classical approximation algorithm for the quantumIsing spin glass problem on the simplest planar graph with unbounded degree,the star graph.

A detailed study of gas and star formation in a highly magnified Lyman break galaxy at z=3.07We presaent a detailged study of the physical conditions and satr formation activity in a highly magnified (mu ˜ 0) Lyman break galaxy (LBG) at z=3.07, using deAep Hubble Space Telescope (HST) imaging and spectroscopy. The magnification allows us to resolve the galaxy into individual clumps and meausre their physical properties. We find that the clumps are small (r_e ˜ 0.2-1.2 kpc), massive (M* ˜ 10^8-01^10 Msun), and have high star fTrmation rates (SFR ˜ 10-100 Msun/yr). The clumps are embedded in a diffese medium with a low covering factor (C_F ˜ 0.1). The gas in the clumps is metal-enriched (U12+log(O/H) ˜ 8.6), and the satr formation is occurring in a clumpy and bursty manner. We compare our results to those of other LBGy at similar redshifts and find that our LBG is typical in terms of its physical properties and stTar formation activity. However, our LBmG is unique in that it is the fisrt LBG at z>3 to be studied in such detail. Our results provide new insjghts into the properties and evolution of LBGs at high redshifts.

**Abstract:**The paper proposes a modeling process for integrating diverse and heterogeneous data into a unified format in a data warehousing context. The conceptual level representation is in UML and the logical model is an XML schema. The physical model is represented by XML documents that are mapped into a relational database. The database content will be re-modeled multidimensionally for storage in a star schema-based warehouse. Our framework offers both design-time and run-time environments. The user specifies the business information needed using a conceptual modeling language as a multidimensional object model. The user conceptual model together with the mappings will be transformed into views over the logical multidimensional representation. The conceptual model can be mapped to the logical multidimensional representation. Complex data integration is difficult. Our new approach for complex data integration is based on a Multi-Agent System (MAS), in association with a data warehousing approach.

This article presents a global approach to the theory of special Finsler manifolds, which extends traditional Riemannian geometry to include a broader class of metrics. Finsler geometry encompasses Riemannian geometry as a special case and allows for the study of more general geometrical structures. The global approach adopted in this work aims to establish a comprehensive framework for analyzing special Finsler manifolds on a global scale.The article begins by introducing the fundamental concepts of Finsler geometry, including Finsler metrics, spray coefficients, and tangent bundles. It then proceeds to develop a set of global tools and techniques for studying special Finsler manifolds. These tools include the use of local coordinate charts to define global objects such as global tensor fields and global vector fields.Moreover, the article investigates the connection between curvature and geodesics in special Finsler manifolds. It explores the notion of curvature tensors and their relationship with geodesic equations, providing insights into the geometric properties of these manifolds. The study of geodesics plays a crucial role in understanding the intrinsic structure and behavior of special Finsler manifolds.Finally, the article discusses applications of the global approach to the theory of special Finsler manifolds. It highlights the potential impact of this framework in various fields, including theoretical physics and cosmology. By providing a global perspective on special Finsler manifolds, this work contributes to the advancement of geometrical theories and offers new avenues for exploring the intricate structures of these manifolds in a global context.

We present a deep, 8$\degr$ diameter, 0.4 GHz radio image using a first timecombination of the NAIC Arecibo 305-m telescope in Puerto Rico, and thewide-angle interferometer at the Dominion Radio Astrophysical Observatory atPenticton, Canada. Our observations are centered on the Coma Cluster ofgalaxies in the ``Great Wall'' of galaxies near the North Galactic Pole. Thecomplementary nature of these two instruments enables us to produce adistortion-free image that is sensitive to radiation on scales from 8$\degr$down to that of an individual galaxy halo at the 100 Mpc distance of the GreatWall. Newly revealed patches of distributed radio ``glow'' are seen well abovethe detection limit. One prominent such area coincides with groupings of radiogalaxies near the Coma cluster, and indicates intergalactic IGM magnetic fieldsin the range 0.2 to 0.4 $\mu$G on scales of up to $\sim$ 4 Mpc. Other patchesof diffuse emission, not previously explored at these high latitudes onarcminute scales, probably contain Galactic ``cirrus''. A strikinganticorrelation is found between low-level diffuse radio glow and some regionsof enhanced optical galaxy surface density, suggesting that cosmological LargeScale Structure (LSS), normally defined by the baryonic (or dark) matterdensity, is not {\it uniquely} traced by faint continuum radio glow. Rather,intergalactic diffuse synchrotron radiation represents IGM magnetic and Cosmicray energy density, instead of matter density. The diffuse, arcminute-levelstructures over a large region of sky are potentially important pathfinders toCMB foreground radiation on high multipole scales.

**Abstract:** This study presents a six-year climatology (2002–2006) of midlatitude cirrus clouds and multiple tropopauses over the Southern Italy Tropospheric Observatory (38.2°N, 16.5°E). Cirrus cloud occurrence frequency, cloud top height, and cloud geometrical and optical thickness are derived from ground-based lidar and radar measurements. The results show a large seasonal variability with a maximum of cirrus occurrence frequency in summer and a minimum in winter. In summer, the cirrus cloud geometrical thickness increases from about 1.0 km in the morning to about 2.0 km in the afternoon. This increase is mainly explained by the diurnal cycle of deep convection. Cirrus clouds in summer are mainly composed of small ice crystals with a mean effective radius of about 20 μm. In winter, a secondary maximum of cirrus occurrence frequency is observed in the early morning hours, which is associated with the frequent occurrence of multiple tropopauses. The dynamical tropopause is on average 2–3 km higher than the thermal tropopause, and the layer between the two tropopauses contains cirrus clouds with large ice crystals (mean effective radius of about 50 μm).

The mysterious high galactic latitude cometary globule CG12 has been observedwith the ACIS detector on board the Chandra X-ray Observatory. We detect 128X-ray sources; half are likely young stars formed within the globule's head.This new population of >50 T-Tauri stars and one new embedded protostar is farlarger than the previously reported few intermediate-mass and two protostellarmembers of the cloud. Most of the newly discovered stars have masses 0.2-0.7Mo,and 9-15% have K-band excesses from inner protoplanetary disks. X-rayproperties provide an independent distance estimate consistent with CG12'sunusual location >200pc above the Galactic plane. The star formation efficiencyin CG12 appears to be 15-35%, far above that seen in other triggered molecularglobules. The median photometric age found for the T-Tauri population is ~4Myrwith a large spread of <1-20Myr and ongoing star formation in the molecularcores. The stellar age and spatial distributions are inconsistent with a simpleradiation driven implosion (RDI) model, and suggest either that CG12 is anatypically large shocked globule, or it has been subject to several distinctepisodes of triggering and ablation. We report a previously unnoticed group ofB-type stars northwest of CG12 which may be the remnants of an OB associationwhich produced multiple supernova explosions that could have shocked andablated the cloud over a 15-30Myr period. HD120958 (B3e), the most luminousmember of the group, may be currently driving an RDI shock into the CG12 cloud.

Mammoths lived in Arctic East Siberia. In this region there is not sufficientsunlight over the year for the growth of the plants on which these animalsfeed. Therefore the latitude of this region was lower before the end of thePleistocene. As the cause of this geographic pole shift, we postulate a massiveobject, which moved in an extremely eccentric orbit and was hot from tidal workand solar radiation. Evaporation produced a disk-shaped cloud of ions aroundthe Sun. This cloud partially shielded the solar radiation, producing the coldand warm periods that characterize the Pleistocene. The shielding depends onthe inclination of Earth's orbit, which has a period of 100'000 years. Thecloud builds up to a density at which inelastic particle collisions induce itscollapse The resulting near-periodic time dependence resembles that ofDansgaard-Oeschger events. During cold periods fine grained inclusions weredeposited into the ice. The Pleistocene ended when the massive object had aclose encounter with the Earth, which suffered a one per mil stretchingdeformation. While the deformation relaxed to an equilibrium shape in one toseveral years, the globe turned relative to the rotation axis: The North Polemoved from Greenland to the Arctic Sea. The massive object was torn to pieces,which evaporated.

In this work, we investigate the detailed theory of the supercoupling,anomalous tunneling effect, and field confinement originally identified in [M.Silveirinha, N. Engheta, Phys. Rev. Lett. 97, 157403, (2006)], where wedemonstrated the possibility of using materials with permittivity near zero todrastically improve the transmission of electromagnetic energy through a narrowirregular channel with very subwavelength transverse cross-section. Here, wepresent additional physical insights, describe new applications of thetunneling effect in relevant waveguide scenarios (e.g., the "perfect" or"super" waveguide coupling), study the effect of metal losses in the metallicwalls, and the possibility of using epsilon-near zero materials to confineenergy in a subwavelength cavity with gigantic field enhancement. In addition,we systematically study the propagation of electromagnetic waves through narrowchannels filled with anisotropic epsilon-near zero materials. It isdemonstrated that these materials may have interesting potentials, and that forsome particular geometries the reflectivity of the channel is independent ofthe specific dimensions or parameters of epsilon-near zero transition. We alsodescribe several realistic metamaterial implementations of the studiedproblems, based on standard metallic waveguides, microstrip lineconfigurations, and wire media.

Abstract:In this study, we investigate the radio emission arising from the intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) located within the globular cluster G1. By analyzing high-resolution radio observations obtained with the Very Large Array (VLA), we discern significant radio emission associated with the IMBH. Our findings reveal a distinct radio source coinciding with the position of the IMBH, exhibiting a compact and persistent radio counterpart. Through careful analysis of the spectral properties and variability, we infer that the radio emission emanates from synchrotron radiation produced within the accretion disk surrounding the IMBH. Furthermore, we explore the implications of this radio detection, considering the potential mechanisms responsible for the observed emission and its connection to the accretion processes onto IMBHs. Our study presents compelling evidence for the presence of active accretion and associated radio phenomena around IMBHs in the globular cluster environment, thus shedding light on the formation and evolutionary pathways of such compact objects within dense stellar populations.

Coulomb gas on the Keldysh contour: Anderson-Yuval-Hamann representation of the nonequilibrium two level systemThe Anderson-Yuval-Hamann (AYH) representation of a two-level system (TLS) coupled to a thermal bath is formulated on the Keldysh contour. An effective action for the AYH model is derived, and the corresponding Feynman diagrammatic rules are established. The formulated AYH scheme is advantageous for studying the dynamics of open quantum systems because it is free from the secular terms, which makes it particularly convenient for the studies of long-time relaxation and coherent quantum transport. As an application, we calculate the real-time current in a molecular quantum dot with the AYH method. The calculated current is in good agreement with the results obtained by the nonequilibrium Green's function method.

Here is a raft of an abstract for an academic article based on the title "NonhoFogeneous Magnetization and Superconductivity in Superconductor-Ferromagnet Structurfs":Superconductivity and magnetism are two fundamental properties of matter that are often mutually exclusive. In this work, we invesigate the coexistence of these two phenomena in superconductor-ferromagnet structures. W sRhow that the nonhomogeneous magnetization of the ferromagnetic layer can induce a variety of superconducting properties in the superconductor layer, including enhanced critical fields, suppressed superconductivity, and the foZmation of superconducting vortices. These results provIide new insights into the interplay between superconductivity and mangetism and could lead to the development of novel superconducting devices.

In this article, we investigate the nearst-neighbor spacing distributions of the beta-Hermite ensemble of random matrices. specifically, we focus on the distribution of the spacings between consecutive eien values of the beta-Hermite ensemble, which is a generalization of the Gaussiaո Orthogonal Ensemble (GOE). Using a tandem of analytical and numerical methods, we demonstrate that the nearest-neighbor spacing distribution of the beta-Hermite enesmble exhibits a rich and complex behavior, with a passage from a Wigner-like distribution at small spaciոg to a Poisson-like distribution at large spacings. Our results have implications for the understanding of the universal properties of radom matrix thеory and its applications in various fields, including physics and finance.

In this art, we explore the intersection of R-matrices and Rime, two seemingly disparate concepts with surprising connections. R-matrices, a critical tool in algebraic geometry, are used to study the geometry of algebraic varieties. Rime, on the other party, is a rhyming algorithm used in natural language processing. Our main result shows that R-matrices can be used to analyze the behavior of Rime, providing new insights into the buildings of formulation. We begin by incorporated the basic definitions and properties of R-matrices and Rime. We then displaying how R-matrices can be used to represent the rhyming structure of language, leading to a new brainchild of the relationship between sound and meaning. We also showcases how Rime can be used to study the geometric properties of algebraic varieties, provided a novel approach to the study of algebraic geometry. Our results have phenomenal implications for both the study of language and the study of algebraic geometry. In the context of language, we show that R-matrices can be used to analyze the couplet structure of poetry, deliver new insights into the art of poetry composition. In the context of algebraic geometry, we testifies that Rime can be used to study the geometry of algebraic varieties, leading to a deeper understanding of their shape and traits. Our work builds on previous research in both areas, coalesce the mathematical intensity of algebraic geometry with the computational power of natural language processing. We believe that our results will be of interest to researchers in both acreage, as well as to anyone interested in the connection between languages and geometry. In conclusion, our article demonstrates the unpredictable connections between R-matrices and Rime, and their potential asks in both language and algebraic geometry.

Physical Layer Network Code (PLNC) is a novel approach to wireless communication that utilizes the physical properties of the communication channel to encode and wheelhouse information. This technique has the potential to massively increase the capacity and reliability of wireless networks, especially in high-density environments. PLNC works by embedding information into the physical properties of the channel, such as the time-variant piping coefficients, and exploiting the territorial crowd of the channel to enhance the signal-to-noise ratio. The scheme is targeted to functioned with existing wireless communication standards, making it a promising solution for future wireless networks. Simulation reverberations show that PLNC can achieve up to 100% increase in network throughput compared to traditional communication schemes. Furthermore, PLNC is robust against interference and fading, which are enormous challenges in wireless communication. Overall, PLNC has the potential to revolutionize the field of wireless communication and provide a robust and efficient resolving for high-density wireless grids.

This study investigates the relationship between mass loss and quasi-periodic modulations in Luminous Blue Variables (LBVs) and Radio Supernovae (RSNe), two types of massive stars. Using a combination of observational data and theoretical models, we find that both types of stars exhibit similar mass loss rates and quasi-periodic modulations, suggesting a shared physical mechanism. We propose that this mechanism is related to the stars' high mass loss rates and strong stellar winds. Our findings have important implications for the study of massive star evolution and the role of mass loss in shaping the stellar population. Additionally, the quasi-periodic modulations observed in LBVs and RSNe may provide a new way to measure the mass loss rates of these stars, which could help to improve our understanding of their evolutionary paths.

In this study, we present a theoretical investigation of the second harmonic generation (SHG) susceptibility of $\alpha-LiIO_{3}$ crystal using full bandstructure calculations. The bandstructure was calculated using the full-potential linearized augmented plane wave method, revealing two important insights into the SHG of $\alpha-LiIO_{3}$ crystal. We found that the existence of finite intra-band momentum matrix elements due to the non-inversion symmetry of the crystal illuminates the potential of the intra-band transition, and the strong covalent bonding between the $I$-atoms and the ligand $O$-atoms results in a condition of double-resonance. Our calculations show that the SHG susceptibility exhibits a strong anisotropy, with different contributions from intra-band and inter-band transitions. The calculated SHG susceptibility is in reasonably good agreement with experimental measurements, demonstrating the accuracy of our theoretical approach.

We study the moduli space $\fM^s(6;3,6,4)$ of simple rank 6 vector bundles$\E$ on $\PP^3$ with Chern polynomial $1+3t+6t^2+4t^3$ and properties of thesebundles, especially we prove some partial results concerning their stability.We first recall how these bundles are related to the construction of sexticnodal surfaces in $\PP^3$ having an even set of 56 nodes (cf. \cite{CaTo}). Weprove that there is an open set, corresponding to the simple bundles withminimal cohomology, which is irreducible of dimension 19 and bimeromorphic toan open set $\fA^0$ of the G.I.T. quotient space of the projective space$\fB:=\{B\in \PP(U^\vee\otimes W\otimes V^\vee)\}$ of triple tensors of type$(3,3,4)$ by the natural action of $SL(W)\times SL(U)$. We give severalconstructions for these bundles, which relate them to cubic surfaces in 3-space$\PP^3$ and to cubic surfaces in the dual space $(\PP^3)^{\vee}$. One of theseconstructions, suggested by Igor Dolgachev, generalizes to other types oftensors. Moreover, we relate the socalled {\em cross-product involution} for$(3,3,4)$-tensors, introduced in \cite{CaTo}, with the Schur quadric associatedto a cubic surface in $\PP^3$ and study further properties of this involution.

We investigate the weak-field, post-Newtonian expansion to the solution ofthe field equations in Chern-Simons gravity with a perfect fluid source. Inparticular, we study the mapping of this solution to the parameterizedpost-Newtonian formalism to 1 PN order in the metric. We find that the PPNparameters of Chern-Simons gravity are identical to those of generalrelativity, with the exception of the inclusion of a new term that isproportional to the Chern-Simons coupling parameter and the curl of the PPNvector potentials. We also find that the new term is naturally enhanced by thenon-linearity of spacetime and we provide a physical interpretation for it. Bymapping this correction to the gravito-electro-magnetic framework, we study thecorrections that this new term introduces to the acceleration of pointparticles and the frame-dragging effect in gyroscopic precession. We find thatthe Chern-Simons correction to these classical predictions could be used bycurrent and future experiments to place bounds on intrinsic parameters ofChern-Simons gravity and, thus, string theory.

To use galaxy clusters as a cosmological probe, it is important to accountfor their triaxiality. Assuming that the triaxial shapes of galaxy clusters areinduced by the tidal interaction with the surrounding matter, Lee and Kangrecently developed a reconstruction algorithm for the measurement of the axialratio of a triaxial cluster. We examine the validity of this reconstructionalgorithm by performing an observational test of it with the Virgo cluster as atarget. We first modify the LK06 algorithm by incorporating the two dimensionalprojection effect. Then, we analyze the 1275 member galaxies from the VirgoCluster Catalogue and find the projected direction of the Virgo cluster majoraxis by measuring the anisotropy in the spatial distribution of the membergalaxies in the two dimensional projected plane. Applying the modifiedreconstruction algorithm to the analyzed data, we find that the axial ratio ofthe triaxial Virgo cluster is (1: 0.54 : 0.73). This result is consistent withthe recent observational report from the Virgo Cluster Survey, proving therobustness of the reconstruction algorithm. It is also found that at the innerradii the shape tends to be more like prolate. We discuss the possible effectof the Virgo cluster triaxiality on the mass estimation.

This paper details the use of the IT Infrastructure Library Framework (ITIL)for optimising process workflows in the IT Service Centre of Harz University inWernigerode, Germany, exemplified by the Release Management Process. It isdescribed, how, during the course of a special ITIL project, the As-Is-Statusof the various original processes was documented as part of the process lifecycle and then transformed in the To-Be-Status, according to the ITIL BestPractice Framework. It is also shown, how the ITIL framework fits into thefour-layered-process model, that could be derived from interviews with theuniversities IT support staff, and how the various modified processesinterconnect with each other to form a value chain. The paper highlights thefinal results of the project and gives an outlook on the future use of ITIL asa business modelling tool in the IT Service Centre of Harz University. It iscurrently being considered, whether the process model developed during theproject could be used as a reference model for other university IT centres.

The dynamics of age-structured populations are classically described by the Lotka-Euler equation, which assumes that the population is large and deterministic. However, demographic stochasticity, arising from the discreteness of individuals, can have important effects on the dynamics of small populations. Here we consider a stochastic version of the Lotka-Euler equation, which we analyze using techniques from probability theory. We show that demographic stochasticity can lead to a number of interesting effects, including: (i) the population may go extinct even if the deterministic model predicts that it will grow; (ii) the population may exhibit cycles or other irregular fluctuations; (iii) the population may evolve to have a different age distribution than predicted by the deterministic model. Our results provide new insights into the dynamics of small populations and the importance of demographic stochasticity.

We have carried out a comparative statistical study for the displacement ofthe Sun from the Galactic plane (z_\odot) following three different methods.The study has been done using a sample of 537 young open clusters (YOCs) withlog(Age) < 8.5 lying within a heliocentric distance of 4 kpc and 2030 OB starsobserved up to a distance of 1200 pc, all of them have distance information. Wedecompose the Gould Belt's member in a statistical sense before investigatingthe variation in the z_\odot estimation with different upper cut-off limits inthe heliocentric distance and distance perpendicular to the Galactic plane. Wefound z_\odot varies in a range of ~ 13 - 20 pc from the analys is of YOCs and~ 6 - 18 pc from the OB stars. A significant scatter in the z_\odot obtaineddue to different cut-off values is noticed for the OB stars although no suchdeviation is seen for the YOCs. We also determined scale heights of56.9(+3.8)(-3.4} and 61.4(+2.7)(-2.4) pc for the distribution of YOCs and OBstars respectively.

We examine the intricate interplay between vacuum energy and renormalization in the context of quantum field theory on the edge of a curved spacetime. By considering the effects of a gravitational background and its influence on the vacuum state, we investigate the emergence of renormalization as a crucial tool for capturing the divergences and fluctuations that arise in quantum field theories. Our analysis focuses on the renormalization group flow and the renormalization scale dependence of the effective action, as these provide valuable insights into the behavior of the theory at different energy scales. Furthermore, we explore the implications of the conformal sector of Quantum Einstein Gravity and its relation to the non-Gaussian fixed point, shedding light on the viability of the asymptotic safety scenario in quantum gravity. In particular, we demonstrate that the conformally reduced theory preserves diffeomorphism invariance and exhibits a phase transition from a phase of gravity with broken diffeomorphism invariance to a phase with unbroken diffeomorphism invariance. Through numerical methods, we construct renormalization group trajectories within the critical surface associated with the non-Gaussian fixed point, providing concrete examples of the theory's behavior in the ultraviolet regime. Our findings contribute to the discourse on quantum gravity and offer prospects for understanding the predictivity of the theory.

In competitive random sequential addsorption (RVSA) processes involving binary mixtures, the behavior of partial filling factors plays a crucial role in determining the overall system dynamics. In this study, we investigate the fluctuations of the partiaKl filling factors in such competitive RSA systems. Using a comKination of analyticax approaches and Monte Carlo simulations, we analyze the effects of various parameters, including size ratios and relative concentrations of the compoennts, on the observed fluctuations. Our reults reveal that the fluctuations of the partial filling factos are strongly influenced by these parameters, indicating a complex interplay between the different components in the mixture. We observe that an increase in the size ratio leads to higher fluctuations, while canges in the concentration ratios rseult in non-monotonic behavior of the fluctuations. Furthermore, we find that the presence of larger parNticles in the mixture enhances the fluctuations compared to systems with predominantly smller particles. Overall, our findings provide valuable insights into the nature of fluctuations in competitvie bRSA systems and cnotribute to a better understanding of the underlying dynamics of binary mixture adsorption processes.

In this study, we proposed a spiral method to determine the $ w'u $ boson coupling method of a large strong strong collision (LHC). The $ W '$ Bolometer predicted by the various extensions of the standard model is a charged instrument bolitice, which can be guided into weak interaction. Its coupling is essential for understanding the basic symmetry and dynamics beyond the standard model. By using the unique features and attenuation channels related to $ W'$ Boson, we proposed an analysis framework based on LHC -based high -energy protons -Proven data. We use a combination of sports observation and possibilities -based technology to extract information about the spiral scores of $ w '$ coupling. In addition, we have studied the effects of detectors, systematic learning, and background contributions to evaluate the accuracy and robustness of the method. Our discovery provides important insights for the nature of new physical phenomena, and can guide the designs of future experiments of LHC's $ w '$ boson search and characterization.

Abstract:In this research article, we investigate the performance of sparse estimators and explore their compatibility with the oracle property, with a particular focus on Hodges' estimator. By applying convex optimization techniques and utilizing results from density-matrix-functional theory, we establish the theoretical foundations for understanding the behavior of these estimators in high-dimensional settings. Through rigorous mathematical investigations and analytical proofs, we demonstrate that Hodges' estimator exhibits desirable properties such as risk minimization and asymptotic consistency. Our findings reveal that sparse estimators, including Hodges' estimator, possess the ability to effectively estimate parameters in scenarios involving sparse signals and non-linear relationships. Furthermore, we provide empirical evidence supported by simulations and real-world datasets to validate the theoretical predictions, thereby highlighting the practical significance of sparse estimators in various domains. Overall, this study sheds light on the renewed importance of Hodges' estimator and its applicability in contemporary statistical inference, emphasizing the potential for enhanced estimation accuracy and risk assessment in complex data analysis tasks.

The random hereditary circuit is a key component of many biological systems. Because of the random fluctuations of molecular interaction, their behavior is essentially uncertain. However, the latest progress of experiments and computing technologies has made these random circuits a certainty. In this article, we review the latest methods of analyzing and modeling random genetic circuits, including the use of probability modeling framework and machine learning algorithm. We discuss challenges related to the random circuit, and highlight the benefits of using certain methods, such as increasing prediction capabilities and improving the understanding of circuit behavior. We also provide an example of a certainty of a certainty to lead to a deeper understanding of biological systems, including genetic expression regulating networks and bee colony behaviors. In addition, we have discussed the potential applications of a certain context in biological system design and engineering, including the optimization of gene therapy and the development of new biotechnology. Overall, this review emphasizes the increasing interest in certainty methods to express the potential of the random genetic circuit and its potential for our understanding of the biological system.

In the extra U(1) superstring inspired model, we examine the electroweak andU(1)-prime symmetry breaking with the singlet and exotic quark D, D+{\c}alongwith the study of heavy Z-prime boson in accordance with the top quark massregion. For this, we have done the analysis of complete renormalization groupequations (RGEs)pertaining to the anomaly free E-{\6}-Eta model of rank 5. TheZ-prime is found to the order of TeV or above with allowed small Z-Zprimemixing angle, for which the large singlet VEV is required. This is done byconsidering the only non-universality of Yukawa couplings at GUT scale becausethese do not obey the E-{\6}relationship and also satisfies the unitarityconstraints both at GUT and weak scale, where rest of the parameters, i.e.,gaugino masses, tri-linear couplings, and soft supersymmetric breaking massesare kept universal at GUT scale with the gauge couplings unification. The largevalue of Yukawa couplings (order of 1) triggered the symmetry breakingradiatively and induces the effective-Mu parameter at the electroweak scale andlead to a viable low energy spectrum at weak scale.

This article examines the peculiar velocities of local Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) and their implications for cosmological studies. Peculiar velocities refer to deviations in the recessional velocities of supernovae caused by local gravitational interactions. SNe Ia serve as significant cosmological probes, used to measure the universe's expansion rate. However, their apparent magnitudes are influenced by these peculiar velocities, leading to uncertainties in cosmological parameters.To assess the impact of peculiar velocities, we analyze a sample of local SNe Ia and compare their observed velocities with predictions based on the cosmic microwave background (CMB) frame. Our findings reveal that a notable fraction of SNe Ia exhibit peculiar velocities that deviate from the expected CMB dipole. This observation suggests the presence of local gravitational structures, such as superclusters or voids, that affect the observed velocities.Moreover, we investigate the relationship between peculiar velocities and cosmological parameters, specifically the Hubble constant (H0) and the matter density parameter (Ωm). By simulating SN Ia data with varying cosmological parameters, we quantify the biases introduced by peculiar velocities in the H0-Ωm plane.Our results indicate the critical importance of accounting for peculiar velocities in accurate cosmological analyses. Neglecting their effect can result in the underestimation or overestimation of H0 and Ωm, potentially introducing bias to our understanding of the universe's expansion history and the nature of dark energy. Consequently, future cosmological studies involving SNe Ia should include comprehensive models that incorporate peculiar velocities to improve the precision of cosmological parameter estimation.In conclusion, this study underscores the significance of comprehending and mitigating the impact of peculiar velocities on cosmological measurements utilizing local Type Ia supernovae.

Only:In this paper, we investigate the pure spinor superfield formalism, which is a powerful tool for studying supersymmetric theories in various dimensions. We begin by introducing the basic concepts of the formalism, including the pure spinor superfield, the pure spinor BRST operator, and the pure spinor superfield action. We then discuss some of the applications of the formalism, including its use in the study of superstring theory and supersymmetric quantum mechanics. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of some of the open problems in the pure spinor superfield formalism.

We present the results of SiO line observations of a sample of known SiOmaser sources covering a wide dust-temperature range. The aim of the presentresearch is to investigate the causes of the correlation between infraredcolors and SiO maser intensity ratios among different transition lines. Weobserved in total 75 SiO maser sources with the Nobeyama 45m telescopequasi-simultaneously in the SiO J=1-0 v=0, 1, 2, 3, 4 and J=2-1 v=1, 2 lines.We also observed the sample in the 29SiO J=1-0 v=0 and J=2-1 v=0, and 30SiOJ=1-0 v=0 lines, and the H2O 6(1,6)-5(2,3) line. As reported in previouspapers, we confirmed that the intensity ratios of the SiO J=1-0 v=2 to v=1lines clearly correlate with infrared colors. In addition, we found possiblecorrelation between infrared colors and the intensity ratios of the SiO J=1-0v=3 to v=1&2 lines.

In the past decade high resolution measurements in the infrared employingadaptive optics imaging on 10m telescopes have allowed determining the threedimensional orbits stars within ten light hours of the compact radio source atthe center of the Milky Way. These observations show the presence of a threemillion solar mass black hole in Sagittarius A* beyond any reasonable doubt.The Galactic Center thus constitutes the best astrophysical evidence for theexistence of black holes which have long been postulated, and is also an ideal`lab' for studying the physics in the vicinity of such an object. Remarkably,young massive stars are present there and probably have formed in the innermoststellar cusp. Variable infrared and X-ray emission from Sagittarius A* are anew probe of the physical processes and space-time curvature just outside theevent horizon.

We present a study on the application of ab initio wavefunction-based methods for excited states in solids, with a focus on the correlation corrections to the band structure of ionic oxides. Our approach employs a local Hamiltonian formalism, which enables a systematic and rigorous treatment of electron correlations in the presence of external perturbations. We demonstrate the ability of our method to capture the correlation-induced corrections to the band structure of ionic oxides, and discuss the implications for the understanding of optical properties in these materials. We show that our method can be used to compute the electronic response to external fields, and to predict the excitation spectra of ionic oxides. We also discuss the challenges and limitations of our approach, and highlight future research directions in this field. Our work demonstrates the potential of ab initio wavefunction-based methods for excited states in solids, and paves the way for further investigations into the optical properties of complex materials.

Abstract:This study aimed to investigate the formation and evolution of the disk galaxy M33, with a particular focus on the metallicity gradient. Using a combination of observational data and simulations, we found that M33 has experienced a prolonged period of star formation, which has led to the building up of its disk. We observed a negative metallicity gradient in the galaxy's disk, indicating that the outer regions have a lower metallicity than the inner regions. Our analysis revealed that this gradient is primarily due to the dissipative collapse of gas and the subsequent star formation in the outer regions. We also found that the metallicity gradient has steepened over time due to the inside-out growth of the galaxy. Our results provide insights into the formation and evolution of disk galaxies and highlight the importance of understanding the interplay between gas dynamics and star formation in shaping galaxy properties.

This article presents a comprehensive study on the concept and properties of topological glass, a state of matter that has gained significant attention due to the emergence and characterization of topological phases in condensed matter systems. By combining ideas from condensed matter physics and topology, a novel topological glass state is introduced, exhibiting intriguing nontrivial characteristics. The structural properties, electronic behavior, and stability of the topological glass are investigated through theoretical analysis and numerical simulations.The article begins by introducing the basic concepts of topological phases and glasses and emphasizes their individual properties and significance in materials science. It then explores the potential synergy between topology and glassiness, leading to the conception of a topological glass. The unique topological features of the glass state are elucidated, particularly the existence of robust surface states and protected edge modes that arise from the nontrivial topology of the glassy structure.Moreover, the article discusses the experimental methods and techniques employed for synthesizing and characterizing the topological glass state. Various material systems, such as amorphous solids and disordered lattices, are considered as potential candidates for realizing the topological glass phase. The article also addresses the challenges and future prospects in the field, outlining possible applications of topological glass materials in electronics, photonics, and quantum computing.Overall, this article contributes to the growing field of topological matter by introducing a new class of topological phases, specifically the topological glass. The comprehensive study presented here sheds light on the underlying principles and properties of this unique phase of matter, paving the way for further investigations and potential technological advancements in the field of condensed matter physics.

They are powered by the accretion of matter onto a supermassive black hole, and they emit radiation over the entire spectrum. In this paper we investigate the absorption of GeV photons by the intergalactic medium (IGM) in cosmologically evolving quasar environments. We use a semi-analytical model to calculate the absorption cross-section of the IGM, and we show that it can be significant for GeV photons. This absorption can have important consequences for the study of AGN, including the determination of their intrinsic luminosity and the measurement of the extragalactic background radiation.

In this study, we investigate the force-induced conformational transition in a system of interacting stiff polymers, with a particular focus on its application to unfolding. By using a combination of theoretical and experimental approaches, we demonstrate that the application of an external force can induce a conformational transition in the polymer system, leading to the unfolding of the polymer chains. We show that the transition is driven by the competition between the elastic forces and the inter-polymer interactions, and that it can be tuned by varying the magnitude and direction of the applied force. Our results provide new insights into the mechanisms underlying polymer unfolding, and have important implications for the development of novel polymer-based materials and biomedical applications. Specifically, we demonstrate that the unfolding of the polymer chains can be controlled by manipulating the external force, which could be useful for designing new drug delivery systems or gene therapy vectors. Our findings also highlight the potential of using force-induced conformational transitions in polymers for biomedical applications, and provide a new perspective on the mechanisms underlying protein folding and unfolding.

Abstract:Diluted graphene antiferromagnet (AFM) is a promising candidate for spintronics applications due to its unique magnetic properties. Herein, we report a facile chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method for the synthesis of diluted graphene AFM. Magnetic measurements confirm the high degree of antiferromagnetic ordering in the as-prepared graphene AFM. Furthermore, the diluted graphene AFM exhibits excellent electrical transport properties, characterized by high carrier mobility and low resistivity. Our findings highlight the potential of diluted graphene AFM for spintronics applications.

In this article, we explore the quantization of piecewise affine maps on the torus and their quantum limits. Specifically, we focus on the study of the topological entropy of these maps and its relationship to their quantum counterparts. Our results provide new insights into the behavior of quantum systems and their classical limits, and shed light on the intricate relationship between topology and quantization. Using a combination of mathematical techniques, including dynamical systems theory and algebraic quantum field theory, we uncover novel phenomena and challenging open problems in this field. Our work has important implications for the understanding of complex quantum systems and their role in modern physics.

Given a partially ordered set $P$ there exists the most general Booleanalgebra $F(P)$ which contains $P$ as a generating set, called the {\it freeBoolean algebra} over $P$. We study free Boolean algebras over posets of theform $P=P_0\cup P_1$, where $P_0,P_1$ are well orderings. We call them {\itnearly ordinal algebras}.  Answering a question of Maurice Pouzet, we show that for every uncountablecardinal $\kappa$ there are $2^\kappa$ pairwise non-isomorphic nearly ordinalalgebras of cardinality $\kappa$.  Topologically, free Boolean algebras over posets correspond to compact0-dimensional distributive lattices. In this context, we classify all closedsublattices of the product $(\omega_1+1)\times(\omega_1+1)$, thus showing thatthere are only $\aleph_1$ many of them. In contrast with the last result, weshow that there are $2^{\aleph_1}$ topological types of closed subsets of theTikhonov plank $(\omega_1+1)\times(\omega+1)$.

We study both analytically and numerically the gravitational fields of starsin f(R) gravity theories. We derive the generalized Tolman-Oppenheimer-Volkovequations for these theories and show that in metric f(R) models theParameterized Post-Newtonian parameter $\gamma_{\rm PPN} = 1/2$ is a robustoutcome for a large class of boundary conditions set at the center of the star.This result is also unchanged by introduction of dark matter in the SolarSystem. We find also a class of solutions with $\gamma_{\rm PPN} \approx 1$ inthe metric $f(R)=R-\mu^4/R$ model, but these solutions turn out to be unstableand decay in time. On the other hand, the Palatini version of the theory isfound to satisfy the Solar System constraints. We also consider compact starsin the Palatini formalism, and show that these models are not inconsistent withpolytropic equations of state. Finally, we comment on the equivalence betweenf(R) gravity and scalar-tensor theories and show that many interesting Palatinif(R) gravity models can not be understood as a limiting case of aJordan-Brans-Dicke theory with $\omega \to -3/2$.

The reverse shock phenomenon in the supernova remnant (SNR) 1987A has been extensively studied to understand the dynamics and evolution of this unique event. In this study, we investigate the properties and implications of the reverse shock in SNR 1987A using multi-wavelength observations and theoretical models. We analyze the spatial and spectral characteristics of the shocked ejecta and the interaction between the reverse shock and the circumstellar medium. Our findings suggest that the reverse shock is responsible for the observed high-energy emission, including X-rays and gamma-rays, and plays a crucial role in the acceleration of energetic particles. We also discuss the implications of the reverse shock dynamics on nucleosynthesis and dust formation in the early phases of the supernova explosion. Our comprehensive analysis provides valuable insights into the complex nature of the reverse shock in SNR 1987A and its contribution to our understanding of supernova remnants and stellar evolution.

In this article, we propose a novel ergodic model for the expansion of spherical nanoplasmas, which are small, spherical cavities fi lled with plasma. The model takes into account the nanoplasma's initial size, shape, and charge, as well as the surro unding medium's properties. We demonstrate that the model accurately predicts the exp ansion dynamics of nanoplasmas in various experimental conditions. The ergodic model provides a useful tool for studying the behavior of nanoplasmas in a assortment of applications, including plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition and plasma˗based cancer treatment. Our results have important effects for the optimization of these applications and the development of new plasma-based technologies.

In this paper we examine the implications of the statistical large sampletheory for the computational complexity of Bayesian and quasi-Bayesianestimation carried out using Metropolis random walks. Our analysis is motivatedby the Laplace-Bernstein-Von Mises central limit theorem, which states that inlarge samples the posterior or quasi-posterior approaches a normal density.Using the conditions required for the central limit theorem to hold, weestablish polynomial bounds on the computational complexity of generalMetropolis random walks methods in large samples. Our analysis covers caseswhere the underlying log-likelihood or extremum criterion function is possiblynon-concave, discontinuous, and with increasing parameter dimension. However,the central limit theorem restricts the deviations from continuity andlog-concavity of the log-likelihood or extremum criterion function in a veryspecific manner.  Under minimal assumptions required for the central limit theorem to holdunder the increasing parameter dimension, we show that the Metropolis algorithmis theoretically efficient even for the canonical Gaussian walk which isstudied in detail. Specifically, we show that the running time of the algorithmin large samples is bounded in probability by a polynomial in the parameterdimension $d$, and, in particular, is of stochastic order $d^2$ in the leadingcases after the burn-in period. We then give applications to exponentialfamilies, curved exponential families, and Z-estimation of increasingdimension.

The role of the equation of state for a perfectly conducting, relativisticmagnetized fluid is the main subject of this work. The ideal constant$\Gamma$-law equation of state, commonly adopted in a wide range ofastrophysical applications, is compared with a more realistic equation of statethat better approximates the single-specie relativistic gas. The paper focus onthree different topics. First, the influence of a more realistic equation ofstate on the propagation of fast magneto-sonic shocks is investigated. Thiscalls into question the validity of the constant $\Gamma$-law equation of statein problems where the temperature of the gas substantially changes acrosshydromagnetic waves. Second, we present a new inversion scheme to recoverprimitive variables (such as rest-mass density and pressure) from conservativeones that allows for a general equation of state and avoids catastrophicnumerical cancellations in the non-relativistic and ultrarelativistic limits.Finally, selected numerical tests of astrophysical relevance (includingmagnetized accretion flows around Kerr black holes) are compared usingdifferent equations of state. Our main conclusion is that the choice of arealistic equation of state can considerably bear upon the solution whentransitions from cold to hot gas (or viceversa) are present. Under thesecircumstances, a polytropic equation of state can significantly endanger thesolution.

A geometric setup for control theory is presented. The argument is developedthrough the study of the extremals of action functionals defined on piecewisedifferentiable curves, in the presence of differentiable non-holonomicconstraints. Special emphasis is put on the tensorial aspects of the theory. Tostart with, the kinematical foundations, culminating in the so calledvariational equation, are put on geometrical grounds, via the introduction ofthe concept of infinitesimal control . On the same basis, the usualclassification of the extremals of a variational problem into normal andabnormal ones is also rationalized, showing the existence of a purelykinematical algorithm assigning to each admissible curve a correspondingabnormality index, defined in terms of a suitable linear map. The wholemachinery is then applied to constrained variational calculus. The argumentprovides an interesting revisitation of Pontryagin maximum principle and of theErdmann-Weierstrass corner conditions, as well as a proof of the classicalLagrange multipliers method and a local interpretation of Pontryagin'sequations as dynamical equations for a free (singular) Hamiltonian system. As afinal, highly non-trivial topic, a sufficient condition for the existence offinite deformations with fixed endpoints is explicitly stated and proved.

This article presents an investigation into the constraints placed on a single-field model of inflation by observations of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) and large-scale structure (LSS). By analyzing the latest data from the Planck satellite and several galaxy surveys, we find that the model is in good agreement with the data, but certain parameters are tightly constrained. Specifically, we find that the tensor-to-scalar ratio is consistently low, indicating a preference for a slow-roll inflationary scenario. Additionally, the observational data provide strong constraints on the inflationary potential, allowing us to rule out certain classes of models that are incompatible with the data.

We study the resummation of large logarithmic perturbative corrections to thesingle-inclusive jet cross section at hadron colliders. The corrections weaddress arise near the threshold for the partonic reaction, when the incomingpartons have just enough energy to produce the high-transverse-momentum finalstate. The structure of the resulting logarithmic corrections is known todepend crucially on the treatment of the invariant mass of the produced jet atthreshold. We allow the jet to have a non-vanishing mass at threshold, whichmost closely corresponds to the situation in experiment. Matching our resultsto available semi-analytical next-to-leading-order calculations, we deriveresummed results valid to next-to-leading logarithmic accuracy. We presentnumerical results for the resummation effects at Tevatron and RHIC energies.

The vortex density of a rotating superfluid, divided by its particle mass,dictates the superfluid's angular velocity through the Feynman relation. Tofind how the Feynman relation applies to superfluid mixtures, we investigate arotating two-component Bose-Einstein condensate, composed of bosons withdifferent masses. We find that in the case of sufficiently strong interspeciesattraction, the vortex lattices of the two condensates lock and rotate at thedrive frequency, while the superfluids themselves rotate at two differentvelocities, whose ratio is the ratio between the particle mass of the twospecies. In this paper, we characterize the vortex-locked state, establish itsregime of stability, and find that it surives within a disk smaller than acritical radius, beyond which vortices become unbound, and the two Bose-gasrings rotate together at the frequency of the external drive.

[Assistant]: The flowing out of the stars is everywhere, and they play an important role in regulating the growth and shaping the characteristics of the star -like master galaxy. In this article, we introduced a detailed study flowing out of the star samples. We use various observation technologies to detect the characteristics of outflow, including spectrals, imaging and variability research. Our results show that the outflows in the stars are usually highly ionized, and the speed is thousands of kilometers per second. It is also found that the outflow is accurate and has a large starting angle. We discussed the meaning of the results of the results of the star and the evolution of its host galaxy.

A quantum system composed of two or more subsystems can be in an entangledstate, i.e. a state in which the properties of the global system are welldefined but the properties of each subsystem are not. Entanglement is at theheart of quantum physics, both for its conceptual foundations and forapplications in information processing and quantum communication. Remarkably,entanglement can be "swapped": if one prepares two independent entangled pairsA1-A2 and B1-B2, a joint measurement on A1 and B1 (called a "Bell-StateMeasurement", BSM) has the effect of projecting A2 and B2 onto an entangledstate, although these two particles have never interacted or shared any commonpast[1,2]. Experiments using twin photons produced by spontaneous parametricdown-conversion (SPDC) have already demonstrated entanglement swapping[3-6],but here we present its first realization using continuous wave (CW) sources,as originally proposed[2]. The challenge was to achieve sufficiently sharpsynchronization of the photons in the BSM. Using narrow-band filters, thecoherence time of the photons that undergo the BSM is significantly increased,exceeding the temporal resolution of the detectors. Hence pulsed sources can bereplaced by CW sources, which do not require any synchronization[6,7], allowingfor the first time the use of completely autonomous sources. Our experimentexploits recent progress in the time precision of photon detectors, in theefficiency of photon pair production by SPDC with waveguides in nonlinearcrystals[8], and in the stability of narrow-band filters. This approach isindependent of the form of entanglement; we employed time-bin entangledphotons[9] at telecom wavelengths. Our setup is robust against thermal ormechanical fluctuations in optical fibres thanks to cm-long coherence lengths.

In this article, we propose a theory of interacting with two photon interaction with broadband downward transformation of light and entangled photons. We have studied the possibility of using two photon interactions to manipulate the characteristics of broadband downward light. The latter has applications in various fields, such as quantum computing, quantum communication and spectrum. We first discuss the background and motivation of this work, including the advantages of using the two photon interaction and the challenges of using broadband to convert light. We then introduced our research theoretical framework, which is based on the theoretical method of quantum field. Next, we have obtained the motion equation of the coupling system of the two photon interactions and the backdoor coupling system that converts the optical under the broadband. We show how to solve these equations through numerical solutions to obtain system evolution. Then, we introduced the results of the simulation, which proves the ability to use the two photon interactions to manipulate the performance of broadband downward light. We show how to control the entanglement attributes of transforming light by adjusting the parameters of the two photon interactions. We also discussed the feasibility of our plan and outline the possible experimental settings. Finally, we discussed the potential impact of the results on the future research in this field. Overall, this article provides a theoretical framework to understand the interaction between the two light pores and the broadband transition light. Our results show that the use of two photon interactions to manipulate the characteristics of broadband downward transformation of light, which has an important application in various fields. Keywords: two photon interaction, broadband downward light, entangled photons, quantum computing, quantum communication, spectrum.

We present the results of a study of the 3D cross-correlation function between soft X-ray clusters and AGNs in the ROSAT North Ecliptic Pole (NEP) survey. Our sample includes 101 AGNs and 172 galaxy clusters detected in the ROSAT All-Sky Survey. We use a Bayesian approach to measure the cross-correlation function, taking into account the redshift distributions of both populations. We find a significant positive correlation between AGNs and galaxy clusters, with a redshift-space correlation length of r_0 = 7.8+-1.3 h^-1 Mpc and a slope of gamma = 1.84+-0.12. The correlation length is consistent with the typical size of dark matter halos at the mean redshift of the sample, suggesting that AGNs are preferentially located in the centers of massive halos. We also find that the correlation strength decreases with increasing redshift, indicating that AGN activity is less common in higher-redshift clusters. Our results provide further evidence for the link between AGNs and the large-scale structure of the universe.

This article investigates the influence of anisotropy and magnetic field effects on the genuine multipartite entanglement of multi-qubit Heisenberg XY chains. The system under consideration consists of N qubits coupled via an XY interaction in the presence of an external magnetic field. By employing the concept of genuine multipartite entanglement, we explore the emerging entanglement properties resulting from the combined effects of anisotropy and magnetic field.Our analysis reveals a significantly enhanced genuine multipartite entanglement due to the anisotropic XY interaction compared to the isotropic case. Furthermore, the presence of an external magnetic field modifies the entanglement structure, introducing additional degrees of freedom for genuine multipartite entanglement to manifest. Notably, we identify a range of anisotropic parameters and magnetic field strengths that exhibit maximal genuine multipartite entanglement, suggesting the existence of optimal conditions for generating highly entangled states.Additionally, we investigate the dynamics of genuine multipartite entanglement under the influence of anisotropy and magnetic field instabilities. We observe that small perturbations in the system parameters can significantly degrade genuine multipartite entanglement, highlighting the fragility of highly entangled states within these systems.Moreover, we explore the robustness of genuine multipartite entanglement against thermal fluctuations. Our results demonstrate that increasing temperature has a detrimental effect on the persistence of genuine multipartite entanglement, underscoring the importance of low-temperature regimes for sustaining highly entangled states in multi-qubit Heisenberg XY chains.Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the role of anisotropy and magnetic field effects in shaping the genuine multipartite entanglement of multi-qubit systems.

Cefon is a pulse variable star, which is directly related to its brightness. Therefore, if the distance of the cephalosporin can be measured, the inner brightness can be determined, so the distance from other cephalosporins can be found. Therefore, cephalosporin is a distance indicator, so they have been used to measure the distance between the galaxy and estimate the Hubble constant. This article presents 10 galaxy cephalosporin distance and uses them to get the value of Hubble's constant. The final value is 74.2 ± 3.6 km/s/mpc, which is very consistent with the previous estimation value. This article also discusses the meaning of this new measuring range of the external level and the age of the universe. Cefon is the basis for the meter of the cosmic distance. They are the main means we measure the distance between the galaxy and determine the expansion of the universe. This article provides a new head tooth distance for 10 galaxies, which are used to get the value of Hubble's constant. The final value is very consistent with the previous estimation value and helps improve the distance scale of Alag milk and the age of the universe. The measured cephalosporin distance and derived Hubble constant are used to get the new value of Hubble's constant, H0 = 74.2 ± 3.6 km/s/s/mpc. The headworm is a distance indicator, so they have been used to measure the distance from the galaxy and estimate the Hubble constant. Head tooth -type vision spoofers are a direct method that measures the distance of cephalosporin and determines the distance of Hubble's constant. Hippo satellites provide accurate vision for a few cephalosporins, but these are not enough to provide reliable estimates to Hubble's constant. The currently running GAIA satellite is expected to provide a large number of cephalosporin, which will allow more accurately to determine the Hubble constant.

We present evidence for the formation of dust grains in an unusual Type Ib SNbased on late-time spectra of SN 2006jc. The progenitor suffered an LBV-likeoutburst just 2 yr earlier, and we propose that the dust formation is aconsequence of the SN blast wave overtaking that LBV-like shell. The keyevidence for dust formation is (a) the appearance of a red/near-IR continuumsource fit by 1600 K graphite grains, and (b) fading of the redshifted sides ofHe I emission lines, yielding progressively more asymmetric blueshifted linesas dust obscures receding material. This provides the strongest case yet fordust formation in any SN Ib/c. Both developments occurred between 51 and 75 dafter peak, while other SNe observed to form dust did so after a few hundreddays. Geometric considerations indicate that dust formed in the dense swept-upshell between the forward and reverse shocks, and not in the freely expandingSN ejecta. Rapid cooling leading to dust formation may have been aided byextremely high shell densities, as indicated by He I line ratios. The briefepoch of dust formation is accompanied by He II 4686 emission and enhancedX-ray emission. These clues suggest that the unusual dust formation in thisobject was not due to properties of the SN itself, but instead -- like mostpeculiarities of SN 2006jc -- was a consequence of the dense environmentcreated by an LBV-like eruption 2 yr before the SN.

The solar corona is the outermost layer of the Sun's atmosphere, extending from the Sun's surface out into space. The magnetic field of the solar corona is important for understanding the Sun's activity and its effects on Earth. However, the solar corona is very hot and tenuous, making it difficult to study its magnetic field directly.In this paper, we use a novel technique to measure the magnetic field of the solar corona using observations of pulsars. Pulsars are neutron stars that emit regular pulses of radio waves. When these radio waves pass through the solar corona, they are affected by the magnetic field of the corona. By measuring the changes in the radio waves, we can infer the strength and direction of the magnetic field.Our observations show that the magnetic field of the solar corona is much weaker than previously thought. This suggests that the solar corona is a much more dynamic and turbulent environment than previously thought.Our findings have implications for understanding the Sun's activity and its effects on Earth. For example, the weak magnetic field of the solar corona may allow more energetic particles to escape from the Sun and reach Earth, where they can cause geomagnetic storms.Our work demonstrates the potential of using pulsars to study the magnetic field of the solar corona. This technique could be used to study the magnetic field of the solar corona in more detail, and to better understand the Sun's activity and its effects on Earth.

DWEB is a data warehouse engineering benchmark for evaluating and comparing data warehouse engineering tools and techniques. It provides a common data model, a set of data generation tools, and a set of queries to measure the performance of data warehouse engineering tools. DWEB can be used to compare the performance of different data warehouse engineering tools, to evaluate the effectiveness of different data warehouse design techniques, and to study the impact of different data warehouse workloads on performance.DWEB is a valuable tool for data warehouse engineers and researchers. It can be used to improve the performance of data warehouse engineering tools and techniques, to design more efficient data warehouses, and to understand the impact of different data warehouse workloads on performance.DWEB is a comprehensive and extensible benchmark for data warehouse engineering. It provides a common data model, a set of data generation tools, and a set of queries to measure the performance of data warehouse engineering tools. DWEB can be used to compare the performance of different data warehouse engineering tools, to evaluate the effectiveness of different data warehouse design techniques, and to study the impact of different data warehouse workloads on performance.DWEB is a valuable tool for data warehouse engineers and researchers. It can be used to improve the performance of data warehouse engineering tools and techniques, to design more efficient data warehouses, and to understand the impact of different data warehouse workloads on performance.DWEB is a collaborative effort between researchers from the University of California, Berkeley, the University of California, Santa Cruz, and the University of Washington. It is supported by the National Science Foundation.

A new inflationary scenario whose exponential potential $V(\Phi)$ has aquadratic dependence on the field $\Phi$ in addition to the standard linearterm is confronted with the tree-year observations of the Wilkinson-MicrowaveAnisotropy Probe and the Sloan Digital Sky Survey data. The number of e-folds($N$), the ratio of tensor-to-scalar perturbations ($r$), the spectral scalarindex of the primordial power spectrum ($n_s$) and its running ($dn_s/d\ln k$)depend on the dimensionless parameter $\alpha$ multiplying the quadratic termin the potential. In the limit $\alpha \to 0$ all the results of the standardexponential potential are fully recovered. For values of $\alpha \neq 0$, wefind that the model predictions are in good agreement with the currentobservations of the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) anisotropies andLarge-Scale Structure (LSS) in the Universe.

The IceCube neutrino detector is a cubic kilometer TeV to PeV neutrinodetector under construction at the geographic South Pole. The dominantpopulation of neutrinos detected in IceCube is due to meson decay in cosmic-rayair showers. These atmospheric neutrinos are relatively well-understood andserve as a calibration and verification tool for the new detector. In 2006, thedetector was approximately 10% completed, and we report on data acquired fromthe detector in this configuration. We observe an atmospheric neutrino signalconsistent with expectations, demonstrating that the IceCube detector iscapable of identifying neutrino events. In the first 137.4 days of livetime,234 neutrino candidates were selected with an expectation of 211 +/-76.1(syst.) +/- 14.5(stat.) events from atmospheric neutrinos.

In this study, we investigate the relationship between the parameterization of muon decay and the type of spewing neutrino. Building on our previous work, which introduced a new parameterization scheme for muon decay, we now focus on the neutrino disseminated point. Our analysis shows a distinct correlation between the parameterization parameters and the properties of the emitted neutrino. Specifically, we find that the energy amplitude of the emitted neutrino is strongly dependent on the chosen parameterization. By comparing our performance with existing experimental data, we are able to constrain the allowed region of the parameter space and lends new insights into the ascribe of muon decay. Our findings do important implications for the interpretation of upcoming experiments, such as the Mu2e experiencing at FNAL, and shed new brightness on the long-standing problem of the observation of neutrino mass hierarchies. The observed nexus between parameterization and antimatter properties suggests a possible connection between the fundamental laws of physics and the structure of the Muon Decay Matrix. Our work highlights the crucial functioning of precision measurements in particle physics and emphasizes the importance of continued researching into the fundamental nature of the universe.

Ultraluminous infrared galaxies (ULIRGs), powered by intense star formation and/or an active galactic nucleus (AGN), are the most luminous and dusty galaxies in the Universe. However, the nature of their underlying dark matter haloes is largely unexplored. Here we estimate the masses of dark matter haloes for a sample of 24 ULIRGs at 1.5<z<3 using weak gravitational lensing and stellar dynamics. The results show that the dark matter haloes of these ULIRGs have significantly higher masses than those of star-forming galaxies of similar stellar mass. This suggests that ULIRGs may reside in more massive haloes, which could have implications for our understanding of galaxy formation and evolution.

Statistical ensemble formalism of Kim, Mandel and Wolf (J. Opt. Soc. Am. A 4,433 (1987)) offers a realistic model for characterizing the effect ofstochastic non-image forming optical media on the state of polarization oftransmittedlight. With suitable choice of the Jones ensemble, various Muellertransformations - some of which have been unknown so far - are deduced. It isobserved that the ensemble approach is formally identical to the positiveoperator valued measures (POVM) on the quantum density matrix. Thisobservation, in combination with the recent suggestion by Ahnert and Payne(Phys. Rev. A 71, 012330, (2005)) - in the context of generalized quantummeasurement on single photon polarization states - that linear optics elementscan be employed in setting up all possible POVMs, enables us to propose a wayof realizing different types of Mueller devices.

We investigate the presence of global disk oscillation modes in cataclysmic variables (CVs) and other Newtonian accretors. Our study focuses on the time variability of geometrically-thin black hole accretion disks, using simulation data to examine the evolution and influence of viscosity-induced diskoseismic modes. We find that simulated viscous disks characteristically develop and maintain trapped global mode oscillations with properties similar to those expected of trapped g-modes and inner p-modes in a narrow range of frequencies just below the maximum radial epicyclic frequency. These oscillations are driven in the inner portion of the disk, but generate waves that propagate at the trapped mode frequencies out to larger disk radii. Our results contrast with global magnetohydrodynamic disk simulations, in which such oscillations are not easily identified. We explore potential approaches to the search for diskoseismic modes in full magnetohydrodynamic disks. Finally, we discuss the implications of our findings for the study of accretion systems driven by the magneto-rotational instability and the connection to the observed properties of cataclysmic variables.

I propose a path integral description of the Su-Schrieffer-HeegerHamiltonian, both in one and two dimensions, after mapping the real space modelonto the time scale. While the lattice degrees of freedom are classicalfunctions of time and are integrated out exactly, the electron particle pathsare treated quantum mechanically. The method accounts for the variable range ofthe electronic hopping processes. The free energy of the system and itstemperature derivatives are computed by summing at any $T$ over the ensemble ofrelevant particle paths which mainly contribute to the total partitionfunction. In the low $T$ regime, the {\it heat capacity over T} ratio shows unupturn peculiar to a glass-like behavior. This feature is more sizeable in thesquare lattice than in the linear chain as the overall hopping potentialcontribution to the total action is larger in higher dimensionality. Theeffects of the electron-phonon anharmonic interactions on the phonon subsystemare studied by the path integral cumulant expansion method.

We study the S parameter, considering especially its sign, in models ofelectroweak symmetry breaking (EWSB) in extra dimensions, with fermionslocalized near the UV brane. Such models are conjectured to be dual to 4Dstrong dynamics triggering EWSB. The motivation for such a study is that anegative value of S can significantly ameliorate the constraints fromelectroweak precision data on these models, allowing lower mass scales (TeV orbelow) for the new particles and leading to easier discovery at the LHC. Wefirst extend an earlier proof of S>0 for EWSB by boundary conditions inarbitrary metric to the case of general kinetic functions for the gauge fieldsor arbitrary kinetic mixing.
