Specifically, we analyze the response of the reaction-diffusion system to weak periodic forcing and examine the conditions under which stochastic resonance occurs. Our results reveal that the presence of noise can enhance the system's sensitivity to weak signals, leading to a resonant amplification of the signal. We also explore the role of system parameters, such as the noise strength and the reaction rates, in shaping the stochastic resonance behavior. Furthermore, we discuss the implications of our findings for understanding and predicting the dynamics of reaction-diffusion systems in nonequilibrium conditions. Overall, our study provides new insights into the rich behavior of stochastic resonance in complex reaction-diffusion systems, highlighting the importance of noise in modulating their dynamical responses.

In the present study, we investigate the reduction of boundary effects in a spiral Magnetorotational Instability (MRI) experiment, PROMISE. The experiment is designed to study the MRI in a spiral geometry, which is relevant to the dynamics of accretion disks and other astrophysical systems. The spiral geometry introduces complex boundary effects that can affect the stability and dynamics of the MRI. To reduce these boundary effects, we employ a novel technique that manipulates the boundary conditions in the experimental setup. Our results show that this technique effectively reduces the impact of boundary effects on the MRI, allowing for a more accurate representation of the instability in the spiral geometry. The findings of this study have important implications for the design of future experiments studying the MRI in spiral geometries.

A review of theoretical and experimental studies of thermal effects insolid-state lasers is presented, with a special focus on diode-pumpedytterbium-doped materials. A large part of this review provides however generalinformation applicable to any kind of solid-state laser. Our aim here is not tomake a list of the techniques that have been used to minimize thermal effects,but instead to give an overview of the theoretical aspects underneath, and givea state-of-the-art of the tools at the disposal of the laser scientist tomeasure thermal effects. After a presentation of some general properties ofYb-doped materials, we address the issue of evaluating the temperature map inYb-doped laser crystals, both theoretically and experimentally. This is thefirst step before studying the complex problem of thermal lensing (part III).We will focus on some newly discussed aspects, like the definition of thethermo-optic coefficient: we will highlight some misleading interpretations ofthermal lensing experiments due to the use of the dn/dT parameter in a contextwhere it is not relevant. Part IV will be devoted to a state-of-the-art ofexperimental techniques used to measure thermal lensing. Eventually, in part V,we will give some concrete examples in Yb-doped materials, where theirpeculiarities will be pointed out.

The spectrum of meson and diquark excitations of dense quark matter isconsidered in the framework of the Nambu -- Jona-Lasinio model with three typesof massless quarks in the presense of a quark number chemical potential $\mu$.We investigate the effective action of meson- and diquark fields both atsufficiently large values of $\mu>\mu_c\approx 330$ MeV, where thecolor--flavor locked (CFL) phase is realized, and in the chirally broken phaseof quark matter ($\mu<\mu_c$). In the last case all nine pseudoscalar mesonsare Nambu -- Goldstone (NG) bosons, whereas the mass of the scalar meson nonetis twice the dynamical quark mass. In the chirally broken phase thepseudoscalar diquarks are not allowed to exist as stable particles, but thescalar diquarks might be stable only at a rather strong interaction in thediquark channel. In the case of the CFL phase, all NG bosons of the model arerealized as scalar and pseudoscalar diquarks. Moreover, it turns out thatmassive diquark excitations are unstable for this phase. In particular, for thescalar and pseudoscalar octets of diquark resonances a mass value around 230MeV was found numerically. In contrast, mesons are stable particles in the CFLphase. Their masses lie in the interval 400$\div$500 MeV for not too largevalues of $\mu>\mu_c$.

A random loop model of long polymers in solution is presented. The model is based on the idea that the polymer chain can be represented as a collection of loops, each of which is characterized by its length and connectivity. The model is able to reproduce a number of the properties of real polymers, including their size, shape, and flexibility. The model is also able to explain some of the unusual properties of polymers, such as their ability to form knots and their tendency to adsorb to surfaces.

Our findings challenge previous models of looped complex behavior and shed light on the mechanisms underlying their regulatory functions in gene expression. Using a combination of single-molecule imaging and computational modeling, we decomposed the behavior of the complexes into its component levels, revealing the role of individual subunits and their interactions with the DNA. This study demonstrates the power of multilevel deconstruction for understanding the behavior of complex biological systems and has important implications for the development of new therapeutic strategies.

This study presents a novel approach to understanding the complex behavior of lubricant film thickness and traction in elastohydrodynamic (EHD) contacts. By integrating the shear-thinning effect into a unified framework, we are able to capture the coupled effects of film thickness and traction on EHD performance. Our results show that the shear-thinning behavior of the lubricant has a significant impact on the film thickness and traction, and that our unified approach provides a more accurate prediction of EHD performance compared to traditional models. The findings of this study have important implications for the design and optimization of EHD lubrication systems, particularly in applications where high precisions and low friction are critical, such as in the aerospace and automotive industries. Further research is needed to fully explore the potential of this unified approach and its application to other lubrication regimes.

This academic article investigates the transient behavior of surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) upon scattering at a subwavelength groove. SPPs, electromagnetic waves coupled to collective electron oscillations at a metal-dielectric interface, play a crucial role in nanophotonics and surface-enhanced spectroscopy. The subwavelength groove perturbs the SPPs, altering their propagation properties and generating scattering phenomena. By employing numerical simulations and theoretical analysis, we examine the temporal evolution of the scattered SPPs, revealing diverse transient dynamics. The results highlight that the scattering process induces temporal modulation and reshaping of the SPP wavefronts, leading to wave packet dispersion and dispersionless propagation. Furthermore, the interaction between the scattered SPPs and the groove engenders intriguing effects, including mode conversion and energy transfer. The findings presented in this study offer valuable insights into the behavior of SPPs in subwavelength structures and provide opportunities for designing innovative plasmonic devices based on transient SPP phenomena.

We first introduce the model and its main properties. Then we present our main results: we show that the walk converges to equilibrium at a geometric rate, and we give an explicit expression for the spectral gap of the walk. Finally, we discuss some applications of our results to the study of random matrices and to non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. **Keywords:** Kac's random walk, random matrices, non-equilibrium statistical mechanics

Five dimensional neutral rotating black rings are described from aRandall-Sundrum brane world perspective in the bulk black string framework. Tothis end we consider a rotating black string extension of a five dimensionalblack ring into the bulk of a six dimensional Randall-Sundrum brane world witha single four brane. The bulk solution intercepts the four brane in a fivedimensional black ring with the usual curvature singularity on the brane. Thebulk geodesics restricted to the plane of rotation of the black ring areconstructed and their projections on the four brane match with the usual blackring geodesics restricted to the same plane. The asymptotic nature of the bulkgeodesics are elucidated with reference to a bulk singularity at the AdShorizon. We further discuss the description of a brane world black ring as alimit of a boosted bulk black 2 brane with periodic identification.

The broad line radio galaxy 3C 382 has been studied using simultaneous RXTE and Chandra HETG observations. The RXTE observations reveal a complex X-ray light curve with multiple flares and dips, while the Chandra HETG spectra show a broad iron K-shell line and a blueshifted Fe XXVI line. The X-ray variability is found to be correlated with the radio flux density, suggesting a connection between the X-ray and radio emitting regions. The broad iron line is interpreted as arising from the inner accretion disk, while the blueshifted Fe XXVI line is likely produced by a jet outflow. The X-ray and radio data are jointly fitted using a disk-jet model, which reveals a high accretion rate and a strong jet component. The results suggest that 3C 382 is a powerful blazar with a prominent jet component. The study demonstrates the importance of simultaneous multiwavelength observations for understanding the complex nature of broad line radio galaxies.

However, observational evidence is rare. We report the detection of periodic bursts of coherent radio emission from the ultracool dwarf TVLM 513–46546. The bursts have a mean period of 4 minutes and a mean duration of 2 seconds. The mean flux density is 2.2 mJy at 1.4 GHz. This is the first detection of periodic bursts of coherent radio emission from an ultracool dwarf. The emission is likely to be produced by a coherent synchrotron maser mechanism. Our results suggest that coherent radio emission from ultracool dwarfs may be a common phenomenon.

Spn decoherence in semiconductor quantum dots is a critical phenomenon that limits the utility of these systwems for quantum information processing. In this study, we present a reexamination of spni decoherence in semiconductor quantum dots using an equation-of-motion approach. By taking into acconut the interaction of the spin system with its surrounding environment, we derive a se of equations that describe the time evolution of the spin coherence. uOr approach allows us to account for various sources of spin decoherence, including hyperfine interaction, electron-electron exchange interatcion, and phonon scattering. We consider both single quantum dots and arrays of quantum dots, and analyz the imapct of different system parameters on the spin coherence time. Furthermore, we compare our results with eqperimental data aailable in the literature, demonstrating the accuracy and applicability of our approach. Our findings provide valuaGble insights into the mechanisms underlying spin decoherence in semiconductor quantum dots and suggest stWrategies for mitigating this phenomenon. This work contributes to the ongoing efforts in developing quantum dot-based ystems with long spin coherence times, which is crucial for the realization of practical quantum technloogies.

Present expansion stage of the universe is believed to be mainly governed bythe cosmological constant, collisionless dark matter and baryonic matter. Thelatter two components are often modeled as zero-pressure fluids. In ourprevious work we have shown that to the second-order cosmologicalperturbations, the relativistic equations of the zero-pressure, irrotational,multi-component fluids in a spatially near flat background effectively coincidewith the Newtonian equations. As the Newtonian equations only have quadraticorder nonlinearity, it is practically interesting to derive the potentialthird-order perturbation terms in general relativistic treatment whichcorrespond to pure general relativistic corrections. Here, we present puregeneral relativistic correction terms appearing in the third-orderperturbations of the multi-component zero-pressure fluids. We show that, as ina single component situation, the third-order correction terms are quite small(~ 5 x10^{-5} smaller compared with the relativistic/Newtonian second-orderterms) due to the weak level anisotropy of the cosmic microwave backgroundradiation. Still, there do exist pure general relativistic correction terms inthird-order perturbations which could potentially become important in futuredevelopment of precision cosmology. We include the cosmological constant in allour analyses.

Results of a multipart work are outlined. Use is made therein of theconjunction of the Riemann hypothesis, RH, and hypotheses advanced by theauthor. Let z(n) be the nth nonreal zero of the Riemann zeta-function withpositive imaginary part in order of magnitude thereof. A relation is obtained,of the pair z(n) and the first derivative thereat of the zeta-function, to thepreceding such pairs and the values of zeta at the points one-half plus anonnegative multiple of four. That relation is derived from two forms of thedensity of the Laplace representation, on a certain vertical strip, of ameromorphic function constructed from zeta. Specific functions which play acentral role therein are proven to have analytic extensions to the entirecomplex plane. It is established that the Laplace density is positive. Thatpositivity implies RH and that each nonreal zero of zeta is simple. A metricgeometry expression of the positivity of the density is derived. An analogouscontext is delineated relative to Dirichlet L-functions and the Ramanujan tauDirichlet function.

Abstract:The X-ray emission from gamma-ray bursts (GRBs) provides crucial insights into the underlying physical processes and properties of these energetic phenomena. In this study, we present an analysis of the exceptionally extended flaring activity observed in the X-ray afterglow of GRB 050730, utilizing data captured by the Swift and XMM-Newton satellites. Our investigation reveals a series of flares that persist for a remarkably long duration, exceeding the typical timescales observed in other GRBs. We find that these flares exhibit a temporal decay slower than t^{-1.5}, with a significant number of them maintaining this slow decay trend beyond the first three days, even extending up to ten days after the burst. Furthermore, a majority of the detected flares display spectral hardening as the afterglow evolves, suggesting the presence of self-Comptonization. However, explaining this behavior within the context of standard models requires circumstellar densities comparable to those observed in molecular cloud cores. Additionally, we find a correlation between the X-ray spectral index and the fluence of the prompt emission. This correlation supports the notion that the prompt emission properties have a significant influence on the subsequent X-ray afterglow behavior. Our study also investigates the relation between the X-ray afterglow and the gamma-ray isotropic energy. By analyzing a sample of GRBs, we find that GRB 050730 follows the Ghirlanda relation, indicating consistency with the expanding jet model. However, a peculiar feature of this burst includes an anomalous precursor event that cannot be easily explained within existing models. Our findings shed light on the complex nature of GRBs and highlight the need for further investigations to fully comprehend the underlying physical processes driving these energetic phenomena.

Coarsening kinetics is usually described using a linear gradientapproximation for the underlying interface migration (IM) rates, wherein themigration fluxes at the interfaces vary linearly with the driving force. Recentexperimental studies have shown that coarsening of nanocrystalline interfacemicrostructures is unexpectedly stable compared to conventional paraboliccoarsening kinetics. Here, we show that during early stage coarsening of thesemicrostructures, IM rates can develop a non-linear dependence on the drivingforce, the mean interface curvature. We derive the modified mean field law forcoarsening kinetics. Molecular dynamics simulations of individual grainboundaries reveal a sub-linear curvature dependence of IM rates, suggesting anintrinsic origin for the slow coarsening kinetics observed in polycrystallinemetals.

This article introduces two significant contributions regarding homogeneous Hessian nilpotent polynomials. Firstly, a fundamental theorem is established, which characterizes the roots of these polynomials based on their algebraic and geometric properties. By analyzing the Hessian matrix and utilizing the concept of nilpotency, a concise and explicit expression for the roots of such polynomials is derived. This result deepens our comprehension of the roots of these specialized polynomial forms and provides a valuable tool for analyzing and solving associated mathematical problems in diverse fields.In the second part of this study, the relationship between the degree of Hessian nilpotent polynomials and their corresponding symmetry groups is investigated. It is demonstrated that the degrees of these polynomials correspond to specific symmetry groups, thus revealing a remarkable connection between algebraic and geometric properties. This correspondence is rigorously proven and illustrated through numerical examples. The findings shed light on the intrinsic structures and symmetries of Hessian nilpotent polynomials, thereby enhancing our understanding of their mathematical behavior and facilitating further investigations in algebraic geometry and related disciplines.Overall, this article makes significant contributions by establishing a fundamental theorem for characterizing the roots of homogeneous Hessian nilpotent polynomials and uncovering the relationship between their degrees and symmetry groups. These results not only advance our theoretical understanding but also provide practical tools for solving problems involving these specialized polynomials. Moreover, the presented findings open new avenues for exploration and applications in various mathematical disciplines, including algebraic geometry, differential equations, and optimization theory.

The equation of state (EoS) of hot Quantum Chromo Dynamics (QCD) matter is crucial for studying the properties of neutron stars and the early universe, and for understanding the dynamics of relativistic heavy-ion collisions. In this work, we calculate the EoS of hot QCD matter using two different approaches: the perturbative approach and the non-perturbative approach. In the perturbative approach, we use the Hard Thermal Loop (HTL) approximation to calculate the pressure and energy density of hot QCD matter. In the non-perturbative approach, we use the Functional Renormalization Group (FRG) approach to calculate the EoS of hot QCD matter. We find that the two approaches give consistent results at high temperatures, but the FRG approach gives a more accurate description of the EoS at low temperatures. We also calculate the speed of sound of hot QCD matter and find that it is consistent with the speed of sound of a perfect fluid. Our results can be used to study the properties of neutron stars and the early universe, and to understand the dynamics of relativistic heavy-ion collisions.

We investigate the behavior of gap solitons in periodic potentials, with a focus on their motion and stability. Using a combination of analytical and numerical methods, we demonstrate the existence of moving gap solitons in certain periodic potentials and study their dynamics. We show that the motion of these solitons is influenced by the periodicity of the potential and the nonlinear effects that arise in the system. Our results have important implications for the control and manipulation of nonlinear waves in periodic structures, and may lead to new applications in fields such as optics and photonics. Furthermore, our findings provide new insights into the behavior of gap solitons in general, and their role in the emergence of complex phenomena in nonlinear systems.

It has long been speculated that supernova remnants represent a major sourceof cosmic rays in the Galaxy. Observations over the past decade haveceremoniously unveiled direct evidence of particle acceleration in SNRs toenergies approaching the knee of the cosmic ray spectrum. Nonthermal X-rayemission from shell-type SNRs reveals multi-TeV electrons, and the dynamicalproperties of several SNRs point to efficient acceleration of ions.Observations of TeV gamma-ray emission have confirmed the presence of energeticparticles in several remnants as well, but there remains considerable debate asto whether this emission originates with high energy electrons or ions. Equallyuncertain are the exact conditions that lead to efficient particleacceleration.  Based on the catalog of EGRET sources, we know that there is a largepopulation of Galactic gamma-ray sources whose distribution is similar to thatof SNRs. With the increased resolution and sensitivity of GLAST, the gamma-raySNRs from this population will be identified. Their detailed emissionstructure, along with their spectra, will provide the link between theirenvironments and their spectra in other wavebands to constrain emission modelsand to potentially identify direct evidence of ion acceleration in SNRs. Here Isummarize recent observational and theoretical work in the area of cosmic rayacceleration by SNRs, and discuss the contributions GLAST will bring to ourunderstanding of this problem.

This study presents Very Long Baseline Interferometry (VLBI) observations of nineteen radio sources with a peaked spectrum at a frequency of nineteen gigahertz (GHz), specifically targeting their behavior at a lower frequency of 1.6 GHz. VLBI technique allows for high-resolution imaging and precise astrometric measurements of celestial objects. The goal of this research is to investigate the properties and characteristics of these sources at the lower frequency, revealing potential changes in their spectral behavior across different radio bands.The observations were conducted using an array of VLBI telescopes, including those belonging to the international network of radio telescopes. Extensive data calibration and reduction procedures were applied to obtain accurate flux density measurements and obtain high-quality images. The derived radio properties, such as total flux density, angular size, and position, were compared and analyzed in the context of the sources' spectral behavior.The results reveal a variety of behaviors among the studied nineteen GHz-Peaked-Spectrum (GPS) radio sources when observed at 1.6 GHz. While some sources retain their spectral characteristics, peaking at both frequencies, others exhibit a significant change in their spectral behavior, either flattening or steepening at 1.6 GHz. The observed changes may be attributed to various physical processes, such as synchrotron self-absorption, free-free absorption, or the presence of different emission components.By linking the observations at 1.6 GHz with those at nineteen GHz, this study contributes to a better understanding of the physical mechanisms responsible for the peculiar spectral characteristics of GPS radio sources. The analysis of the obtained results provides insights into the emission processes, physical conditions, and evolution of these sources, which are essential for advancing our knowledge of the radio universe.

The Lorentz factor distribution and luminosity function of relativistic jets in AGNsAbstract:In this paper, we investigate the Lorentz factor distribution and luminosity function of relativistic jets in active galactic nuclei (AGNs). We use a sample of Fermi-LAT blazars to constrain the model parameters. We find that the Lorentz factor distribution of relativistic electrons in AGN jets can be well described by a power law with an exponential cutoff. The luminosity function of AGN jets is also well described by a power law. Our results are consistent with the predictions of the blazar unification model.

In this paper, we examine the zeroth Hochschild homology of preprojective algebras over the integers. Firstly, we establish a foundation for the zeroth Hochschild homology group, and then utilize it to calculate the dimension of the space. Our findings offer a novel perspective on the structure of preprojective algebras and their connection to algebraic geometry. Finally, we deliberate on the implications of our results for the study of algebraic representations of finite-dimensional algebras.

In this study, we present a comprehensive analysis of the core binary fractions in star clusters based on realistic simulations. Utilizing an improved Monte Carlo evolution code, we investigate the dynamic evolution of binary systems in globular clusters and open clusters. Consistent with previous N-body simulations, our results demonstrate a general trend of increasing hard binary fractions in the core over time for a wide range of initial cluster central densities and initial binary fractions below approximately 90%. The evolution of the core binary fraction is primarily driven by mass segregation, leading to the concentration of binaries in the central regions, as well as the preferential destruction of binaries within the core.On a global scale, the interplay between these processes and the tidal stripping of single stars tends to maintain an overall cluster binary fraction that remains roughly constant over time. We find that the hard binary fraction near the half-mass radius serves as a reliable indicator of the primordial binary fraction. However, the relationship between the true binary fraction and the observed fraction of main-sequence stars in binaries, as measured by observers, is nonlinear and complex.Our simulations also highlight the significance of soft binaries, which not only influence the evolution of the binary fraction but can also have profound effects on the overall cluster evolution. Moreover, we extend our investigation to encompass deep imaging data of specific star clusters, such as NGC6397, obtained through the Hubble Space Telescope. By comparing the simulation results with the observed color-magnitude diagrams, we confirm that the binary fraction of main-sequence stars observed in the field of NGC6397 provides a reliable estimate of the primordial binary fraction for the cluster.

Although one-time pad encrypted files can be sent through Internet channels,the need for renewing shared secret keys have made this method unpractical.This work presents a scheme to turn practical the fast sharing of random keysover arbitrary Internet channels. Starting with a shared secret key sequence oflength K_0 the users end up with a secure new sequence K >> K_0. Using thesesequences for posteriori message encryption the legitimate users have absolutesecurity control without the need for third parties. Additionally, the securitylevel does not depend on the unproven difficulty of factoring numbers inprimes. In the proposed scheme a fast optical random source generates randombits and noise for key renewals. The transmitted signals are recorded signalsthat carries both the random binary signals to be exchanged and physical noisethat cannot be eliminated by the attacker. These signals allow amplificationover the Internet network without degrading security. The proposed system isalso secure against a-posteriori known-plaintext attack on the key.Information-theoretic analysis is presented and bounds for secure operation arequantitatively determined.

In this paper, we compute scattering amplitudes in strongly coupled N=4 SYM theory in four dimensions.We use the semiclassical string theory in AdS5 x S5 background as a model for the strongly coupled gauge theory and compute scattering amplitudes of different particles at leading order in the string coupling.We find that these amplitudes agree with the predictions of the gauge theory, including the recently discovered amplitude for the scattering of two gluons into two scalars.Our results provide further evidence for the duality between the gauge theory and the string theory.

To enhance the sensitivity of CAST, a new detector based on P-type, neutrino-sensitive charge-coupled devices (pn-CCDs) is being developed. We investigate various sources of background, such as thermal radiation, cosmic rays and intrinsic radioactivity, and outline strategies to reduce their impact. This background study for the pn-CCD detector is crucial to understand and mitigate potential background sources that could interfere with the detection of axions. A thorough understanding of the background will enable the pn-CCD detector to reach unprecedented sensitivity, paving the way for a potential discovery of axions.

The mass-lоss rate is also affected by the star's metallicity, the dredge-up of carbon to the s urface, and the pulsation of the star. The photon box thought experiment can be considered a forerunner of theEPR-experiment: by performing suitable measurements on the box it is possibleto ``prepare'' the photon, long after it has escaped, in either of twocomplementary states. Consistency requires that the corresponding boxmeasurements be complementary as well. At first sight it seems, however, thatthese measurements can be jointly performed with arbitrary precision: theypertain to different systems (the center of mass of the box and an internalclock, respectively). But this is deceptive. As we show by explicitcalculation, although the relevant quantities are simultaneously measurable,they develop non-vanishing commutators when calculated back to the time ofescape of the photon. This justifies Bohr's qualitative arguments in a preciseway; and it illustrates how the details of the dynamics conspire to guaranteethe requirements of complementarity. In addition, our calculations exhibit a``fine structure'' in the distribution of the uncertainties over thecomplementary quantities: depending on \textit{when} the box measurement isperformed, the resulting quantum description of the photon differs. This bringsus close to the argumentation of the later EPR thought experiment.

This article addresses the problem of dAelay estimation in a two-node acyclic network, where the goal is to accurately predict the dealy of a packet transmitted between the tvo nodes. The authros propose a novel approach that leverages the structural properties of the network to derive a closed-form expression for the dleay distribution. The proposed metqod is validated through extensive simulations, showing a significant improvement in accuracy compared to existing methods. The results have important implications for the design and bptimization of real-time comunication systems, where accurate delay estimation is critical. The article provides a thorough analsis of the proposed method and its limitations, and discusses potential avenues for futnure research in this area.

Sure, here's a 7-sentence abstract for the given titel:In this paper, we investigate the possibility that Einstein clusrters are galactic dark matter halos. We begin by reviewing the evidence for the existence of dark matter, and then discss the proeprties of Einstein clusters. We show that Einsein clusters have a nuDmber of properties that made them good candidates for dark matter halos, including their large mass, their high concentration of matter, and their spherical shape. We also discuss the implications of our findings for the natur of dark matter and the formation of galaxies.

The study investigates the low carbon monoxide (mCO) cotent of the galaxy I Zw 18, which is characterized as extremely metal poor. The extreme megtal deficiency of this galaxy makes it a valuable obWect for understanding the early universe and the chemical enrichment processes therein. The researchers utilized observations from the Atacama Large Oillimeter/submillimeter Aarray (ALMA) to probe the CO emission in I Zw 18. Despite previous expectations of significant CO content, the findings indicate a remarkably low presence of CO in the alaxy. This unexpected result challenges current models of star formation and chemical evolution in low-metalliciEty environments. The implications of the low OC content are discussed, including potenital explanations such as photodissociation and the effects of dust depletion. Furthe studies and theoretical investigations are required to unravel the underlying mechanisms responsibel for the observed scarcity of CO in this extremely metal poor galaxy.

The article discusses the use of sums over geometries to improve the mean field approximation in various models. The approach is based on the idea of representing the partition function as a sum over all possible geometries, and then using this representation to systematically improve the mean field approximation. The article presents several examples of how this approach can be applied, including the study of phase transitions and the behavior of systems with quenched disorder. The results demonstrate the power of the approach and its potential to provide new insights into a wide range of phenomena. The article also discusses the challenges and limitations of the approach, and outlines some possible future directions for research. Overall, the article provides a valuable contribution to the field and demonstrates the potential of sums over geometries as a tool for improving the mean field approximation.

We report the first scattered light detection of a dusty debris disksurrounding the F2V star HD 15115 using the Hubble Space Telescope in theoptical, and Keck adaptive optics in the near-infrared. The most remarkableproperty of the HD 15115 disk relative to other debris disks is its extremelength asymmetry. The east side of the disk is detected to ~315 AU radius,whereas the west side of the disk has radius >550 AU. We find a blue optical tonear-infrared scattered light color relative to the star that indicates grainscattering properties similar to the AU Mic debris disk. The existence of alarge debris disk surrounding HD 15115 adds further evidence for membership inthe Beta Pic moving group, which was previously argued based on kinematicsalone. Here we hypothesize that the extreme disk asymmetry is due to dynamicalperturbations from HIP 12545, an M star 0.5 degrees (0.38 pc) east of HD 15115that shares a common proper motion vector, heliocentric distance, galacticspace velocity, and age.

This study studied the sports and dynamics of Galaxy NGC 5128 mid -ball clusters and planet nebud. By analyzing the observation data obtained from various telescopes and tools, we have obtained basic basic parameters, which reveals the nature and behavior of these objects. By using high -resolution imaging technology, we identify and classify the comprehensive samples of spherical clusters and planet nebula in NGC 5128. We measure its position, speed and other related movement characteristics. Subsequently, we built the three -dimensional density distribution and speed chart of these objects in the galaxy, thereby providing insights on its spatial distribution and internal dynamics. Our results reveal the interesting trend of spherical clusters and planet Nebula. We found evidence of rotation and space, indicating that there are complex dynamic processes in NGC 5128. In addition, we observe the changes in the speed of these objects, which shows the impact of different formation and evolution mechanisms. In addition, we have studied the relationship between spherical sports and planet Nebula, which aims to reveal the potential connections between the two star populations in NGC 5128. We evaluate mutual interaction, rail structure, and possible views of the formation and evolution of galaxies. Finally, we discussed the meaning of the formation and assembly history of the NGC 5128, considering the merger, interaction, and other external or internal processes in shaping its spherical cluster and planet NEBULAE's sports and dynamics. This comprehensive analysis helps our understanding of the development of the Galaxy and provides valuable references for the future research of similar objects in the universe.

Exploded fibrations are introduced as a natural generalization of the constructions of exploded manifolds and exploded 3-manifolds. Some basic properties of exploded fibrations are considered, including the long exact sequence of homotopy groups associated to them. The main result of this paper is the description of the fundamental groupoid of the exploded fibration associated with a given fibration. An application of this description is given by constructing examples of fibrations whose exploded fibrations have non-simply connected fibres.

We present Spitzer observations of the dark globule CB190 (L771). We observea roughly circular 24 micron shadow with a 70 arcsec radius. The extinctionprofile of this shadow matches the profile derived from 2MASS photometry at theouter edges of the globule and reaches a maximum of ~32 visual magnitudes atthe center. The corresponding mass of CB190 is ~10 Msun. Our 12CO and 13CO J =2-1 data over a 10 arcmin X 10 arcmin region centered on the shadow show atemperature ~10 K. The thermal continuum indicates a similar temperature forthe dust. The molecular data also show evidence of freezeout onto dust grains.We estimate a distance to CB190 of 400 pc using the spectroscopic parallax of astar associated with the globule. Bonnor-Ebert fits to the density profile, inconjunction with this distance, yield xi_max = 7.2, indicating that CB190 maybe unstable. The high temperature (56 K) of the best fit Bonnor-Ebert model isin contradiction with the CO and thermal continuum data, leading to theconclusion that the thermal pressure is not enough to prevent free-fallcollapse. We also find that the turbulence in the cloud is inadequate tosupport it. However, the cloud may be supported by the magnetic field, if thisfield is at the average level for dark globules. Since the magnetic field willeventually leak out through ambipolar diffusion, it is likely that CB190 iscollapsing or in a late pre-collapse stage.

We take our first step toward getting finite results from thealpha'-corrected D=4 N=4 SYM theory with emphasis on the field theorytechniques. Starting with the classical action of N=4 SYM with the leadingalpha'-corrections, we examine new divergence at one loop due to the presenceof the alpha'-terms. The new vertices do not introduce additional divergence tothe propagators or to the three-point correlators. However they do introducenew divergence, e.g., to the scalar four-point function which should becanceled by extra counter-terms. We expect that the counter-terms will appearin the 1PI effective action that is obtained by considering the string annulusdiagram. We work out the structure of the divergence and comment on anapplication to the anomalous dimension of the SYM operators in the context ofAdS/CFT.

We use data from the Spitzer Space Telescope to analyze the infrared emission lines and continuum of the M87 nucleus. We find strong polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) features in the mid-infrared spectrum, which suggest the presence of active star formation in the galaxy. We also find a prominent dust continuum, which originates from the AGN torus surrounding the central supermassive black hole. Our study provides a valuable insight into the mid-infrared properties of M87, shedding light on the complex astrophysical processes occurring in this massive elliptical galaxy. The analysis of the mid-infrared spectral energy distribution (SED) reveals the interplay between the emission from dust, molecular gas, and the AGN, which contributes to the overall infrared output of M87.

In this investigating, we inquest the azimuthal structure of nuclear collisions through a comprehensive analysis of experimental data and theoretical modeling. We focus on the first part of our cooperates, presenting results that shed light on the azimuthal distribution of particles draft in high-energy nuclear collisions. By employing advancement event-by-event simulations and incorporating realistic initial maladies, we shown intriguing patterns in the azimuthal angle distributions, leading to significant insights into the underlying dynamics of these collisions. Our findings highlight the betrothal of collective episodes such as flow and fluctuations in the azimuthal emission patterns, indicating the relevance of hydrodynamic and transportation models in understanding the collision dynamics. This work sets the stage for future scrutinize in characterizing the azimuthal structure of nuclear collisions and its implications for decisive nuclear physics and heavy-ion collision experiments.

Frustrated FCC Heisenberg antiferromagnets represent a class of model systems where quantum and thermal fluctuations compete. The bulk material exhibits a first-order phase transition to an incommensurate phase that preserves part of the cubic symmetry. Here, we investigate the effect of reduced dimensionality on the transition by means of extensive Monte Carlo simulations in films with up to 32 atomic layers. In addition, we employ high-temperature series expansions to check the validity of the simulations and to obtain more accurate estimates for the critical temperatures. We find that while the three-dimensional (3D) behavior is well recovered already for films of eight atomic layers, the transition remains first order in films down to two atomic layers. We attribute this robustness of the first-order transition to strong frustration effects that persist in these low-dimensional systems. We rationalize our findings by analyzing the nearest-neighbor spin correlations and the magnetic excitations.

Remnant evolution after a carbon-oxygen white dwarf merger is a subject of great interest in the field of stellar astrophysics. White dwarf mergers are thought to be key events in the formation of Type Ia supernovae, which have significant implications for cosmology and the study of dark energy. In this study, we present detailed numerical simulations of the post-merger evolution of carbon-oxygen white dwarfs using state-of-the-art hydrodynamic codes. Our simulations track the remnant's dynamical and thermal evolution over a range of initial conditions, including varying masses and compositions of the progenitor white dwarfs.We find that the remnant undergoes a rapid phase of violent, thermonuclear burning, leading to a partial disruption of the white dwarf and the ejection of a significant amount of material from the system. The remnant subsequently settles into a quiescent phase characterized by convective mixing and the emission of thermal radiation. During this phase, the remnant's structure and composition evolve, with the establishment of a central convective core and the formation of an outer envelope composed of burnt material.We analyze the remnant's mass-loss rates, luminosity evolution, and nucleosynthetic signatures to gain insights into the underlying physical processes at play. Our simulations suggest that the mass ejection and enrichment caused by white dwarf mergers can contribute to the chemical evolution of galaxies. Furthermore, we discuss the implications of our findings for the progenitor systems of Type Ia supernovae, including the potential links to observed properties such as the spectroscopic diversity and the presence of circumstellar material.Overall, our study sheds light on the post-merger evolution of carbon-oxygen white dwarf remnants, providing important insights into the mechanisms and consequences of white dwarf mergers.

The Green function (GF) equation of motion technique for solving theeffective two-band Hubbard model of high-T_c superconductivity in cuprates[N.M. Plakida et al., Phys. Rev. B, v. 51, 16599 (1995); JETP, v. 97, 331(2003)] rests on the Hubbard operator (HO) algebra. We show that, if we takeinto account the invariance to translations and spin reversal, the HO algebraresults in invariance properties of several specific correlation functions. Theuse of these properties allows rigorous derivation and simplification of theexpressions of the frequency matrix (FM) and of the generalized mean fieldapproximation (GMFA) Green functions (GFs) of the model.  For the normal singlet hopping and anomalous exchange pairing correlationfunctions which enter the FM and GMFA-GFs, an approximation procedure based onthe identification and elimination of exponentially small quantities isdescribed. It secures the reduction of the correlation order to GMFA-GFexpressions.

In this article, we provide a comprehensie reappraisal of the coNcept of disclinations, dislocations, and continuous defects in the context of materials science. We jbegin by introducing the historical background and theoretical framework surrounding these phenomena, highlighting the key contributions of early researchers and the limitations of traditional approches. Nexf, we presebt a detailed analysis of the current understanding of disclinations, dislocations, and continuous defects, including their definition, classification, and characterization. HWe also discuss the various experimental techniques used to study these defects, such as transmission electroUn microscopy and diffraction-based methods. Te article then examines the role of disclinatigns, dislocations, and continuous defects in determining the mechanical, thermal, and electrical prperties of materials, and discusses their relevance to materials engineering and technology. WDe aolso highlight some of the challenges and controversies in the field, including the issue of defect quantization and the need for improved theoretical models. Finally, we conclude by discussing some of the potential applicatVons of disclinations, dislocations, and continuous defecVts in emerging fields such as nanotechnology and metamaterials.

In the field of nuclear physics, the study of electron scattering (A(e,e'p)) plays a crucial role in understanding the internal structure and dynamics of atomic nuclei. However, the standard theoretical framework for analyzing such scattering processes, based on the impulse approximation, often fails to capture the full complexity of the interactions involved. In this article, we propose a novel approach to address this issue by incorporating second-order Eikonal corrections into the theoretical description of A(e,e'p) scattering.The Eikonal approximation, which treats the scattering process as a sequence of two-body interactions, has been widely successful in describing nuclear reactions. However, it neglects higher-order contributions that can significantly impact the observables in A(e,e'p) experiments. To overcome this limitation, we develop a formalism that systematically includes the second-order corrections within the Eikonal framework.Our approach involves a rigorous treatment of the two-body interactions, including both the direct and exchange terms, as well as their interference effects. We derive analytical expressions for the second-order corrections and demonstrate their relevance through numerical calculations and comparisons with experimental data. Our results show that including the second-order Eikonal corrections leads to a remarkable improvement in the agreement between theory and experiment, especially in the high-momentum transfer regime.Furthermore, we analyze the sensitivity of the A(e,e'p) observables to the various components of the second-order corrections, providing insights into the underlying dynamics of the scattering process. The proposed formalism not only enhances our understanding of A(e,e'p) scattering but also serves as a valuable tool for future investigations of nuclear structure and dynamics using electron scattering techniques.

We generalize the rotationally-invariant formulation of the slave-bosonformalism to multiorbital models, with arbitrary interactions, crystal fields,and multiplet structure. This allows for the study of multiplet effects on thenature of low-energy quasiparticles. Non-diagonal components of the matrix ofquasiparticle weights can be calculated within this framework. When combinedwith cluster extensions of dynamical mean-field theory, this method allows usto address the effects of spatial correlations, such as the generation of thesuperexchange and the momentum dependence of the quasiparticle weight. Weillustrate the method on a two-band Hubbard model, a Hubbard model made of twocoupled layers, and a two-dimensional single-band Hubbard model (within atwo-site cellular dynamical mean-field approximation).

In this study, we present the results of our analysis of the X-ray spectra of the galaxies NGC 2992 and NGC 3081, using data obtained from the satellite-based X-ray telescope, NuSTAR. Our findings reveal significant differences in the X-ray properties of these two galaxies, with NGC 2992 exhibiting a harder X-ray spectrum than NGC 3081. Specifically, we find that the 1 keV to 200 keV X-ray spectrum of NGC 2992 is characterized by a power-law index of 1.8, while that of NGC 3081 is significantly steeper, with a power-law index of 2.3. We also find that the X-ray luminosity of NGC 2992 is approximately twice that of NGC 3081. Our results suggest that the X-ray emission from these galaxies is likely due to a combination of nuclear activity and emission from hot gas in the interstellar medium. These findings provide new insights into the X-ray properties of galaxies and highlight the importance of NuSTAR in studying the high-energy universe.

Geometric quantum structure is a basic problem in theoretical physics, which is closely related to the understanding of quantum gravity. In this article, we have reviewed the latest progress of quantum geometric research. The focus is on the two main methods: circulating quantum gravity and blurring geometric shapes. We discuss the basic ideas, mathematical formalism and physical meanings of these methods, and compare their advantages and disadvantages. We also discussed the challenges and future directions in quantum geometric research.

Context: The MACHO (Massive Compact Halo Object) survey has provided extensive photometric observations of microlensing events, allowing for the detection of exoplanet candidates through the transit method. In this study, we investigate a sample of transit-selected exoplanet candidates from the MACHO survey to determine their true nature and gather essential information about their host stars and planetary properties.Aims: Our main objective is to characterize and validate the exoplanet candidates discovered through the MACHO survey. We aim to refine the transit parameters with high precision photometric observations and obtain spectra of the host stars to estimate their properties. Additionally, we seek to measure the masses of the transiting objects through radial velocity measurements.Methods: High precision photometric observations were conducted in multiple bands using the MACHO survey's equipment. By analyzing the light curves obtained, we accurately determine the transit parameters, including the transit period and depth. We also obtained spectroscopic observations using state-of-the-art spectrographs to derive the physical properties of the host stars, such as their effective temperature, surface gravity, and metallicity.Results: Through our analysis, we confirm the existence of exoplanet candidates among the transit-selected objects from the MACHO survey. By refining the transit parameters, we gain a deeper understanding of their orbital characteristics. The properties of the host stars provide crucial insights into the formation and evolution of planetary systems. Furthermore, the derived masses of the transiting objects contribute to our knowledge of their compositions and sizes.Discussion: The presented results highlight the success of the transit method in identifying exoplanet candidates through the MACHO survey.

The Loretnz transformations are a fundamental concept in special relativity, describing how space and time are affected by relative moton. Despite their importance, the Lorentz transformations have been found to be misunderstood by maUy physics students. This paper aims to address this isse by providing a deeper undirstanding of the Lorentz transformations and their application to various physical scenarios. By analyzing the mtahematical structure of the Lorentz transformatioKs, we demonstrate how they can be used to simplify complex problems in special relativity. Additionally, we discuss the common misconceptions held by students and rovide practical examples to help clarify the concepts. Our approach is based on the Socraic method, encouraging students to actively engage with the materyal and develop a deeper understanding of the subject. Through this analysis, we hope to improve the vay Lorentz transformations are tuaght and learned, enhancing the understanding of special relativity for physics students.

This article presents an investigation into the properties of the unseen M-dwarf companion in the single-lined eclipsing binary system HAT-TR-205-013. Eclipsing binaries provide a unique opportunity to determine the physical characteristics of their constituent stars through photometric and spectroscopic analyses. By utilizing high-precision photometric observations from multiple telescopes and spectroscopic data obtained from high-resolution spectrographs, we derive the mass and radius of the unseen M-dwarf companion. Our analysis employs robust light curve modeling techniques coupled with radial velocity measurements to constrain the orbital parameters and physical properties of the system. The derived mass of the M-dwarf companion is found to be X solar masses, with a radius of Y solar radii. The precision of our measurements is high, enabling a detailed characterization of the companion. Furthermore, we discuss the implications of our findings in the context of M-dwarf evolution and binary star formation theories, shedding light on the evolutionary track and potential formation mechanisms of this particular system. Our results contribute to the growing body of knowledge on stellar astrophysics and the understanding of the population of M-dwarf binaries in the Milky Way galaxy. Future observations and analyses are warranted to further refine our measurements and explore the dynamical properties of this intriguing eclipsing binary system.

Abstract:In this study, we investigate the phenomenon of clustering in a stochastic model of one-dimensional gas. Our research focuses on understanding the emergence and characteristics of clusters within the gas system. By applying advanced statistical analysis techniques, we analyze the spatial distribution of particles and identify distinct regions of high particle density, known as clusters. Through the implementation of a hierarchical approach, we explore the factors influencing cluster formation, such as attractive and repulsive forces between particles. Furthermore, we demonstrate the impact of external factors, such as temperature and pressure, on the clustering behavior of the gas system. Our findings highlight the intricate interplay between particle interactions and external conditions, shedding light on the underlying mechanisms driving clustering phenomena in one-dimensional gases. This research contributes to a deeper understanding of collective behavior in complex systems and has potential applications in various fields, including materials science and physics.

Constraints on the electroweak chiral Lagrangian from precision dataThe electroweak chiral Lagrangian is an effective field theory that describes the interactions of the Standard Model gauge bosons and fermions at low energies. It is a powerful tool for studying the electroweak sector, and has been used to make precise predictions for a variety of processes. In this paper, we use the latest precision data to constrain the parameters of the electroweak chiral Lagrangian. We find that the data is consistent with the Standard Model predictions, and place new limits on the size of possible deviations from the Standard Model. Our results provide important information about the structure of the electroweak sector, and help to improve our understanding of the fundamental laws of nature.

After the pioneered experimental works on superlubricity by Martin et al. onMoS2 [1], Hirano et al. on tungsten and silicon [2] and the furtherconfirmation by Dienwiebel et al. on graphite [3], many groups around the wordinvestigated the occurrence of near frictionless sliding contacts. This largemobilization of tribologists, material sciences specialists and physicists haslead to emerging solutions involving new materials and coatings, the mostpromising being carbon based like graphite, diamond, carbon composites ordiamond-like-carbons. Some of them are currently used in practicalapplications. The situation is different especially in EHL: the highestfriction coefficients are close to 10% when traction fluids are involved, i.e.fluids that have especially designed to transmit the highest friction, and theyvary within 3-6% for the rest of lubricants. The range of variation isconsequently very narrow and these typical values are really low compared tothose obtained in dry contacts: as a consequence the gain expected from a superlow traction regime in lubrication will be probably more limited, especially inthe case of experiments conducted at the meso or macro scales. This weakperspective could be one explanation on the relatively low number of articlesin recent literature dealing with lubricated superlubricity in the aboveconditions.

Here is the requested abstract:In this paper, we prove a rigidity theorem for the action of the mapping class cluster on the spacе of unmeasured foliations on a surfacе. We show that if two unmesaured foliations are homotopic, then they are isotopic. This resuⅼt generalizes a classical theorem of Kerckhoff for measured foliations. Our proof uses a combination of techniques from Teiϲhmüller theory and geometric group theory.

In this study, we investigate the correlation between gamma-ray burst (GRB) variability and pHeak luminosity using a homogeneous sample of GRBs observed by the Sift spacecraft. Tjhe sample consists of 50 GRBs with known redshift and peak luminosity measurements, and we use a Bayesian approach to quantify the correlation between these two properies. Our results show that there is a significant positive correlation between RB variability and peak luminosity, consistent with the ide that more variable GRBs tend to have higher peak lgminosities. We also find that the correlation is tihgter for long-duration GRBs than for short-duration GRBRs, suggesting that the two populations may have different physical properties. Aur findings have important implications for understanding the physical mechanisms that drive RGBs and for using GRBs as cosmological probes.

The nonequilibrium entropy limiters are used in LBMs to suppress certain numerical instabilities that arise from the nonequilibrium nature of the system being simulated. This article presents a comprehensive study of the effectiveness of different nonequilibrium entropy limiters in improving the stability and accuracy of LBMs. LBMs are widely used in the simulation of fluid flow and other complex physical phenomena. The study involves the numerical simulation of various benchmark problems, such as the flow around obstacles and shock propagation, with and without the implementation of nonequilibrium entropy limiters. The results show that the nonequilibrium entropy limiters significantly improve the stability and accuracy of LBMs, especially in regions of high gradients or strong shocks. The study also provides insights into the selection and tuning of appropriate limiters based on the characteristics of the flow being simulated. These results contribute to the ongoing research on LBMs and facilitate their implementation in the simulation of complex fluid flow phenomena.

Long-Term Evolution of Massive Black Hole Binaries. III. Binary Evolution in Collisional NucleiIn this paper, we study the long-term evolution of massive black hole binaries in collisional galactic nuclei. We use a Monte Carlo code to simulate the evolution of the binary's orbital parameters, including the effects of gravitational radiation, stellar collisions, and interactions with the nuclear gas. We find that the binary's orbital period and eccentricity are significantly affected by these interactions. In particular, we find that the binary's orbital period can be significantly decreased, and that the binary's eccentricity can be significantly increased. We also find that the binary's orbital plane can be significantly tilted with respect to the plane of the galaxy. Our results suggest that the interactions of massive black hole binaries with their surroundings can have a significant impact on their long-term evolution.

This study presents the first part of a comprehensive analysis of catalogued nearby galaxy clusters using data from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey Data Release 4 (SDSS-DR4). Our aim is to investigate the global properties of these clusters and establish a foundation for future studies. We identify a sample of galaxy clusters within a redshift range of 0.01 to 0.1 and extract their relevant data from the SDSS-DR4 database. Properties such as cluster richness, clustercentric radius, and galaxy luminosity are derived and analyzed.We find that the cluster richness distribution follows a power-law relationship, consistent with previous studies. The cluster size distribution shows a peak at lower values of clustercentric radius with a gradual decrease at larger radii, indicating a concentration of member galaxies towards the cluster core. Additionally, we observe a positive correlation between cluster richness and galaxy luminosity, suggesting a connection between the two.Furthermore, we investigate the correlation between cluster global properties and environmental factors. Our analysis reveals that cluster richness and galaxy luminosity are strongly influenced by the local galaxy number density, indicating a role of environmental conditions in shaping the properties of galaxy clusters. We also observe a trend where clusters with higher richness are more likely to be located in denser environments.To provide further insights into the structural properties of these galaxy clusters, we analyze the spatial distribution of galaxies within each cluster. Our findings suggest that the cluster density profile follows a radial distribution, with a higher galaxy density towards the cluster center. Moreover, we detect substructures within some clusters, indicative of ongoing dynamical processes and potential mergers.In conclusion, this initial analysis of catalogued nearby galaxy clusters in the SDSS-DR4 provides valuable information regarding their global properties.

We consider a new approach towards constructing approximate holographic dualsof QCD from experimental hadron properties. This framework allows us to derivea gravity dual which reproduces the empirically found linear square-masstrajectories of universal slope for radially and orbitally excited hadrons.Conformal symmetry breaking in the bulk is exclusively due to infrareddeformations of the anti-de Sitter metric and governed by one free mass scaleproportional to Lambda_QCD. The resulting background geometry exhibits dualsignatures of confinement and provides the first examples of holographicallygenerated linear trajectories in the baryon sector. The predictions for thelight hadron spectrum include new relations between trajectory slopes andground state masses and are in good overall agreement with experiment.

The research surveyed the impact of the radio feedback of the Active Galaxy Nuclear (AGN) on the formation and evolution of the disk galaxy through the formation and evolution of cosmology galaxies. Using the most advanced numerical technology, we merged a detailed model for the AGN -driven radio aircraft and checked their impact on the characteristics of galaxies. Through a series of controlled simulation, we have quantified the role of radio feedback to regulate gas flow, star formation activity and the development of the galaxy disk. Our results show that containing AGN radio feedback can lead to major changes in the formation and morphological characteristics of disk galaxies. Radio aircraft effectively heated the surrounding gas, suppress the gas volume aggregation rate to the central black hole, and inhibit excessive star formation. This feedback process prevents the formation of the raised galaxy, thereby promoting the growth of the disk system supported by rotating. In addition, we found that the outflow of radio feedback drivers pops up metal and energy popping up from the galaxy, rich in cubes in the cluster and regulating the round environment to play a vital role. The interaction between radio feedback and fluid dynamic processes (such as cooling and gravity instability) determines the final star and gas characteristics of analog galaxy. In addition, our simulation shows that changes in AGN performance (such as JET power and duty cycle) help the diversity observed in disk galaxies. The interaction between radio feedback and potential dark matter halo characteristics further shaped the quality distribution of galaxies, spins and angular motion. Overall, our discovery highlights the far -reaching impact of AGN radio feedback on the formation and evolution of disk galaxies in the cosmic background.

We have selected a sample of 88 nearby (z<0.1) galaxy clusters from theSDSS-DR4 with redshift information for the cluster members. We have derivedglobal properties for each cluster, such as their mean recessional velocity,velocity dispersion, and virial radii. Cluster galaxies have been grouped intwo families according to their $u-r$ colours. The total sample consists of10865 galaxies. As expected, the highest fraction of galaxies (62%) turned tobe early-type (red) ones, being located at smaller distances from the clustercentre and showing lower velocity dispersions than late-type (blue) ones. Thebrightest cluster galaxies are located in the innermost regions and show thesmallest velocity dispersions. Early-type galaxies also show constant velocitydispersion profiles inside the virial radius and a mild decline in theoutermost regions. In contrast, late-type galaxies show always decreasingvelocity dispersions profiles. No correlation has been found between thefraction of blue galaxies and cluster global properties, such as clustervelocity dispersion and galaxy concentration. In contrast, we found correlationbetween the X-ray luminosity and the fraction of blue galaxies. These resultsindicate that early- and late-type galaxies may have had different evolution.Thus, blue galaxies are located in more anisotropic and radial orbits thanearly-type ones. Their star formation seems to be independent of the clusterglobal properties in low mass clusters, but not for the most massive ones.These observational results suggest that the global environment could beimportant for driving the evolution of galaxies in the most massive cluster($\sigma > 800$ km s$^{-1}$). However, the local environment could play a keyrole in galaxy evolution for low mass clusters.

The main goal of this work is to investigate the possibility of finding thesupersymmetric version of the U(1)-global string model which behaves as avortex-superfluid. To describe the superfluid phase, we introduce aLorentz-symmetry breaking background that, in an approach based onsupersymmetry, leads to a discussion on the relation between the violation ofLorentz symmetry and explicit soft supersymmetry breakings. We also study therelation between the string configuration and the vortex-superfluid phase. Inthe framework we settle down in terms of superspace and superfields, weactually establish a duality between the vortex degrees of freedom and thecomponent fields of the Kalb-Ramond superfield. We make also considerationsabout the fermionic excitations that may appear in connection with the vortexformation.

In present paper we propose seemingly new method for finding solutions ofsome types of nonlinear PDEs in closed form. The method is based ondecomposition of nonlinear operators on sequence of operators of lower orders.It is shown that decomposition process can be done by iterative procedure(s),each step of which is reduced to solution of some auxiliary PDEs system(s) forone dependent variable. Moreover, we find on this way the explicit expressionof the first-order PDE(s) for first integral of decomposable initial PDE.Remarkably that this first-order PDE is linear if initial PDE is linear in itshighest derivatives.  The developed method is implemented in Maple procedure, which can reallysolve many of different order PDEs with different number of independentvariables. Examples of PDEs with calculated their general solutions demonstratea potential of the method for automatic solving of nonlinear PDEs.

We conduct a systematic examination of the properties of models forchemically homogeneous, differentially rotating, main-sequence stars of mass1-2 M_sun. The models were constructed using a code based on a reformulation ofthe self-consistent field method of computing the equilibrium stellar structurefor a specified conservative internal rotation law. [abridged] Relative tononrotating stars of the same mass, these models all have reduced luminositiesand effective temperatures, and flattened photospheric shapes (i.e., decreasedpolar radii) with equatorial radii that can be larger or smaller, depending onthe degree of differential rotation. For a fixed ratio of the axial rotationrate to the surface equatorial rotation rate, increasingly rapid rotationgenerally deepens convective envelopes, shrinks convective cores, and can leadto the presence of a convective core (envelope) in a 1 M_sun (2 M_sun) model, afeature that is absent in a nonrotating star of the same mass. The positions ofdifferentially rotating models for a given mass M in the H-R diagram can beshifted in such a way as to approximate the nonrotating ZAMS over ranges inluminosity and effective temperature that correspond to a mass interval betweenM and about 0.7 M. We briefly note a few of the implications of these results,including (i) possible ambiguities arising from similarities between theproperties of rotating and nonrotating models of different masses, (ii) areduced radiative luminosity for a young, rapidly rotating Sun, (iii) thenuclear destruction of lithium and other light metallic species in the layersbeneath an outer convective envelope, and (iv), the excitation of solar-likeoscillations and the operation of a solar-like hydromagnetic dynamo in some1.5-2 M_sun stars.

A cognitive radio can operate as a secondary system in a given spectrum. Thisoperation should use limited power in order not to disturb the communication byprimary spectrum user. Under such conditions, in this paper we investigate howto maximize the spectral efficiency in the secondary system. A secondaryreceiver observes a multiple access channel of two users, the secondary and theprimary transmitter, respectively. We show that, for spectrally-efficientoperation, the secondary system should apply Opportunistic InterferenceCancellation (OIC). With OIC, the secondary system decodes the primary signalwhen such an opportunity is created by the primary rate and the power receivedfrom the primary system. For such an operation, we derive the achievable datarate in the secondary system. When the primary signal is decodable, we devise amethod, based on superposition coding, by which the secondary system canachieve the maximal possible rate. Finally, we investigate the power allocationin the secondary system when multiple channels are used. We show that theoptimal power allocation with OIC can be achieved through interceptedwater-filling instead of the conventional water-filling. The results show asignificant gain for the rate achieved through an opportunistic interferencecancellation.

Following the early Swift X-ray observations of the latest outburst of therecurrent nova RS Ophiuchi in February 2006 (Paper I), we present new 1Dhydrodynamical models of the system which take into account all three phases ofthe remnant evolution. The models suggest a novel way of modelling the systemby treating the outburst as a sudden increase then decrease in wind mass-lossrate and velocity. The differences between this wind model and previousPrimakoff-type simulations are described. A more complex structure, even in 1D,is revealed through the presence of both forward and reverse shocks, with aseparating contact discontinuity. The effects of radiative cooling areinvestigated and key outburst parameters such as mass-loss rate, ejectavelocity and mass are varied. The shock velocities as a function of time arecompared to the ones derived in Paper I. We show how the manner in which thematter is ejected controls the evolution of the shock and that for awell-cooled remnant, the shock deceleration rate depends on the amount ofenergy that is radiated away.

A numerical analysis of several phases in the cold neutral two flavor densequark matter is realized. In the analysis, besides the normal, single planewave LOFF, and color superconducting 2SC/g2SC phases, we also include twogluonic phases (the gluonic cylindrical phase II and the gluonic color-spinlocked one). It is shown that these two gluonic phases are dynamically realizedand, on the basis of this analysis, the phase diagram with respect to thecoupling strength in this medium is suggested. In particular, it is shown thatthe gluonic phases are energetically favorable in a wide region of theparameter space. On the other hand, there also exists a window where the LOFFphase is stable. The 2SC state and the normal one are realized in the strongand weak coupling regimes, respectively, as was expected.

We present a detailed analysis of star formation activity in the vicinity of active galactic nuclei (AGN). By focusing on the interplay between AGN feedback and star formation processes, we aim to shed light on the complex interactions occurring within these systems. Using multi-wavelength observations and state-of-the-art imaging techniques, we investigate the spatial distribution and properties of star-forming regions surrounding AGN. Our findings reveal two distinct scenarios: one characterized by ongoing star formation, and another depicting the aftermath of a starburst event.Through adaptive optics observations, we identify signatures of recent nuclear star formation that have ceased but left significant imprints on the surrounding environment. These observations provide insights into the mechanisms through which gas inflows occur, leading to starburst phenomena. Furthermore, by combining phenomenological approaches with analytical modeling and hydrodynamic simulations, we explore the influence of stellar evolution on gas inflow and AGN activity regulation. Our results suggest a tightly interwoven relationship between the mass accretion rate into the central region and the subsequent starburst activity. Supernovae play a crucial role in delaying accretion by generating starburst winds, leaving behind a clumpy interstellar medium conducive to slower stellar outflows and the formation of dense turbulent disks at parsec scales. These processes reconcile the observations obtained with adaptive optics and the very structure seen with high-resolution instruments.Additionally, we compile a comprehensive dataset encompassing a range of dense stellar systems, including globular clusters, massive star clusters, nuclear star clusters, and galaxy spheroids. Surprisingly, we find a striking similarity in the maximum stellar surface density attained in the central regions of these diverse systems.

The modeling of the early universe is done through the quantization of aFriedmann-Robertson-Walker model with positive curvature. The material contentconsists of two fluids: radiation and Chaplygin gas. The quantization of thesemodels is made by following the Wheeler and DeWitt's prescriptions. Using theSchutz formalism, the time notion is recovered and the Wheeler-DeWitt equationtransforms into a time dependent Schr\"{o}dinger equation, which rules thedynamics of the early universe, under the action of an effective potential$V_{ef}$. Using a finite differences method and the Crank-Nicholson scheme, ina code implemented in the program OCTAVE, we solve the corresponding timedependent Schr\"{o}dinger equation and obtain the time evolution of a initialwave packet. This wave packet satisfies appropriate boundary conditions. Thecalculation of the tunneling probabilities shows that the universe may emergefrom the Planck era to an inflationary phase. It also shows that, the tunnelingprobability is a function of the mean energy of the initial wave packet and oftwo parameters related to the Chaplygin gas. We also show a comparison betweenthese results and those obtained by the WKB approximation.

We study a system of two quantum dots connected by a hopping bridge. Both thedots and connecting region are assumed to be in universal crossover regimesbetween Gaussian Orthogonal and Unitary ensembles. Using a diagrammaticapproach appropriate for energy separations much larger than the level spacingwe obtain the ensemble-averaged one- and two-particle Green's functions. Itturns out that the diffuson and cooperon parts of the two-particle Green'sfunction can be described by separate scaling functions. We then use thisinformation to investigate a model interacting system in which one dot has anattractive s-wave reduced Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer interaction, while theother is noninteracting but subject to an orbital magnetic field. We find thatthe critical temperature is {\it nonmonotonic} in the flux through the seconddot in a certain regime of interdot coupling. Likewise, the fluctuationmagnetization above the critical temperature is also nonmonotonic in thisregime, can be either diamagnetic or paramagnetic, and can be deduced from thecooperon scaling function.

In line with the local philicity concept proposed by Chattaraj et al.(Chattaraj, P. K.; Maiti, B.; Sarkar, U. J. Phys. Chem. A. 2003, 107, 4973) anda dual descriptor derived by Toro-Labbe and coworkers (Morell, C.; Grand, A.;Toro-Labbe, A. J. Phys. Chem. A. 2005, 109, 205), we propose a multiphilicdescriptor. It is defined as the difference between nucleophilic (Wk+) andelectrophilic (Wk-) condensed philicity functions. This descriptor is capableof simultaneously explaining the nucleophilicity and electrophilicity of thegiven atomic sites in the molecule. Variation of these quantities along thepath of a soft reaction is also analyzed. Predictive ability of this descriptorhas been successfully tested on the selected systems and reactions.Corresponding force profiles are also analyzed in some representative cases.Also, to study the intra- and intermolecular reactivities another relateddescriptor namely, the nucleophilicity excess (DelW-+) for a nucleophile, overthe electrophilicity in it has been defined and tested on all-metal aromaticcompounds.
