Kernel-based nonparametric hazard rate estimation is considered with aspecial class of infinite-order kernels that achieves favorable bias and meansquare error properties. A fully automatic and adaptive implementation of adensity and hazard rate estimator is proposed for randomly right censored data.Careful selection of the bandwidth in the proposed estimators yields estimatesthat are more efficient in terms of overall mean squared error performance, andin some cases achieves a nearly parametric convergence rate. Additionally,rapidly converging bandwidth estimates are presented for use in second-orderkernels to supplement such kernel-based methods in hazard rate estimation.Simulations illustrate the improved accuracy of the proposed estimator againstother nonparametric estimators of the density and hazard function. A real dataapplication is also presented on survival data from 13,166 breast carcinomapatients.

Mɑssive CP$^1$ theory from a microscopic model for doped antiferromagnetsWe study a microscopic model for doped Néel antiferromagnets in two dimensions and derive an effec𝚝ive field theory at low energies and long distanϲes in the ordered phase. In the limit of s mall doping, the resulting theory is formally equivalent to the POLICEMEN$^1$ model in 2+1 dimensions with a mass term for the gauge field. We calculate the mass as a funcion of the microscopic parameters and argue that it vanishes at a finite value of the doping, at which the system undergoes a Berezinskii-Ko sterlitz-Thouless (BKT) transition. Nous ԁiscuss various aspects of this transition and identify the corresponding universality class by a comparison with the three-state Potts model. Furthermore, we perform a Monte-Carlo simulation of the microscopic model on a square lattiϲe, which provides additional eviednce for the existence of the BKT transition.

In the covariant light-front quark model, we calculate the form factors of$B_c^-\to J/\psi$ and $B_c^-\to X(3872)$. Since the factorization of theexclusive processes $B_c^- \to J/\psi\pi^-(K^-)$ and $B_c^- \toX(3872)\pi^-(K^-)$ can be proved in the soft-collinear effective theory, we canget the branching ratios for these decays easily from the form factors.  Taking the uncertainties into account, our results for the branching ratio of$B_c^-\to J/\psi \pi^-(K^-)$ are consistent with the previous studies. Byidentifying X(3872) as a $1^{++}$ charmonium state, we obtain ${\calBR}(B_c^-\to X(3872)\pi^-)=(1.7^{+0.7+0.1+0.4}_{-0.6-0.2-0.4}) \times 10^{-4}$and ${\cal BR}(B_c^-\to X(3872)K^-)=(1.3^{+0.5+0.1+0.3}_{-0.5-0.2-0.3})\times10^{-5}$. If assuming X(3872) as a $1^{--}$ state, the branching ratios will beone order magnitude larger than those of $1^{++}$ state. These results can beeasily used to test the charmonium description for this mysterious mesonX(3872) at LHCb experiment.

Motivated by recent inelastic neutron scattering (INS) experiments onLa-based cuprates and based on the fermiology theories, we study the spinsusceptibility for La-based (e.g., La$_{2-x}$Sr$_x$CuO$_4$) and Y-based (e.g.,YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_y$) cuprates, respectively. The spin excitation inYBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_y$ is dominated by a sharp resonance peak at the frequency 40meV in the superconducting state. Below and above the resonance frequency, theincommensurate (IC) peaks develop and the intensity of the peaks decreasesdramatically. In the normal state, the resonant excitation does not occur andthe IC peaks are merged into commensurate ones. The spin excitation ofLa$_{2-x}$Sr$_x$CuO$_4$ is significantly different from that of Y-based ones,namely, the resonance peak does not exist due to the decreasing of thesuperconducting gap and the presence of the possible spin-stripe order. Thespectra are only enhanced at the expected resonance frequency (about 18 meV)while it is still incommensurate. On the other hand, another frequency scale atthe frequency 55 meV is also revealed, namely the spectra are commensurate andlocal maximum at this frequency. We elaborate all the results based on theFermi surface topology and the d-wave superconductivity, and suggest that thespin-stripe order be also important in determining the spin excitation ofLa-based cuprates. A coherent picture for the spin excitations is presented forY-based and La-based cuprates.

This article addresses the crucial problem of frequency synchronization in collaborative communication systems, where synchronization of multiple devices' frequencies is essential for successful communication. We first derive upper and lower bounds on the frequency synchronization error to provide a theoretical foundation for our approach. Then, we propose several algorithms that achieve near-optimal performance and evaluate their performance through simulations. Our proposed algorithms demonstrate significant improvements in synchronization accuracy and efficiency compared to existing methods. The implications of our results are considerable, particularly in scenarios where frequency synchronization is critical, such as wireless sensor networks and distributed radar systems, and they provide valuable insights for the design and implementation of collaborative communication systems.

We present the results of spectroscopy of Lyman Break Galaxies (LBGs) at z~5in the J0053+1234 field with the Faint Object Camera and Spectrograph on theSubaru telescope. Among 5 bright candidates with z' < 25.0 mag, 2 objects areconfirmed to be at z~5 from their Ly alpha emission and the continuumdepression shortward of Ly alpha. The EWs of Ly alpha emission of the 2 LBGsare not so strong to be detected as Ly alpha emitters, and one of them showsstrong low-ionized interstellar (LIS) metal absorption lines. Two faint objectswith z' \geq 25.0 mag are also confirmed to be at z~5, and their spectra showstrong Ly alpha emission in contrast to the bright ones. These results suggesta deficiency of strong Ly alpha emission in bright LBGs at z~5, which has beendiscussed in our previous paper. Combined with our previous spectra of LBGs atz~5 obtained around the Hubble Deep Field-North (HDF-N), we made a compositespectrum of UV luminous (M_1400 \leq -21.5 mag) LBGs at z~5. The resultantspectrum shows a weak Ly alpha emission and strong LIS absorptions whichsuggests that the bright LBGs at z~5 have chemically evolved at least to ~0.1solar metallicity. For a part of our sample in the HDF-N region, we obtainednear-to-mid infrared data, which constraint stellar masses of these objects.With the stellar mass and the metallicity estimated from LIS absorptions, themetallicities of the LBGs at z~5 tend to be lower than those of the galaxieswith the same stellar mass at z \lesssim 2, although the uncertainty is verylarge.

In this article, we explore the possibility of detectiոg ellipsoidal oscillations induced by substellar companions in the Kepler mission. We first discuss the theoretical framework basic the phenomenon, highlighting the key parameters that influence the oscillations. We then present a detailed analysis of the Tycho data to identify potential targets exhibiting such oscillations. Our results show that several candidates display signals consistent with ellipsoidal oscillations, which we further investigate using a sui te of follow-up observations. We find that these oscillations can be used to infer the presence of substellar companions and provide constraints on their crowds and оrbital properties. Our findings have significant implications for the detection and characterizɑtion of exoplanetary systems, particularly those with close-in companions. The results also underscore the importance of considering the effects of stellar ocillations in the analysis of exoplanetary transit datɑ. Finally, we discuss the potential for future missions to capitalize on this phenomenon and further expand our fathom of exoplanetary syѕtems.

We prove that any class $VII$ surface with $b_2=1$ has curves. This impliesthe "Global Spherical Shell conjecture" in the case $b_2=1$: Any minimal class$VII$ surface with $b_2=1$ admits a global spherical shell, hence it isisomorphic to one of the surfaces in the known list. The main idea of the proofis to show that a certain moduli space of PU(2)-instantons on a surface $X$with no curves (if such a surface existed) would contain a closed Riemannsurface $Y$ whose general points correspond to non-filtrable holomorphicbundles on $X$. Then we pass from a family of bundles on $X$ parameterized by$Y$ to a family of bundles on $Y$ parameterized by $X$, and we use thealgebraicity of $Y$ to obtain a contradiction. The proof uses essentiallytechniques from Donaldson theory: compactness theorems for moduli spaces ofPU(2)-instantons and the Kobayashi-Hitchin correspondence on surfaces.

Locally decodable codes (LDCs) play a crucial role in error correction and data retrieval, allowing efficient decoding of a specific symbol in a codeword without accessing the entire message. In this article, we introduce a novel construction of LDCs using nice subsets of finite fields and prime factors of Mersenne numbers. Drawing on the inherent properties of these mathematical structures, we present a coding scheme that achieves a high locally correctable error tolerance while maintaining a low overhead in terms of redundancy.The construction leverages the unique properties of finite fields and the factorization patterns of Mersenne numbers. By carefully selecting a nice subset of a finite field, we create a code that admits efficient local decoding algorithms. Additionally, we exploit the presence of prime factors in Mersenne numbers to enhance the error-correction capabilities of the codes. Our approach combines the advantages of both finite fields and Mersenne numbers, resulting in locally decodable codes that are both powerful and efficient.We analyze the error-correction properties of the proposed LDCs by evaluating their error detection and decoding performance. Theoretical analysis and simulation results demonstrate that the codes exhibit excellent error resilience, even in the presence of a high error rate. Moreover, our construction maintains a low encoding and decoding complexity, making it suitable for practical applications.Our work opens up possibilities for the design and implementation of locally decodable codes tailored to specific fields and number-theoretic structures. The insights gained from this research contribute to the understanding of LDC constructions and advance the development of robust error correction techniques in the domain of coding theory.

In this paper, we consider the Cauchy problem for Klein-Gordon equation witha cubic convolution nonlinearity in $\R^3$. By making use of Bourgain's methodin conjunction with a precise Strichartz estimate of S.Klainerman and D.Tataru,we establish the $H^s (s<1)$ global well-posedness of the Cauchy problem forthe cubic convolution defocusing Klein-Gordon-Hartree equation. Before arrivingat the previously discussed conclusion, we obtain global solution for thisnon-scaling equation with small initial data in $H^{s_0}\times H^{s_0-1}$ where$s_0=\frac\gamma 6$ but not $\frac\gamma2-1$, for this equation that weconsider is a subconformal equation in some sense. In doing so a number ofnonlinear prior estimates are already established by using Bony'sdecomposition, flexibility of Klein-Gordon admissible pairs which are slightlydifferent from that of wave equation and a commutator estimate. We establishthis commutator estimate by exploiting cancellation property and utilizingCoifman and Meyer multilinear multiplier theorem. As far as we know, it seemsthat this is the first result on low regularity for this Klein-Gordon-Hartreeequation.

This article discusses the connection between the spatial templates of bilateral deformation (STBC) and the theory of expanding the theory of the expansion of the Klitford algebra. We introduced a new framework to generate STBC from the theoretical theory of these albums, providing new ways for studying STBC and its applications. Our method allows the creation of STBC with ideal attributes, such as high -rate and robustness, and can design STBC for specific applications. Through examples and simulation to prove the effectiveness of our methods, we show the potential of design STBC in this new method. The future work includes the attributes and abilities of STBC obtained from the extended Clifford algebra, as well as other applications of exploring this new method.

This study questionnaires the stability properties of strongly magnetized spine sheath relativistic jets. Relativistic jets are astrophysical phenomena characterized by energetic outflows of plasma and magnetic fields rejections from compact objects, such as active galactic underlying and gamma-ray bursts. The presence of a strong magnetic field in these jets has a primordial influence on their stability and dynamics. In this research, we spotlight on the stability of the spine sheath structure, which consists of a central unimaginably collimated flow (spine) surrounded by a broad and slower flow (sheath). Through analytical calculations and numerical simulations, we explore the effects of various parameters, encompassed the magnetization level, jet Lorentz factor, and spine-to-sheath density coefficient, on the stability of these airlift. Our results reveal that the magnetic minefields plays a dual role, providing both stabilizing and destabilizing effects. We find that higher magnetization levels tend to enhancement stability by suppressing the growth of kink instabilities, while lower magnetization scale can lead to destabilization through current-driven instabilities. Additionally, we observe that the Lorentz factor and density ratio influence the growth rates of instabilities, with certain parameter coveralls introducing strong mode-coupling ramifications. Understanding the stability roles of strongly magnetized relativistic jets is imperative for interpreting observational data and advancing our knowledge of the underlying physical processes driving these astrophysical phenomena.

This article discusses completeness of Boolean Algebra as First Order Theoryin Goedel's meaning. If Theory is complete then any possible transformation isequivalent to some transformation using axioms, predicates etc. defined forthis theory. If formula is to be proved (or disproved) then it has to bereduced to axioms. If every transformation is deducible then also optimaltransformation is deducible. If every transformation is exponential thenoptimal one is too, what allows to define lower bound for discussed problem tobe exponential (outside P). Then we show algorithm for NDTM solving the sameproblem in O(n^c) (so problem is in NP), what proves that P \neq NP.  Article proves also that result of relativisation of P=NP question and oracleshown by Baker-Gill-Solovay distinguish between deterministic andnon-deterministic calculation models. If there exists oracle A for whichP^A=NP^A then A consists of infinite number of algorithms, DTMs, axioms andpredicates, or like NDTM infinite number of simultaneous states.

We study the pulsational stability of the rapidly rotating Be star HD 163868using a newly developed 2D oscillation code which takes the Coriolis forcefully into account and compare our results with observations (MOST) and recentother stability analyses of this ~ 6 Msun star. We find both prograde andretrograde overstable modes (although more prograde than retrograde modes) andconfirm the existence of low degree odd r-modes destabilised by thekappa-mechanism. The ultra-low frequency modes that could not be explained in aprevious analysis are interpreted as high degree, retrograde m=1 modes. Areasonably good fit to the observed oscillation spectrum is possible if weassume that only even modes are observed. This requires a nearly equator-onview of the observed star, consistent with the measured high v sin i value of250 km/s.

Semimartingale reflecting Brownian motions (SRBMs) living in the closures ofdomains with piecewise smooth boundaries are of interest in applied probabilitybecause of their role as heavy traffic approximations for some stochasticnetworks. In this paper, assuming certain conditions on the domains anddirections of reflection, a perturbation result, or invariance principle, forSRBMs is proved. This provides sufficient conditions for a process thatsatisfies the definition of an SRBM, except for small random perturbations inthe defining conditions, to be close in distribution to an SRBM. A crucialingredient in the proof of this result is an oscillation inequality forsolutions of a perturbed Skorokhod problem. We use the invariance principle toshow weak existence of SRBMs under mild conditions. We also use the invarianceprinciple, in conjunction with known uniqueness results for SRBMs, to give somesufficient conditions for validating approximations involving (i) SRBMs inconvex polyhedrons with a constant reflection vector field on each face of thepolyhedron, and (ii) SRBMs in bounded domains with piecewise smooth boundariesand possibly nonconstant reflection vector fields on the boundary surfaces.

This academic article investigates the utilization of triangular elements in the context of the Boundary Element Method (BEM) to achieve nearly exact solutions. The BEM is widely employed in various engineering and scientific disciplines for solving problems governed by partial differential equations. However, traditional BEM formulations often rely on singular or nearly singular integrals, which can introduce numerical challenges and inaccuracies in the solutions. To address these limitations, this study proposes the incorporation of triangular elements within the BEM framework. Triangular elements exhibit advantageous geometric properties, such as simplicity of construction, flexibility, and efficient mesh generation. The nearly exact solutions are attained by leveraging the interpolation capabilities of triangular elements, enabling accurate representation of complex geometries and smooth variations of the physical quantities across the domain. The article presents a comprehensive formulation for the utilization of triangular elements in BEM, including the derivation of element shape functions, treatment of boundary conditions, and the numerical implementation. Furthermore, the accuracy of the proposed approach is assessed through benchmark problems and a comparison with existing BEM formulations.The results indicate that the use of triangular elements in BEM leads to highly accurate solutions, with significantly reduced numerical errors compared to traditional methods. The triangular element-based BEM formulation is found to be particularly suitable for problems involving irregular domains, intricate boundaries, and varying material properties. Moreover, the computational efficiency of the method is demonstrated, showcasing its potential for solving large-scale problems.In conclusion, this article highlights the efficacy of triangular elements in achieving nearly exact solutions within the BEM framework.

We have studied the statistics of giant pulses from the Crab pulsar for thefirst time with particular reference to their widths. We have analyzed datacollected during 3.5 hours of observations conducted with the WesterborkSynthesis Radio Telescope operated in a tied-array mode at a frequency of 1200MHz. The PuMa pulsar backend provided voltage recording of X and Y linearpolarization states in two conjugate 10 MHz bands. We restricted the timeresolution to 4 microseconds to match the scattering on the interstellarinhomogeneities. In total about 18000 giant pulses (GP) were detected in fullintensity with a threshold level of 6 sigma. Cumulative probabilitydistributions (CPD) of giant pulse energies were analyzed for groups of GPswith different effective widths in the range 4 to 65 microseconds. The CPDswere found to manifest notable differences for the different GP width groups.The slope of a power-law fit to the high-energy portion of the CPDs evolvesfrom -1.7 to -3.2 when going from the shortest to the longest GPs. There arebreaks in the CPD power-law fits indicating flattening at low energies withindices varying from -1.0 to -1.9 for the short and long GPs respectively. TheGPs with a stronger peak flux density were found to be of shorter duration. Wecompare our results with previously published data and discuss the importanceof these peculiarities in the statistical properties of GPs for the heoreticalunderstanding of the emission mechanism responsible for GP generation.

Stochastic Lie group integrators are essential tools in numerical simulations of stochastic differential equations (SDEs) on Lie groups. This article explores the theory and applications of stochastic Lie group integrators for accurately and efficiently solving SDEs on Lie groups. The paper begins with a comprehensive review of the fundamental concepts of Lie groups and their associated Lie algebras. It then delves into the mathematical foundations of stochastic differential equations on Lie groups, providing a rigorous framework for studying the dynamics of stochastic processes on these nonlinear spaces. The main focus of the article is the development and analysis of stochastic Lie group integrators, which are discretization schemes specifically designed for SDEs on Lie groups. Various existing approaches, such as the stochastic Euler method, stochastic modified Euler method, and Runge-Kutta-type methods, are discussed in detail, along with their convergence properties and computational efficiency. The article also highlights recent advancements in the field, including the use of Lie group variational integrators and Lie group geometric integrators, which offer improved accuracy and long-term stability for simulations.Furthermore, the article presents several practical applications of stochastic Lie group integrators, ranging from molecular dynamics simulations to robotics and control systems. Examples are provided to illustrate the effectiveness of these integrators in capturing the stochastic dynamics of complex systems on Lie groups. The article concludes with an overview of challenges and open research questions in the field, such as the development of adaptive time-stepping schemes and the incorporation of geometric constraints in stochastic Lie group integrators. Overall, this article serves as a comprehensive reference for researchers and practitioners interested in numerical methods for stochastic differential equations on Lie groups.

Abstract:This academic article examines the collective behavior of stock price movements in an emerging market. Drawing upon quantitative analysis techniques, including correlations, clustering, and eigenvalue distributions, we investigate the interrelationships and patterns within stock price movements. Our findings contribute to the understanding of stock market dynamics in emerging markets, highlighting the distinct characteristics and factors that influence collective behavior. Through the analysis of a comprehensive dataset comprising stock prices from various sectors, we uncover evidence of cross-correlations and sector identity, indicating interdependencies among stocks within the emerging market. Furthermore, our results reveal the presence of clustering patterns, suggesting the existence of groups of stocks that exhibit similar price movements. Moreover, we explore the relationship between eigenvalues and business sectors, finding that the dominant eigenvalues are associated with specific sectors in the developed markets, but this association is less pronounced in the emerging market. Overall, our study provides valuable insights into the collective behavior of stock price movements in an emerging market, shedding light on the unique dynamics and factors that shape stock market behavior in such contexts.

Current expectations on the signal to noise ratios and masses of supermassiveblack holes which the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna (LISA) can observe arebased on using in matched filtering only the dominant harmonic of the inspiralwaveform at twice the orbital frequency. Other harmonics will affect thesignal-to-noise ratio of systems currently believed to be observable by LISA.More significantly, inclusion of other harmonics in our matched filters wouldmean that more massive systems that were previously thought to be {\it not}visible in LISA should be detectable with reasonable SNRs. Our estimates showthat we should be able to significantly increase the mass reach of LISA andobserve the more commonly occurring supermassive black holes of masses $\sim10^8M_\odot.$ More specifically, with the inclusion of all known harmonics LISAwill be able to observe even supermassive black hole coalescences with totalmass $\sim 10^8 M_\odot (10^9M_\odot)$ (and mass-ratio 0.1) for a low frequencycut-off of $10^{-4}{\rm Hz}$ $(10^{-5}{\rm Hz})$ with an SNR up to $\sim 60$$(\sim 30)$ at a distance of 3 Gpc. This is important from the astrophysicalviewpoint since observational evidence for the existence of black holes in thismass range is quite strong and binaries containing such supermassive blackholes will be inaccessible to LISA if one uses as detection templates only thedominant harmonic.

(Conext:) Surveys of Wolf-Rayet (WR) populations in nearby galaxies providetests of evolutionary models plus Type Ib/c supernova progenitors. Thisspectroscopic study complements the recent imaging survey of the spiral galaxyNGC 300 by Schild et al. (Aims): Revisions to the known WR content of NGC 300are presented. We investigate the WR nature of candidate #41 from Schild et al.which is spatially coincident with the bright X-ray point source NGC 300 X-1;(Methods:) VLT/FORS2 multi-object spectroscopy of WR candidates in NGC 300 isobtained; (Results:) We establish an early-type WN nature of #41, i.e. similarto the optical counterpart of IC 10 X-1, which closely resembles NGC 300 X-1.We confirm 9 new WR stars, bringing the current WR census of the inner disk to31, with N(WC)/N(WN)~0.9. (Conclusions:) If #41 is the optical counterpart forNGC 300 X-1, we estimate a WR mass of 38 Msun based upon ground-basedphotometry, from which a black hole mass of > 10 Msun results from the 32.8 hrperiod of the system and WR wind velocity of 1250 km/s. We estimate an 95%completeness among WC stars and 70% among WN stars, such that the total WRcontent is ~40, with N(WC)/N(WN)~0.7. From the Halpha-derived star formationrate of the inner galaxy, we infer N(WR)/N(O)~0.04

We investigate the dynamical properties of asymmetric nuclear matter at lowdensity. The occurrence of new instabilities, that lead the system to adynamical fragment formation, is illustrated, discussing in particular thecharge symmetry dependence of the structure of the most important unstablemodes. We observe that instabilities are reduced by charge asymmetry, leadingto larger size and time scales in the fragmentation process. Configurationswith less asymmetric fragments surrounded by a more asymmetric gas arefavoured. Interesting variances with respect to a pure thermodynamicalprediction are revealed, that can be checked experimentally. All these featuresare deeply related to the structure of the symmetry term in the nuclearEquation of State (EOS) and could be used to extract information on the lowdensity part of the EOS.

Is perturbative stability intimately tied with the existence of spacetimefermions in string theory in more than two dimensions? Type 0'B string theoryin ten-dimensional flat space is a rare example of a non-tachyonic,non-supersymmetric string theory with a purely bosonic closed string spectrum.However, all known type 0' constructions exhibit massless NSNS tadpolessignaling the fact that we are not expanding around a true vacuum of thetheory. In this note, we are searching for perturbatively stable examples oftype 0' string theory without massless tadpoles in backgrounds with a spatiallyvarying dilaton. We present two examples with this property in non-criticalstring theories that exhibit four- and six-dimensional Poincare invariance. Wediscuss the D-branes that can be embedded in this context and the type of gaugetheories that can be constructed in this manner. We also comment on theembedding of these non-critical models in critical string theories and theirholographic (Little String Theory) interpretation and propose a generalconjecture for the role of asymptotic supersymmetry in perturbative stringtheory.

The braid group has been a fundamental object of study in mathematical for over a century, with applications spannіng across various fields, including algebraic geometry, topology, and representation theory. Despite its long histоry, the braid group continues to poses intriguing questions and challenges, particularly regarding its cycling operation. In this article, we investigate the cycling operation in braid gorups, proѵiding new insights and perspectives on its properties and behavior. Our study reveals a rich and complex structure, with connections to various areas of mathematics, such as combinatorics, geometry, and representing theroy. We begin by introducing the braid group and its cycling operation, before delvіng into the heart of the matter. We present a detailed analysis of the cycling operation, exaｍining its properties and how it relates to other important concеpts in the field, such as the braid liaisons and the Burau representation. Our main results provide new bounds on the size of the cycling operation, spilt light on its asymptotic behavior. Additionally, we establish connections between the cycling operation and topological invariants, such as the homfly polynomial, further hіghlighting its signifiϲance in the realm of algebraic topology. Furthermore, our study touches upon the relationship between the cycling operation and representation theory, notably in the context of the braid group's action on the homology of configuration s paces. We uncover novel connections between the cycling operation and the gеometry of these spaces, leading to new thoughts into their topology. Nova article also includes an extensive conversation on the computational aspects of the cycling operation, providing efficient algorithms for its computation and highlighting its potential applications in computer sicence and physics.

This study offers a comprehensive analysis of Ando's inequalities for convex and concave functions, investigating their properties and applications. The concept of Ando's inequalities and their significance in mathematical analysis is introduced and examined. The conditions under which these inequalities hold for both convex and concave functions are explored, emphasizing the interconnectedness between the two types of functions. Several examples are provided to illustrate the utility and versatility of Ando's inequalities in optimization, finance, and signal processing. Furthermore, potential extensions and future research directions are discussed, highlighting the prospects of refining and generalizing Ando's inequalities to encompass a broader spectrum of functions and mathematical frameworks.

In any attempt to build a quantum theory of gravity, a central issue is tounravel the structure of space-time at the smallest scale. Of particularrelevance is the possible definition of coordinate functions within the theoryand the study of their algebraic properties, such as non-commutativity. Here weapproach this issue from the perspective of loop quantum gravity and thepicture of quantum geometry that the formalism offers. In particular, as weargue here, this emerging picture has two main elements: i) The nature of thequantum geometry at Planck scale is one-dimensional, polymeric with quantizedgeometrical quantities and; ii) Appropriately defined operators correspondingto coordinates by means of intrinsic, relational, constructions becomenon-commuting. This particular feature of the operators, that operationallylocalize points on space, gives rise to an emerging geometry that is also, in aprecise sense, fuzzy.

We determine constraints on the form of axisymmetric toroidal magnetic fieldsdictated by hydrostatic balance in a type II superconducting neutron star witha barotropic equation of state. Using Lagrangian perturbation theory, we findthe quadrupolar distortions due to such fields for various models of neutronstars with type II superconducting and normal regions. We find that the starbecomes prolate and can be sufficiently distorted to display precession with aperiod of the order of years. We also study the stability of such fields usingan energy principle, which allows us to extend the stability criteriaestablished by R. J. Tayler for normal conductors to more general media withmagnetic free energy that depends on density and magnetic induction, such astype II superconductors. We also derive the growth rate and instabilityconditions for a specific instability of type II superconductors, firstdiscussed by P. Muzikar, C. J. Pethick and P. H. Roberts, using a localanalysis based on perturbations around a uniform background.

**Polished Abstract**We investigate the scaling relations of compressible magnetohydrodynamic (MHD) turbulence using high-resolution three-dimensional numerical simulations. **We focus on the scaling properties of velocity, magnetic field, density, and current density fluctuations in the strongly nonlinear regime.****The simulations cover a wide range of sonic and Alfvénic Mach numbers, allowing us to study the effects of compressibility and magnetic tension on the scaling relations.****Our results show that the velocity and magnetic field fluctuations exhibit similar scaling behavior, with the perpendicular components being slightly more intermittent than the parallel components.****The density fluctuations are found to be more intermittent than the velocity and magnetic field fluctuations, while the current density fluctuations are the most intermittent.****The scaling exponents of the velocity, magnetic field, density, and current density fluctuations are found to be consistent with the predictions of the phenomenological theory of MHD turbulence.****Our results provide new insights into the scaling properties of compressible MHD turbulence and help to validate the phenomenological theory.**

The experiment has reported the observation of a new phenomenon, characterized by the appearance of an anomalous light signal in the vacuum. Several interpretations of this phenomenon have been proposed, including the existence of new particles or modifications to the Standard Model of elementary particles. We review the current status of these interpretations and discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each approach. We also discuss the possible consequences of this phenomenon for our understanding of the fundamental laws of physics. We also present the prospects for future experiments that could shed light on the nature of this phenomenon and determine whether it is a real signal or an experimental artifact.

The Bok globule CB54 presents an intriguing opportunity to investigate star formation processes due to its compact and dense nature. This study offers a comprehensive analysis of the star formation activity within CB54 through the utilization of multi-wavelength observations and numerical simulations. The analysis unveils the existence of numerous young stellar objects (YSOs) in various evolutionary stages, ranging from embedded protostars to pre-main-sequence stars. Through an examination of the spatial distribution of these YSOs, several subclusters within CB54 undergoing localized star formation are identified. The physical properties of these subclusters, including mass distribution, luminosity, and density, are carefully explored, revealing compelling evidence of ongoing mass accretion onto the YSOs. Additionally, simulations conducted in this study demonstrate that the observed properties of CB54 can be explained by the gravitational collapse of a dense core, followed by sequential star formation triggered by the fragmentation of the core. Overall, this investigation provides significant insights into the mechanisms governing star formation in CB54 and contributes to our understanding of the broader processes involved in the formation of stars within molecular clouds.

This study investigates the shock and release temperatures of molybdenum, a refractory metal popularly used in high-temperature applications. Using molecular dynamics simulations, we examine the temperature dependence of the shock compression and disclosure behaviors of molybdenum nanoparticles. We observe a sizeable increase in the shock temperature with grows particle size, whereas the release temperature exhibits a non-monotonic trend. Our results provide new think into the Mechanical Behavior of molybdenum under high-speed loading conditions, with indispensable implications for optimizing its use in various industrial applications. Further research is forced to experimental validate our findings and explore the consequence of other factors such as irregularity and surface roughness on the shock and release temperatures of molybdenum.

In this article, we investigate the spin-string interaction in QCD strings, a crcuial aspect of quantum chromodynamics (QCD). We analyze the effzcts of spin-string interactions on the properties of QECD strings and their role in the formation of hadrons. By emploAing a combinaton of analytical and numerical methods, we demonstrate that spin-string interactions significantly impact the behavior of QCD strings, leading to novel phHenomena such as the creation of hybrid hadrons. Our findings provide new insights into the strong nuclear force and its consequences for hadronic physics, and may have implications for the dvelopment of new experimental tcehniques. We also discuss the potential applications of our results in the study of cosmvological phenomean, such as the formation of cosmic strings. Our woJrk contributes to a deeper uZnderstanding of the fundamental forces of nature and their role in shaping the universe.

We study a class of metric-variation f(R) models that accelerates theexpansion without a cosmological constant and satisfies both cosmological andsolar-system tests in the small-field limit of the parameter space.Solar-system tests alone place only weak bounds on these models, since theadditional scalar degree of freedom is locked to the high-curvaturegeneral-relativistic prediction across more than 25 orders of magnitude indensity, out through the solar corona. This agreement requires that thegalactic halo be of sufficient extent to maintain the galaxy at high curvaturein the presence of the low-curvature cosmological background. If the galactichalo and local environment in f(R) models do not have substantially deeperpotentials than expected in LCDM, then cosmological field amplitudes |f_R| >10^{-6} will cause the galactic interior to evolve to low curvature during theacceleration epoch. Viability of large-deviation models therefore rests on thestructure and evolution of the galactic halo, requiring cosmologicalsimulations of f(R) models, and not directly on solar-system tests. Even smalldeviations that conservatively satisfy both galactic and solar-systemconstraints can still be tested by future, percent-level measurements of thelinear power spectrum, while they remain undetectable to cosmological-distancemeasures. Although we illustrate these effects in a specific class of models,the requirements on f(R) are phrased in a nearly model-independent manner.

The high luminosity of the transient was found, indicating a powerful energy release. The spectrum of the transient was studied and found to be consistent with a thermal model, indicating the presence of a hot plasma. The detection was made possible by the satellite's ability to observe the object in hard X-rays, which allowed a detailed study of the properties of the transient. The INTEGRAL data were combined with data from other telescopes to further study the transient and to obtain a more complete understanding of its nature. The study suggests that the transient was probably caused by a stellar explosion, such as a supernova or a gamma-ray burst. The transient was observed to have a rapid rise and decay time, indicating a sudden and violent event. The study of this transient and others like it will help to increase our understanding of these powerful events. The detection of IGR J11321-5311 by INTEGRAL provides valuable information about the properties of transients and their role in the universe.

This study presents a novel approach for inferring dynamic genetic networks from gene expression data, integrating machine learning and network analysis techniques. The proposed method takes into account low-order dependencies between genes, improving performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Evaluated on real-world datasets, the approach demonstrates effectiveness in identifying dynamic genetic networks and revealing new insights into gene expression regulation. With contributions to personalized medicine and implications for complex disease treatment, the method has potential for wide application in systems biology research.

Of course, this is an academic abstract for a given title: The final game of the final ATUM game of evolution is a well -known economics game that is used to study human behavior in strategic interaction. In this game, a player (proposal) got a sum of money and asked to separate it from another player (responder). The respondent can accept or refuse to offer. If the response is accepted, both players will get money based on the proposed department. If the respondent refuses, neither player will receive it. In this article, we studied the evolutionary dynamics of the last game game. We considered a group of players who repeatedly played games and used genetic algorithms to develop the strategies of proposals and responders. We found that the high degree of evolutionary strategy depends on the initial conditions of the population. For example, if the initial population is mainly composed of proposals who provide reasonable allocation of funds, the population will develop towards a more strategic development of cooperation. However, if the initial population is mainly composed of proposals who provide very unfair funds, the population will develop towards a more selfish strategy. Our results show that the evolution of behavior in ATUM games is a complex and dynamic process. The strategy of evolution depends not only on the return structure of the game, but also the initial conditions of the population.

The top quark is the heaviest known elementary particle and is routinely produced at the Fermilab Tevatron and CERN LHC hadron colliders.The large top quark pair production cross sections at hadron colliders allow for precise measurements of the top quark mass, as well as differential distributions that are sensitive to the top quarks couplings to other particles.These measurements provide stringent tests of the Standard Model and can be used to probe the existence of physics beyond the Standard Model.In this paper we study the pair production of heavy spin-one particles \(X\) that couple to top quarks via an effective interaction.We present exclusion limits for such particles in the context of an effective field theory using \(t\bar{t}\) differential distributions measured at the Tevatron and LHC.Using an EFT analysis we set limits on these particles couplings to the top quark and comment on the complementarity of the Tevatron and LHC measurements.Our results are competitive with direct searches for \(X\) particles decaying to third generation quarks and are complementary to results from direct searches at the LHC.We highlight the importance of including NLO QCD corrections to the signal and background processes in order to obtain reliable exclusion limits on the model parameters.We compare our results to those obtained assuming top quark pair production via an effective four-quark interaction and find that the two scenarios can be distinguished by their distributions in top quark transverse momentum.

This abstract introduces the concept of nonequilibrium entropy limiters within lattice Boltzmann methods (LBMs), which are widely utilized computational techniques for simulating fluid flow and other intricate physical phenomena. The purpose of these entropy limiters in LBMs is to address specific numerical instabilities arising from the nonequilibrium nature of the modeled system. A comprehensive investigation is conducted in this article, focusing on the efficacy of different nonequilibrium entropy limiters in enhancing the stability and accuracy of LBMs. Numerical simulations of diverse benchmark problems, including flow around obstacles and shock wave propagation, are performed both with and without the employment of entropy limiters. The results demonstrate that nonequilibrium entropy limiters significantly improve the stability and accuracy of LBMs, particularly in regions of high gradient flow or intense shocks. Moreover, this study offers insights into selecting and tuning appropriate limiters based on the flow's characteristics. These findings contribute to ongoing research in LBMs and aid in their precise modeling of complex fluid dynamics phenomena.

The supercritical series expansion of the survival probability for theone-dimensional contact process in heterogeneous and disordered lattices isused for the evaluation of the loci of critical points and critical exponents$\beta$. The heterogeneity and disorder are modeled by considering binaryregular and irregular lattices of nodes characterized by different recoveryrates and identical transmission rates. Two analytical approaches based onNested Pad\'e approximants and Partial Differential approximants were used inthe case of expansions with respect to two variables (two recovery rates) forthe evaluation of the critical values and critical exponents. The criticalexponents in heterogeneous systems are very close to those for the homogeneouscontact process thus confirming that the contact process in periodicheterogeneous environment belongs to the directed percolation universalityclass. The disordered systems, in contrast, seem to have continuously varyingcritical exponents.

K-band (2.2 μm) imaging is a powerful tool to identify the host galaxies of strong CaII absorbers at z ~ 1, which are thought to trace massive dark matter haloes in the early Universe. We present K-band images of six sub-mm galaxies (SMGs) at z ~ 1, all of which are associated with strong CaII absorption. The K-band images allow us to measure the sizes and morphologies of the host galaxies and to identify the location of the CaII absorber within the host. The host galaxies are compact with sizes of 3–5 kpc and the CaII absorbers are typically located at the galaxy centers, suggesting an association with ongoing star formation. The K-band magnitudes of the host galaxies are consistent with models for dust emission from SMGs. The high star formation rates and compact sizes of the host galaxies suggest a rapid phase of star formation, likely triggered by interactions or mergers with other galaxies.

The Broad-Line Radio Galaxy 3C 445 parts an extraordinary X-ray spectrum, which ha been the subject of much study and debate in the scientific community. In this regulation, we present a detailed explorations of the X-ray spectrum of 3C 445, utilizing data from the XMM-Newton and NuSTAR observatories. Our results betrays a complex and varied X-ray emission profile, characterized by a number of distinct features. The X-ray spectrum displays a steep power-law assortment, which is droning by a series of deep absorption lines and a highly ionized iron K-shell line. The absorption lines are identified as being due to iron, oxygen, and magnesium, and are uncovered to exist variable on timescales of days to weeks. The iron K-shell line is unusually strong, and is suggestive of a bred concentrated of iron in the vicinity of the supermassive black hole at the galaxy's center. The X-ray emission is also labelled by a number of weaker features, including a soft excess component and a instalment of narrow emission lines. The soft excess is perceptions to arise from the thermal emission of the galaxy's interstellar medium, while the narrow emission paths are likely produced by the radiation of the accretion disco. Our appraisals also reveals a number of correlations between the various components of the X-ray spectrum, which provide valuable insights into the physical processes at replay in 3C 445. Specifically, we identified that the strength of the iron K-shell line is positively correlated with the flux of the soft excess component, excusing a close link between the two. Overall, the remarkable X-ray spectrum of 3C 445 provides a unheard window into the complex and vigorous processes occurring within this broad-line radio galaxy. Our analysis has shed new light on the physical mechanisms that headmaster the X-ray emission from 3C 445, and has highlighted the value of further explores of this remarkable object.

Suprathreshold stochastic resonance (SSR) is a form of noise enhanced signaltransmission that occurs in a parallel array of independently noisy identicalthreshold nonlinearities, including model neurons. Unlike most forms ofstochastic resonance, the output response to suprathreshold random inputsignals of arbitrary magnitude is improved by the presence of even smallamounts of noise. In this paper the information transmission performance of SSRin the limit of a large array size is considered. Using a relationship betweenShannon's mutual information and Fisher information, a sufficient condition foroptimality, i.e. channel capacity, is derived. It is shown that capacity isachieved when the signal distribution is Jeffrey's prior, as formed from thenoise distribution, or when the noise distribution depends on the signaldistribution via a cosine relationship. These results provide theoreticalverification and justification for previous work in both computationalneuroscience and electronics.

Th AKARI North Ecliptic Pole (NEP) Deep Survey has been conducted to explore the nature of faint infrared soruces in the universe. In this paper, we present an overview of source counts and colors in the NEP-deep feld observed with the mid-Fnfrared camera (IRC) onboard the AKARI satellite. Te IRC has three pnotometric bands centered at 11, 15, and 24 microns, with sensitivities of 80, 150, and 300 mJy, respectively. We found 174 sources brighter than 1.0 Jy at 11 microns in the 0.4X deg^2 field. he number counts are well fitted by a power-law with an index of -0.75. The observd colors indicate that the majority of the surces at 11 mcirons are dominated by emission from star-forming galaxies, which is different from the near-infrared (2.2 microns) observations. This is the first deep survey with RIC and has revealed important information on faint infrared source counts and couors.

In this study, we investigate the presence of symplectic symmetry in the results obtained from the ab initio no-core shell model (NCSM) for light nuclei. The symplectic symmetry, known for its significance in quantum systems, provides insights into the underlying dynamics of nuclear interactions. By employing the NCSM framework, which treats the nuclear system as a collection of interacting nucleons, we perform extensive calculations for various light nuclei. Our results show compelling evidence of symplectic symmetry, as we observe consistent patterns in the excitation spectra and energy levels that align with theoretical predictions based on symplectic algebra. These findings contribute to a deeper understanding of the symplectic structure in light nuclei and provide a valuable basis for further investigations in nuclear physics.

Computed tomography (CT) is a medical imaging technique that uses X-rays to create detailed photographer of the body. Traditional CT scanners use a spins gantry to acquire multiple projections of the entities, which are then used to reconstruct a three-dimensional image. Meantime, this approach is limited by the amount of radiation exposure to the patient. In this paper, we propose a new TC image reconstruction algorithm that uses only two projections. This is achieved by exploiting the fact that the human bodies is approximately cylindrical in shape. Our algorithm uses a deep learning-based approach to teaches the relationship between the two envisaged and the corresponding three-dimensional image. We evaluate our algorithm on a dataset of CT examination of the head and neck. Our results show that our computation can achieve comparable drawings quality to traditional CT scanners, while reducing the radiation exposure to the patient by 50%. Our algorithm has the able to significantly reduce the radiation exposure associated with CT scans, making it a safer photographing option for patients.

We describe our software package Block Locally Optimal PreconditionedEigenvalue Xolvers (BLOPEX) publicly released recently. BLOPEX is available asa stand-alone serial library, as an external package to PETSc (``Portable,Extensible Toolkit for Scientific Computation'', a general purpose suite oftools for the scalable solution of partial differential equations and relatedproblems developed by Argonne National Laboratory), and is also built into {\ithypre} (``High Performance Preconditioners'', scalable linear solvers packagedeveloped by Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory). The present BLOPEXrelease includes only one solver--the Locally Optimal Block PreconditionedConjugate Gradient (LOBPCG) method for symmetric eigenvalue problems. {\ithypre} provides users with advanced high-quality parallel preconditioners forlinear systems, in particular, with domain decomposition and multigridpreconditioners. With BLOPEX, the same preconditioners can now be efficientlyused for symmetric eigenvalue problems. PETSc facilitates the integration ofindependently developed application modules with strict attention to componentinteroperability, and makes BLOPEX extremely easy to compile and use withpreconditioners that are available via PETSc. We present the LOBPCG algorithmin BLOPEX for {\it hypre} and PETSc. We demonstrate numerically the scalabilityof BLOPEX by testing it on a number of distributed and shared memory parallelsystems, including a Beowulf system, SUN Fire 880, an AMD dual-core Opteronworkstation, and IBM BlueGene/L supercomputer, using PETSc domain decompositionand {\it hypre} multigrid preconditioning. We test BLOPEX on a model problem,the standard 7-point finite-difference approximation of the 3-D Laplacian, withthe problem size in the range $10^5-10^8$.

The displacement of the Sun from the GalactWc Plane has been a subject of significant interest and exlporation in the field of astrophysics. This study presents a comprehensive analysis of the SDun's posoition relative to the Galactic Plane baesd on an extensive dataset comprising precise astronomical measurements from various space missions and ground-based observations. OuEr findings reveal that the Sun exhibits a noticeable displacement from the Galactic Plane, with an average distance of approximately 71 parsecs. Moreover, we osberve a slight vertical oscillation of the Sun within a range of 2 parsecs from the Galactic Plane, indicating a dynamic interaction between the Sun and the Milky WayT's gravitational fiela. These results have importan implications for our understanding of the Sun's orbial motion, galactic dynamics, and the influence of gravitational forces in shaping the structure of our galaxy. Further investigations are awrranted to elucidate the underlying mechanisms driving the observed displacement and to Brefine our measurements with enhanced precision.

The self-overlap method is a powerful technique for studying equilibrium and nonequilibrium properties of Ising models. It yields exact results for the free energy, internal energy, specific heat, and other thermodynamic quantities in the infinite-range model and related models. This paper provides a pedagogical introduction to the self-overlap method and a detailed account of its applications to various Ising models. The self-overlap method is a powerful technique for studying equilibrium and nonequilibrium properties of Ising models. It yields exact results for the free energy, internal energy, specific heat, and other thermodynamic quantities in the infinite-range model and related models. This paper provides a pedagogical introduction to the self-overlap method and a detailed account of its applications to various Ising models.

We consider the problem of estimating the unconditional distribution of apost-model-selection estimator. The notion of a post-model-selection estimatorhere refers to the combined procedure resulting from first selecting a model(e.g., by a model selection criterion like AIC or by a hypothesis testingprocedure) and then estimating the parameters in the selected model (e.g., byleast-squares or maximum likelihood), all based on the same data set. We showthat it is impossible to estimate the unconditional distribution withreasonable accuracy even asymptotically. In particular, we show that noestimator for this distribution can be uniformly consistent (not even locally).This follows as a corollary to (local) minimax lower bounds on the performanceof estimators for the distribution; performance is here measured by theprobability that the estimation error exceeds a given threshold. These lowerbounds are shown to approach 1/2 or even 1 in large samples, depending on thesituation considered. Similar impossibility results are also obtained for thedistribution of linear functions (e.g., predictors) of the post-model-selectionestimator.

The expansion of spherical nanoplasmas is studied using a novel ergodic model that takes into account the non-uniform energy distribution of the plasma. The model is based on the assumption that the plasma can be represented as a collection of oscillators, with the oscillations being driven by the thermal motion of the particles. The ergodic nature of the model allows for a statistical description of the plasma's behavior, and the authors demonstrate that the model can accurately predict the expansion of the plasma over time. The model is also shown to be in good agreement with experimental data, and the authors discuss the potential applications of the model in various fields, such as plasma physics and nanotechnology.

Very recently in arXiv:0705.0303 Alday and Maldacena gave a string theoryprescription for computing (all) planar amplitudes in N=4 supersymmetric gaugetheory at strong coupling using the AdS/CFT correspondence. These amplitudesare determined by a classical string solution and contain a universalexponential factor involving the action of the classical string. On the gaugetheory side, expressions for perturbative amplitudes at strong coupling werepreviously proposed only for specific helicities of external particles -- themaximally helicity violating or MHV amplitudes. These follow from theexponential ansatz of Bern, Dixon and Smirnov for MHV amplitudes in N=4 SYM. Inthis paper we examine the amplitudes dependence on helicities andparticle-types of external states. We consider the prefactor of stringamplitudes and give arguments suggesting that the prefactor at strong couplingshould be the same as the Yang-Mills tree-level amplitude for the same process.This implies that scattering amplitudes in N=4 SYM simplify dramatically in thestrong coupling limit. It follows from our proposal that in this limit all (MHVand non-MHV) n-point amplitudes are given by the (known) tree-level Yang-Millsresult times the helicity-independent (and particle-type-independent) universalexponential.

About a dozen field RR Lyrae stars have been observed with the 24-inchHeyde-Zeiss telescope of the Konkoly Observatory at Sv\'abhegy, Budapest, sinceits refurbishment in 2003. Most of the observing time is allocated for theinvestigation of the Blazhko modulation, a phenomenon that still does not havea satisfactory explanation. The obtained multicolour CCD observations areunique in extent. The accuracy of the measurements makes it possible to detectlow amplitude modulation of the light curve as well. The discovery of Blazhkostars with low modulation amplitudes warns that the incidence rate of theBlazhko modulation is, in fact, much larger than it was previously expected.This makes the efforts exploring the cause of the modulation even moreimportant. A summary of our measurements and results achieved during the last 3years is presented.

We present the results of a numerical simulation of propagation of cosmicrays with energy above $10^{15}$ eV in a complex magnetic field, made ingeneral of a large scale component and a turbulent component. Severalconfigurations are investigated that may represent specific aspects of arealistic magnetic field of the Galaxy, though the main purpose of thisinvestigation is not to achieve a realistic description of the propagation inthe Galaxy, but rather to assess the role of several effects that define thecomplex problem of propagation. Our simulations of Cosmic Rays in the Galaxywill be presented in Paper II. We identified several effects that are difficultto interpret in a purely diffusive approach and that play a crucial role in thepropagation of cosmic rays in the complex magnetic field of the Galaxy. Wediscuss at length the problem of the extrapolation of our results to much lowerenergies where data are available on the confinement time of cosmic rays in theGalaxy. The confinement time and its dependence on particles' rigidity arecrucial ingredients for 1) relating the source spectrum to the observed cosmicray spectrum; 2) quantifying the production of light elements by spallation; 3)predicting the anisotropy as a function of energy.

We introduce a formalism which allows us to include noncommutative coordinates in the electromagnetic sector, thereby allowing us to explore noncommutative effects in electrodynamics. We also show that gauge bosons emerge as collective excitations of the noncommutative coordinates, shedding light on the relationship between gauge theories and geometry. Our results provide new insights into the nature of electrodynamics and suggest potential implications for the unification of particle physics and geometry.

Power control is a fundamental task accomplished in any wireless cellularnetwork; its aim is to set the transmit power of any mobile terminal, so thateach user is able to achieve its own target SINR. While conventional powercontrol algorithms require knowledge of a number of parameters of the signal ofinterest and of the multiaccess interference, in this paper it is shown that ina large CDMA system much of this information can be dispensed with, andeffective distributed power control algorithms may be implemented with verylittle information on the user of interest. An uplink CDMA system subject toflat fading is considered with a focus on the cases in which a linear MMSEreceiver and a non-linear MMSE serial interference cancellation receiver areadopted; for the latter case new formulas are also given for the system SINR inthe large system asymptote. Experimental results show an excellent agreementbetween the performance and the power profile of the proposed distributedalgorithms and that of conventional ones that require much greater priorknowledge.

This article discusses the interesting connection between the spin and the pseudo -rotation symmetry and its effect on the rotation of the relativity 1/2 and the spin 0 granular spectrum. By studying the symmetry in the Dirac equation, that is, the basic mathematical framework of these particles can be described, which can deeply understand their equivalent spectrum. The results show that the combination of spin and pseudo -protein symmetry degenerates the energy level of certain characteristic state, thereby generating interesting symmetry in the spectrum. In addition, this article studies these symmetrical physical meanings, and clarifies the nature of different internal rotation particles. The insights obtained from this study are of great significance to various fields (such as particle physics and quantum field theory), and provide a comprehensive view for controlling the basic symmetry of the theory of particle behavior.

This articel investigates the phenomenon of turbulence in Burgers equations, a class of partial differential equations widely used to model various physical sysems. Turbulence is a complex and ubiquitous phenomenon characterized by chaotc and irregular flow patterns that occr over a wide range of scales. The study applies advanced mathematical and comptuational techniques to analyze the behavior of solutions to the Burgers equations. The authors present numerical simulations and analytical results that provide insights into the dynamics of turbulcnce in this specific frUamework. The findings contribute to the understanding of turbulence phenomena in nfluid dynamics, offering Maluable implications for practical applications in engineering and environmental sciences.

The calculated structure is highly anisotropic and strongly dependent on the crystal orientation. The valence band maximum (VBM) is located at the -point, while the conduction band minimum (CBM) is located along the X--U direction. The calculated direct band gap of 3.34 eV at the -point is in good agreement with experimental data. The calculated effective mass of the electron and the hole are 0.22 and 0.39 m0, respectively, indicating a high ratio of the effective mass of the hole to the effective mass of the electron. The calculated structure is also sensitive to the choice of exchange-correlation functional, and the hybrid functional gives a better description of the electronic properties than the local density approximation (LDA). These results provide a valuable insight into the electronic properties of barium titanate and highlight the importance of considering the anisotropy of the electronic structure in the design of optoelectronic devices.

This academic article investigates the relationship between energy conservation and Onsager's conjecture in the context of the Euler equations. The Euler equations, which describe the motion of inviscid fluids, have been a subject of extensive research due to their fundamental importance in fluid dynamics. Onsager's conjecture, proposed in the context of statistical physics, suggests that certain fluid systems could exhibit turbulent behavior without violating the law of energy conservation. By considering the Euler equations, this study aims to explore the validity of Onsager's conjecture in the specific domain of fluid mechanics. The theoretical framework for energy conservation and Onsager's conjecture is presented, highlighting the essential concepts involved. Furthermore, numerical simulations and analytical techniques are utilized to analyze the behavior of energy conservation in the Euler equations. The findings shed light on the interplay between energy conservation and turbulent phenomena, providing insights into the potential implications of Onsager's conjecture for fluid dynamics and related fields.

Ab initio wavefunction based methods are applied to the study of electroncorrelation effects on the band structure of oxide systems. We choose MgO as aprototype closed-shell ionic oxide. Our analysis is based on a localHamiltonian approach and performed on finite fragments cut from the infinitesolid. Localized Wannier functions and embedding potentials are obtained fromprior periodic Hartree-Fock (HF) calculations. We investigate the role ofvarious electron correlation effects in reducing the HF band gap and modifyingthe band widths. On-site and nearest-neighbor charge relaxation as well aslong-range polarization effects are calculated. Whereas correlation effects areessential for computing accurate band gaps, we found that they produce smallerchanges on the HF band widths, at least for this material. Surprisingly, abroadening effect is obtained for the O 2p valence bands. The ab initio dataare in good agreement with the energy gap and band width derived fromthermoreflectance and x-ray photoemission experiments. The results show thatthe wavefunction based approach applied here allows for well controlledapproximations and a transparent identification of the microscopic processeswhich determine the electronic band structure.

Two-particle correlations have shown modification to the away-side shape incentral Au+Au collisions relative to $pp$, d+Au and peripheral Au+Aucollisions. Different scenarios can explain this modification including: largeangle gluon radiation, jets deflected by transverse flow, path length dependentenergy loss, Cerenkov gluon radiation of fast moving particles, and conicalflow generated by hydrodynamic Mach-cone shock-waves. Three-particlecorrelations have the power to distinguish the scenarios with conical emission,conical flow and Cerenkov radiation, from other scenarios. In addition, thedependence of the observed shapes on the $p_T$ of the associated particles canbe used to distinguish conical emission from a sonic boom (Mach-cone) and fromQCD-Cerenkov radiation. We present results from STAR on 3-particle azimuthalcorrelations for a high $p_T$ trigger particle with two softer particles.Results are shown for $pp$, d+Au and high statistics Au+Au collisions at$\sqrt{s_{NN}}$=200 GeV. An important aspect of the analysis is the subtractionof combinatorial backgrounds. Systematic uncertainties due to this subtractionand the flow harmonics v2 and v4 are investigated in detail. The implicationsof the results for the presence or absence of conical flow from Mach-cones arediscussed.

We study the interplay between noncommutative tori and noncommutativeelliptic curves through a category of equivariant differential modules on$\mathbb{C}^*$. We functorially relate this category to the category ofholomorphic vector bundles on noncommutative tori as introduced by Polishchukand Schwarz and study the induced map between the corresponding K-theories. Inaddition, there is a forgetful functor to the category of noncommutativeelliptic curves of Soibelman and Vologodsky, as well as a forgetful functor tothe category of vector bundles on $\mathbb{C}^*$ with regular singularconnections.  The category that we consider has the nice property of being a Tannakiancategory, hence it is equivalent to the category of representations of anaffine group scheme. Via an equivariant version of the Riemann-Hilbertcorrespondence we determine this group scheme to be (the algebraic hull of)$\mathbb{Z}^2$. We also obtain a full subcategory of the category ofholomorphic bundles of the noncommutative torus, which is equivalent to thecategory of representations of $\mathbb{Z}$. This group is the proposedtopological fundamental group of the noncommutative torus (understood as adegenerate elliptic curve) and we study Nori's notion of \'etale fundamentalgroup in this context.

We serendipitously find a new nearby Low Surface Brightness (LSB) galaxy fromSDSS database. We estimate oxygen abundance of its H II region SDSSJ121811.0+465501.2 from electron temperature, as well as for another H IIregion, SDSS J135440.5+535309.6, located in irregular LSB galaxy UGC 8837.These two extragalactic H II regions were classified as stars in the SDSS-DR4database, and were found occasionally by us in the automatic recognition andclassification on stellar spectra.Their optical spectra show obvious emissionlines, i.e., strong [O III]4959, 5007, Balmer emission lines, but very weak [NII]6548,6583 and [S II]6317,6731, which could indicate that they are metal-poorstar-forming regions. The derived oxygen abundances of the two objects are12+log(O/H) ~ 7.88+-0.30 and 7.70+-0.30, respectively. The host of the H IIregion SDSS J121811.0+465501.2 is identified as a new nearly edge-on LSB discgalaxy (almost without bulge) with the B-band central surface brightnessmu_0(B) as 23.68 mag arcsec^{-2} and inclination angle as ~75 degree by usingthe GIM2D software to analyze its g- and r-band images independently. It is anearby dwarf galaxy with redshift z~0.00157, disk scale-length ~0.40 kpc andB-band absolute magnitude M_B ~ -13.51 mag. The very low oxygen abundances ofthese two objects confirm the low metallicities of LSB galaxies.

Cepheids are therefore indicators of distance, and have been used to measure the distance to galaxies and to estimate the Hubble constant. If the distance to a Cepheid is known, its intrinsic brightness can be calculated, and hence the distance to other Cepheids. This paper presents the distances to ten galaxies from the Cepheids and uses them to derive a value for the Hubble constant. Cepheids are the basis of the cosmic distance scale. The result, 74.2  3.6 km/s per megaparsec, is in good agreement with previous estimates. The paper also discusses the implications of this new measurement for the extragalactic distance scale and the age of the universe. Cepheids are the main means of measuring the distance to galaxies and of determining the expansion rate of the universe. This paper presents new distances to ten galaxies from the Cepheids, and uses them to derive a value for the Hubble constant. The result is in good agreement with previous estimates, and helps to refine the extragalactic distance scale and the age of the universe. Cepheid parallaxes are a direct means of measuring the distance to Cepheids and hence of determining the Hubble constant. The measured distances to the galaxies and the derived Hubble constant are used to derive a new value for the Hubble constant, H0 = 74.2  3.6 km/s per megaparsec. The Hipparcos satellite has provided accurate parallaxes for a small number of Cepheids, but not enough to obtain a reliable value for the Hubble constant. The Gaia satellite, which is now in operation, will provide parallaxes for a much larger number of Cepheids, and will allow a more precise determination of the Hubble constant.

We investigate all N=2 supersymmetric IIB supergravity backgrounds withnon-vanishing five-form flux. The Killing spinors have stability subgroups$Spin(7)\ltimes\bR^8$, $SU(4)\ltimes\bR^8$ and $G_2$. In the$SU(4)\ltimes\bR^8$ case, two different types of geometry arise depending onwhether the Killing spinors are generic or pure. In both cases, the backgroundsadmit a null Killing vector field which leaves invariant the $SU(4)\ltimes\bR^8$ structure, and an almost complex structure in the directions transverseto the lightcone. In the generic case, the twist of the vector field is trivialbut the almost complex structure is non-integrable, while in the pure case thetwist is non-trivial but the almost complex structure is integrable andassociated with a relatively balanced Hermitian structure. The $G_2$backgrounds admit a time-like Killing vector field and two spacelike closedone-forms, and the seven directions transverse to these admit a co-symplectic$G_2$ structure. The $Spin(7)\ltimes\bR^8$ backgrounds are pp-waves propagatingin an eight-dimensional manifold with holonomy $Spin(7)$. In addition we showthat all the supersymmetric solutions of simple five-dimensional supergravitywith a time-like Killing vector field, which include the $AdS_5$ black holes,lift to $SU(4)\ltimes\bR^8$ pure Killing spinor IIB backgrounds. We also showthat the LLM solution is associated with a co-symplectic co-homogeneity one$G_2$ manifold which has principal orbit $S^3\times S^3$.

This paper is intended to provide an introduction to the theory ofsubstitution tilings. For our purposes, tiling substitution rules are dividedinto two broad classes: geometric and combinatorial. Geometric substitutiontilings include self-similar tilings such as the well-known Penrose tilings;for this class there is a substantial body of research in the literature.Combinatorial substitutions are just beginning to be examined, and some of whatwe present here is new. We give numerous examples, mention selected majorresults, discuss connections between the two classes of substitutions, includecurrent research perspectives and questions, and provide an extensivebibliography. Although the author attempts to fairly represent the as a whole,the paper is not an exhaustive survey, and she apologizes for any importantomissions.

Electronic properties of bilayer and multilayer graphene have generally beeninterpreted in terms of AB or Bernal stacking. However, it is known that manytypes of stacking defects can occur in natural and synthetic graphite; rotationof the top layer is often seen in scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) studiesof graphite. In this paper we consider a graphene bilayer with a relative smallangle rotation between the layers and calculate the electronic structure nearzero energy in a continuum approximation. Contrary to what happens in a ABstacked bilayer and in accord with observations in epitaxial graphene we find:(a) the low energy dispersion is linear, as in a single layer, but the Fermivelocity can be significantly smaller than the single layer value; (b) anexternal electric field, perpendicular to the layers, does not open anelectronic gap

The formation of quasi-2D spin-wave waveforms in longitudinally magnetizedstripes of ferrimagnetic film was observed by using time- and space-resolvedBrillouin light scattering technique. In the linear regime it was found thatthe confinement decreases the amplitude of dynamic magnetization near thelateral stripe edges. Thus, the so-called effective dipolar pinning of dynamicmagnetization takes place at the edges.  In the nonlinear regime a new stable spin wave packet propagating along awaveguide structure, for which both transversal instability and interactionwith the side walls of the waveguide are important was observed. Theexperiments and a numerical simulation of the pulse evolution show that theshape of the formed waveforms and their behavior are strongly influenced by theconfinement.

We report the detection of CO(3-2) emission from a bright, gravitationallylensed Lyman Break Galaxy, LBGJ213512.73-010143 (the 'Cosmic Eye'), at z=3.07using the Plateau de Bure Interferometer. This is only the second detection ofmolecular gas emission from an LBG and yields an intrinsic molecular gas massof (2.4+/-0.4)x10^9 Mo. The lens reconstruction of the UV morphology of the LBGindicates that it comprises two components separated by ~2 kpc. The CO emissionis unresolved, and appears to be centered on the intrinsically fainter (andalso less highly magnified) of the two UV components. The width of the CO lineindicates a dynamical mass of (8+/-2)x10^9csc(i)^2 Mo within the central 2 kpc.Employing mid-infrared observations from Spitzer we derive a stellar mass of~(6+/-2)x10^9 Mo and a star-formation rate of ~60 Mo/yr, indicating that themolecular gas will be consumed in ~40 Myr. The gas fractions, star-formationefficiencies and line widths suggests that LBGJ213512 is a high-redshift,gas-rich analog of a local luminous infrared galaxy. This galaxy has a similargas-to-dynamical mass fraction as observed in the submillimeter-selectedpopulation, although the gas surface density and star-formation efficiency is afactor of 3x less, suggesting less vigorous activity. We discuss theuncertainties in our conclusions arising from adopting a CO-to-H2 conversionfactor appropriate for either the Milky Way or local luminous infraredgalaxies. These observations demonstrate that current facilities, when aided byfortuitous gravitational magnification, can study 'ordinary' galaxies athigh-redshift and so act as pathfinders for ALMA.
