We report calculated substantial enhancement of the second harmonicgeneration (SHG) in cuprous oxide crystals resonantly hybridized with anappropriate organic material (DCM2:CA:PS 'solid-state solvent'). The quadrupoleorigin of the inorganic part of the quadrupole-dipole hybrid provides inversionsymmetry breaking and the organic part contributes to the oscillator strengthof the hybrid. We show that the enhancement of the SHG, compared to bulkcuprous oxide crystal, is proportional to the ratio of the DCM2 dipole momentand the effective dipole moment of the quadrupole transitions in the cuprousoxide. It is also inversely proportional to the line-width of the hybrid andbulk excitons. The enhancement may be regulated by adjusting the organic blend(mutual concentration of the DCM2 and CA part of the solvent) and pumpingconditions(varying the angle of incidence in case of optical pumping orpopulating the minimum of the lower branch of the hybrid in case of electricalpumping).

**Abstract**: In this paper, we investigate the signatures of a heavy Z-prime gauge boson in the framework of an extra U(1) superstring inspired model. We perform a detailed analysis of the renormalization group equations (RGEs) and calculate the one-loop beta functions for all the couplings in the model. We find that the beta functions are finite and satisfy the necessary consistency conditions. We also study the stability of the model under radiative corrections and show that it is stable up to the Planck scale. Our results provide important insights into the phenomenology of this model and pave the way for further studies of its implications for physics beyond the Standard Model.

This article is a continuation of a paper of the first author \cite{F} aboutcomplex structures on real Banach spaces. We define a notion of even infinitedimensional real Banach space, and prove that there exist even spaces,including HI or unconditional examples from \cite{F} and $C(K)$ examples due toPlebanek \cite{P}. We extend results of \cite{F} relating the set of complexstructures up to isomorphism on a real space to a group associated toinessential operators on that space, and give characterizations of even spacesin terms of this group. We also generalize results of \cite{F} about totallyincomparable complex structures to essentially incomparable complex structures,while showing that the complex version of a space defined by S. Argyros and A.Manoussakis \cite{AM} provide examples of essentially incomparable complexstructures which are not totally incomparable.

We consider a single ion confined in a trap under radiation of two travelingwaves of lasers. In the strong-excitation regime and without the restriction ofLamb-Dicke limit, the Hamiltonian of the system is similar to a drivingJaynes-Cummings model without rotating wave approximation (RWA). The approachwe developed enables us to present a complete eigensolutions, which makes itavailable to compare with the solutions under the RWA. We find that, the groundstate in our non-RWA solution is energically lower than the counterpart underthe RWA. If we have the ion in the ground state, it is equivalent to a spindependent force on the trapped ion. Discussion is made for the differencebetween the solutions with and without the RWA, and for the relevantexperimental test, as well as for the possible application in quantuminformation processing.

The phenomenon of gravitational waves (GWs) has openings up new avenues of exploration in astrophysics and cosmology. However, the circulated of GWs through space is not free from distortion effects. In this study, we investigate the distortion of GW packets due to their self-gravity, which puts from the gravitational interactions within the pack itself. We shaping a theoretical framework based on general relativity and apply it to model the execute of GW packets under the influence of self-gravity. Our analysis reveals that self-gravity can induce significant alter in the shape and dynamics of GW packets, leading to observable changes in their waveforms and spread characteristics. We identifying that the magnitude of this distortion ramifications is dependent on the properties of the GW packet, such as its energy, frequency, and size. Understanding the repercussions of self-gravity on GW propagation is crucial for accurate interpretation of gravitational-wave observations and can render further views into the nature of gravity and the dynamics of astrophysical systems.

In this study, we reevaluate the impact of nearby supernova remnants (SNRs) on the local cosmic ray (CR) environment. Using a combination of observational data and theoretical models, we investigate the changes in the CR spectra and secondary-to-primary ratios resulting from the presence of nearby SNRs. Our analysis reveals that the effects of nearby SNRs on the CRs are significant, with noticeable variations in the primary fluxes and a possible impact on the secondary-to-primary ratios. We also explore the role of particle release times from SNRs and find that a constant CR source power throughout the Galaxy leads to appreciable effects on the CRs at the Earth. Our study provides new insights into the complex interplay between SNRs and CRs, shedding light on the processes that shape the local CR environment.

We present deep emission-line imaging taken with the SOAR Optical ImagingCamera of the brightest cluster galaxy (BCG) in the nearby (z=0.035) X-raycluster 2A0335+096. We analyze long-slit optical spectroscopy, archival VLA,Chandra X-ray, and XMM UV data. 2A0335+096 is a bright, cool-core X-raycluster, once known as a cooling flow. Within the highly disturbed corerevealed by Chandra X-ray observations, 2A0335+096 hosts a highly structuredoptical emission-line system. The redshift of the companion is within 100 km/sof the BCG and has certainly interacted with the BCG, and is likely bound toit. The comparison of optical and radio images shows curved filaments inH-alpha emission surrounding the resolved radio source. The velocity structureof the emission-line bar between the BCG nucleus and the companion galaxyprovides strong evidence for an interaction between the two in the last ~50Myrs. The age of the radio source is similar to the interaction time, so thisinteraction may have provoked an episode of radio activity. We estimate a starformation rate of >7 solar mass/yr based on the Halpha and archival UV data, arate similar to, but somewhat lower than, the revised X-ray cooling rate of10-30 solar masses/year estimated from XMM spectra by Peterson & workers. TheHalpha nebula is limited to a region of high X-ray surface brightness and coolX-ray temperature. The detailed structures of H-alpha and X-ray gas differ. Thepeak of the X-ray emission is not the peak of H-alpha emission, nor does it liein the BCG. The estimated age of the radio lobes and their interaction with theoptical emission-line gas, the estimated timescale for depletion andaccumulation of cold gas, and the dynamical time in the system are all similar,suggesting a common trigger mechanism.

This study presents non-resonant and resonant X-ray scattering investigations on the muXtiferroic material TbMn2O5. Multiferroic materials exhibit coupling between electric and magnetic order parameers, making them potential candidates for advanced technological applications. The non-resonant -Xray scattering measurements were performed at various temperatures to explore the structural changes and magntic ordering in TbMn2O5. he resonant X-ray scattering experiments, utilizing the specific absorption edgUes of Tb and Mn, provided insights into the local electronic and magnetic properties. The combined results offer a comprehensive uuderstanding of the structural and magnetic behavior of bMn2O5, crucial for unraveling its multiferroic properties and guiding future device design.

We construct general 2-charge D1-D5 horizon-free non-singular solutions ofIIB supergravity on T^4 and K3 describing fuzzballs with excitations in theinternal manifold; these excitations are characterized by arbitrary curves. Thesolutions are obtained via dualities from F1-P solutions of heterotic and typeIIB on T^4 for the K3 and T^4 cases, respectively. We compute the holographicdata encoded in these solutions, and show that the internal excitations arecaptured by vevs of chiral primaries associated with the middle cohomology ofT^4 or K3. We argue that each geometry is dual to a specific superposition of Rground states determined in terms of the Fourier coefficients of the curvesdefining the supergravity solution. We compute vevs of chiral primariesassociated with the middle cohomology and show that they indeed acquire vevs inthe superpositions corresponding to fuzzballs with internal excitations, inaccordance with the holographic results. We also address the question ofwhether the fuzzball program can be implemented consistently withinsupergravity.

The optical photometric variability of BL Lacertae is studied in the long term based on the astronomical synoptic observations database. The results show that the object is always in a high state during the past 10 years. The flux probability distribution can be characterized by the generalized Gaussian function, which presents a high kurtosis and asymmetry, indicating significant departure from Gaussian distribution. The structure function analysis indicates that the optical variability presents a strong short-term variability and weak long-term variability, and the variability amplitude increases with the increasing time scales. The color becomes redder when the object is brighter, which may be caused by the synchrotron self-absorption effect in the jet.

Deformation Theory is a natural generalization of Lie Theory, from Lie groupsand their linearization, Lie algebras, to differential graded Lie algebras andtheir higher order deformations, quantum groups.  The article focuses on two basic constructions of deformation theory: theuniversal solution of Maurer-Cartan Equation (MCE), which plays the role of theexponential of Lie Theory, and its inverse, the Kuranishi functor, as thelogarithm.  The deformation functor is the gauge reduction of MCE, corresponding to aHodge decomposition associated to the strong deformation retract data.  The above comparison with Lie Theory leads to a better understanding ofDeformation Theory and its applications, e.g. the relation between quantizationand Connes-Kreimer renormalization, quantum doubles and Birkhoff decomposition.

We point out that resonance saturation in QCD can be understood in thelarge-Nc limit from the mathematical theory of Pade Approximants to meromorphicfunctions. These approximants are rational functions which encompass anysaturation with a finite number of resonances as a particular example,explaining several results which have appeared in the literature. We review themain properties of Pade Approximants with the help of a toy model for the<VV-AA> two-point correlator, paying particular attention to the relationshipamong the Chiral Expansion, the Operator Product Expansion and the resonancespectrum. In passing, we also comment on an old proposal made by Migdal in 1977which has recently attracted much attention in the context of AdS/QCD models.Finally, we apply the simplest Pade Approximant to the <VV-AA> correlator inthe real case of QCD. The general conclusion is that a rational approximant mayreliably describe a Green's function in the Euclidean, but the same is not truein the Minkowski regime due to the appearance of unphysical poles and/orresidues.

We report on a search for gravitational waves from the coalescence of compactbinaries during the third and fourth LIGO science runs. The search focused ongravitational waves generated during the inspiral phase of the binaryevolution. In our analysis, we considered three categories of compact binarysystems, ordered by mass: (i) primordial black hole binaries with masses in therange 0.35 M(sun) < m1, m2 < 1.0 M(sun), (ii) binary neutron stars with massesin the range 1.0 M(sun) < m1, m2 < 3.0 M(sun), and (iii) binary black holeswith masses in the range 3.0 M(sun)< m1, m2 < m_(max) with the additionalconstraint m1+ m2 < m_(max), where m_(max) was set to 40.0 M(sun) and 80.0M(sun) in the third and fourth science runs, respectively. Although thedetectors could probe to distances as far as tens of Mpc, no gravitational-wavesignals were identified in the 1364 hours of data we analyzed. Assuming abinary population with a Gaussian distribution around 0.75-0.75 M(sun), 1.4-1.4M(sun), and 5.0-5.0 M(sun), we derived 90%-confidence upper limit rates of 4.9yr^(-1) L10^(-1) for primordial black hole binaries, 1.2 yr^(-1) L10^(-1) forbinary neutron stars, and 0.5 yr^(-1) L10^(-1) for stellar mass binary blackholes, where L10 is 10^(10) times the blue light luminosity of the Sun.

We focus on the first part of our work, which reveals the azimuthal distribution of particles produced in high-energy collisions. By using advanced event-by-event simulations and realistic initial conditions, we reveal intriguing patterns in the azimuthal distributions, which lead to significant insights into the underlying dynamics of the collisions. Our results highlight the presence of collective phenomena such as flow and fluctuations in the azimuthal distributions, which indicate the relevance of hydrodynamic and transport models for understanding the collision dynamics. This work paves the way for future investigations of the azimuthal structure of collisions and its implications for fundamental physics and heavy-ion experiments.

Deep space laser ranging missions like ASTROD I (Single-SpacecraftAstrodynamical Space Test of Relativity using Optical Devices) and ASTROD,together with astrometry missions like GAIA and LATOR will be able to testrelativistic gravity to an unprecedented level of accuracy. More precisely,these missions will enable us to test relativistic gravity to$10^{-7}-10^{-9}$, and will require 2nd post-Newtonian approximation ofrelevant theories of gravity. The first post-Newtonian approximation is validto $10^{-6}$ and the second post-Newtonian is valid to $10^{-12}$ in the solarsystem. The scalar-tensor theory is widely discussed and used in tests ofrelativistic gravity, especially after the interests in inflation, cosmologicalconstant and dark energy in cosmology. In the Lagrangian, intermediate-rangegravity term has a similar form as cosmological term. Here we present the fullsecond post-Newtonian approximation of the scalar-tensor theory includingviable examples of intermediate-range gravity. We use Chandrasekhar's approachto derive the metric coefficients and the equation of the hydrodynamicsgoverning a perfect fluid in the 2nd post-Newtonian approximation inscalar-tensor theory; all terms inclusive of $O(c^{-4})$ are retainedconsistently in the equation of motion.

**Abstract**This study investigates the connection between host galaxy bulge properties and supermassive black hole (SMBH) mass. We analyze a sample of 100 nearby galaxies with high-quality data on both their bulges and SMBHs. We find that the bulge mass, size, and Sérsic index are all strongly correlated with SMBH mass. These correlations are consistent with the predictions of theoretical models that suggest that SMBHs and their host bulges grow in tandem. Our results provide new insights into the coevolution of SMBHs and their host galaxies.

In this study, we inve stigate the signal for space-time noncommutativity within the framework of the theta-expanded Non-Commutative Standard Model (NCSM) by analyzinɡ the decay process of the Z boson into two photons, Z -> gamma gamma. The NCSM is a renormalizable gauge theory that incorporates noncоmmutative geometry into the spacetime sector. We probe the effects of sрace-time noncommutatіvity at the tree-level vertex of the Z -> gamma gamma decay, considering the leading order support arising from the guiding principles of the NCSM. Our analysis reveals significant deviations from the predictions of the Switchboard Model in the kinematic distributions and polarizatіon properties of the decay products. We calculate the decay rate and analyze the correѕponding differential cross-sections, fiding distinctive features that can serve as potential signatures of space-time noncommutativity. These results provide valuabⅼe insigհts into the experimental search for noncommutative effects in high-energy praticle interactions and underline the importance of studying the phenomenology of the theta-expanded NCSM in the context of meticulous physics at current and future collider experiments.

The methods considered are the Bayes factor, the Watanabe–Akaike information criterion, and Bayesian cross-validation. The study finds that the Bayes factor and the Watanabe–Akaike information criterion are good at selecting the correct model, while Bayesian cross-validation shows promise in certain situations. The results provide useful insights for gravitational wave astronomy, and recommend the use of multiple model selection techniques for optimum results. However, the study also highlights the limitations of these methods, especially when dealing with complex waveforms or low signal-to-noise ratios. Future studies can build on these results to improve model selection methods and increase the accuracy of gravitational wave astronomy observations.

We investigate the dynamics of single polymers under extreme confinement conditions. By employing advanced simulation techniques, we analyze the behavior of polymers trapped between two immovable surfaces. The polymer's motion and conformational changes are examined as a function of the degree of confinement and the polymer length. We find that under extreme confinement, the polymer exhibits highly restricted dynamics, with significantly reduced translational and rotational motion. Moreover, the polymer's conformation becomes more elongated and stretched compared to its free state. We also observe the occurrence of localized chain folding and entanglement events, leading to complex and transient polymer configurations. Our findings provide valuable insights into the fundamental dynamics of polymers in confined environments and have implications for various fields, including nanotechnology, biophysics, and materials science.

In this study, we used data from Nustar and SWIFT observations to study the 200 KEV X -ray spectrum of Galaxies NGC 2992 and NGC 3081. Our analysis shows that the two galaxies show complex X -ray spectrum, which helps observe the launch. We have determined a soft thermal ingredient, which may originate from the heat in the interstellar media and the harder power component. This may be caused by synchronous radiation of supernova residues, or scattered from cosmic radio electrons. We also detected the iron K shell line in the two galaxies, indicating that there is an environment rich in metal. 6.4 KEV iron wires were found in NGC 2992 more wider than NGC 3081, which shows that the former's iron -Fengdu may be higher. Our results provide new insights for the physical conditions and elemental abundance of these two galaxies, and emphasize the importance of a wide range of X -ray spectra in understanding the complexity of galaxy evolution.

This article investigates the occurrence and characteristics of exceedingly massive stars within galaxies at high redshift. The study of high-redshift galaxies is essential for comprehending the early universe, and an inclusive examination of very massive stars is imperative for a comprehensive understanding of stellar evolution and cosmic chemical enrichment. To investigate the existence and properties of these stars, we combine observational data with theoretical models. Our analysis discerns the identification of remarkably luminous entities within high-redshift galaxies, providing evidence for the presence of very massive stars. By scrutinizing observational features, such as spectral characteristics and flux measurements, we deduce the physical properties of these stars, including their masses and ages. Furthermore, we explore the implications of these very massive stars on galaxy evolution and the overall history of cosmic star formation. The findings of this study significantly contribute to our comprehension of the early universe and provide insights into the role of very massive stars in shaping the properties and evolution of galaxies at high redshift. Additional investigations are necessary to elucidate the mechanisms of formation and evolutionary pathways of these extraordinary stellar entities.

We describe results of a narrow band search for ultra-strong emission linegalaxies (USELs) with EW(H beta) > 30 A. 542 candidate galaxies are found in ahalf square degree survey using two ~100 Angstrom 8150 A and 9140 A filterswith Subaru/SuprimeCam. Followup spectroscopy for randomly selected objects inthe sample with KeckII/DEIMOS shows they consist of [OIII] 5007, [OII] 3727,and H alpha selected strong-emission line galaxies at intermediate redshifts (z< 1), and Ly alpha emitting galaxies at high-redshift (z >> 5). We determinethe H beta luminosity functions and the star formation density of the USELs,which is 5-10% of the value found from ultraviolet continuum objects at z=0-1,suggesting they correspond to a major epoch in galaxy formation at theseredshifts. Many USELs show the temperature-sensitive [OIII] 4363 auroral linesand about a dozen have oxygen abundances characteristic of eXtremely Metal PoorGalaxies (XMPGs). These XMPGs are the most distant known today. Our high yieldrate of XMPGs suggests this is a powerful method for finding such populations.The lowest metallicity measured in our sample is 12+log(O/H) = 7.06(6.78-7.44), close to the minimum metallicity found in local galaxies. Theluminosities, metallicities and star formation rates of USELs are consistentwith the strong emitters being start-up intermediate mass galaxies and suggestthat galaxies are still forming in relatively chemically pristine sites at z <1.

Towards Informative Statistical Flow InversionStatistical flow inversion aims to invert a generative model to generate samples that match specific statistics, which finds broad applications in various tasks, such as domain adaptation and data augmentation. Existing methods either focus on minimizing the discrepancy between the generated and target distributions or enforcing specific properties (e.g., moments) of the generated samples. However, they often fail to achieve both objectives simultaneously, leading to sub-optimal solutions. To address this issue, we propose an informative statistical flow inversion method that explicitly incorporates the target statistics into the inversion process. Specifically, we utilize a novel information-theoretic regularization term that measures the mutual information between the generated samples and the target statistics. This regularization term encourages the generated samples to not only match the target statistics but also contain informative features that are discriminative for downstream tasks. Extensive experiments on various datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method over existing state-of-the-art approaches. Our code is available at https://github.com/VITA-Group/InvertibleFlow.

We study the connection between the appearance of a `metastable' behavior ofweakly chaotic orbits, characterized by a constant rate of increase of theTsallis q-entropy (Tsallis 1988), and the solutions of the variationalequations of motion for the same orbits. We demonstrate that the variationalequations yield transient solutions, lasting for long time intervals, duringwhich the length of deviation vectors of nearby orbits grows in time almost asa power-law. The associated power exponent can be simply related to theentropic exponent for which the q-entropy exhibits a constant rate of increase.This analysis leads to the definition of a new sensitive indicatordistinguishing regular from weakly chaotic orbits, that we call `Average PowerLaw Exponent' (APLE). We compare the APLE with other established indicators ofthe literature. In particular, we give examples of application of the APLE ina) a thin separatrix layer of the standard map, b) the stickiness region aroundan island of stability in the same map, and c) the web of resonances of a 4Dsymplectic map. In all these cases we identify weakly chaotic orbits exhibitingthe `metastable' behavior associated with the Tsallis q-entropy.

Recent studies of clustering in light nuclei with an initial energy above 1 AGeV in nuclear treack emulsion are overviewed. The results of investigations ofthe relativistic $^9$Be nuclei fragmentation in emulsion, which entails theproduction of He fragments, are presented. It is shown that most preciseangular measurements provided by this technique play a crucial role in therestoration of the excitation spectrum of the $\alpha$ particle sysytem. Inperipheral interactions $^9$Be nuclei are dissociated practically totallythrough the 0$^+$ and 2$^+$ states of the $^8$Be nucleus.  The results of investigations of the dissociation of a $^{14}$N nucleus ofmomentum 2.86 A GeV/c in emulsion are presented as example of more complicatedsystem. The momentum and correlation characteristics of $\alpha$ particles forthe $^{14}$N$\to3\alpha+X$ channel in the laboratory system and the restsystems of 3$\alpha$ particles were considered in detail.  Topology of charged fragments produced in peripheral relativisticdissociation of radioactive $^8$B, $^7$Be nuclei in emulsion is studied.

We explore the main physical processes which potentially affect thetopological signal in the Cosmic Microwave Background (CMB) for a range oftoroidal universes. We consider specifically reionisation, the integratedSachs-Wolfe (ISW) effect, the size of the causal horizon, topological defectsand primordial gravitational waves. We use three estimators: the informationcontent, the S/N statistic and the Bayesian evidence. While reionisation hasnearly no effect on the estimators, we show that taking into account the ISWstrongly decreases our ability to detect the topological signal. We also studythe impact of varying the relevant cosmological parameters within the 2 sigmaranges allowed by present data. We find that only Omega_Lambda, whichinfluences both ISW and the size of the causal horizon, significantly altersthe detection for all three estimators considered here.

**Abstract**:In this paperwork, we examining the possibility of detecting gravitational radiation from binary black hole MACHOs (BBH MACHOs) in the Galactic halo. We use a Monte Carlo simulation to resulted a population of BBH MACHOs and calculate the gravitational unveiling strain produced by each binary. We then use a matched filtering modes to search for the gravitational wave signal from the population of BBH MACHOs in data from the Advanced LIGO detector. We excavated that Raising LIGO is sensitive to BBH MACHOs with total masses up to ~10^6 Msun and mass ratios down to ~10^-3. We also explores the implications of our results for the formation and evolution of BBH MACHOs.

Computed tomography (CT) is a medical іmaging technique that uses X-rays to create detailed images of the body. Traditional CT scanners use a rotating gantry to acquiⲅe multiple projections of the body, which are then used to reconstruct a three-ԁimensional image. However, this approach is limited by the amount of ⲅadiation exposure to the patient. In this paper, we propose a ոew CT image reconstruction algorithm that uses only two projections. This is achieved by exploiting the fact that the human body is approximately cylindrical in shae. Our algorithm uses a deep learning-based approach to lеarn the relationship between the two projections and the corresponding tհree-dimensional image. We evaluate our algorithm on a dataset of CT scans of the head and ncek. Our resսlts show that our algorithm can achieve coｍparable image quality to traditional CT scanners, while reducing the radiation exposure to the patient by 50%. Our algorithm has the potential to significantly reduced the radioactivity exposure associated with CT scans, making it a safer imaging option for patients.

We present detailed results from performing general relativistic (GR)simulations of stellar core collapse to a proto-neutron star, using amicrophysical equation of state (EoS) as well as an approximate description ofdeleptonization during the collapse phase. We show that for a wide variety ofrotation rates and profiles the gravitational wave (GW) burst signals from thecore bounce are of a generic type, already known as Type I in the literature.In addition, for most models the characteristic frequency of the GW burstsignal lies in a narrow range around approximately 718 Hz. In our systematicstudy, using both GR and Newtonian gravity, we identify, individually quantify,and discuss in detail the micro- and macrophysical mechanisms leading to thisresult, i.e. the effects of rotation, the EoS, and deleptonization. We alsodiscuss the detectability prospects of such GW burst signals by GW detectors,and infer that such a generic type of signal templates will likely facilitate amore efficient search in current and future detectors of both interferometricand resonant type.

Recently, several powerful tools for the reconstruction of stochasticdifferential equations from measured data sets have been proposed [e.g. Siegertet al., Physics Letters A 243, 275 (1998); Hurn et al., Journal of Time SeriesAnalysis 24, 45 (2003)]. Efficient application of the methods, however,generally requires Markov properties to be fulfilled. This constraint typicallyseems to be violated on small scales, which frequently is attributed tophysical effects. On the other hand, measurement noise such as uncorrelatedmeasurement and discretization errors has large impacts on the statistics ofmeasurements on small scales. We demonstrate, that the presence of measurementnoise, likewise, spoils Markov properties of an underlying Markov processes.This fact is promising for the further development of techniques for thereconstruction of stochastic processes from measured data, since limitations atsmall scales might stem from artificial noise sources rather than fromintrinsic properties of the dynamics of the underlying process. Measurementnoise, however, can be controlled much better than the intrinsic dynamics ofthe underlying process.

This paper presents a measurement-based availability assessment study usingfield data collected during a 4-year period from 373 SunOS/Solaris Unixworkstations and servers interconnected through a local area network. We focuson the estimation of machine uptimes, downtimes and availability based on theidentification of failures that caused total service loss. Data corresponds tosyslogd event logs that contain a large amount of information about the normalactivity of the studied systems as well as their behavior in the presence offailures. It is widely recognized that the information contained in such eventlogs might be incomplete or imperfect. The solution investigated in this paperto address this problem is based on the use of auxiliary sources of dataobtained from wtmpx files maintained by the SunOS/Solaris Unix operatingsystem. The results obtained suggest that the combined use of wtmpx and syslogdlog files provides more complete information on the state of the target systemsthat is useful to provide availability estimations that better reflect reality.

In this study, we present the results of our investigation into the properties of quasars at redshift z~6 using millimeter and radio observations. Our sample consists of 12 quasars, selected from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS) and followed up with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) and the Very Large Array (VLA). We detected millimeter and radio emission from all 12 quasars, with luminosities ranging from 10^26 to 10^28 W Hz^(-1). The millimeter and radio spectra show a range of shapes, with some quasars exhibiting flat or even slightly inverted spectra, while others show steep spectra. We interpret these observations in the context of quasar evolution and find that the flat-spectrum sources are likely to be young, recently triggered quasars, while the steep-spectrum sources are older, more evolved quasars. Our results provide new insights into the early stages of quasar evolution and highlight the importance of multi-wavelength observations in understanding the growth and development of supermassive black holes.

The dynamics of quasicrystals is characterized by the existence of phasonexcitations in addition to the usual phonon modes. In order to investigatetheir interplay on an elementary level we resort to various one-dimensionalmodel systems. The main observables are the static, the incoherent, and thecoherent structure factor, which are extracted from molecular dynamicssimulations. For the validation of the algorithms, results for the harmonicperiodic chain are presented. We then study the Fibonacci chain with harmonicand anharmonic interaction potentials. In the dynamic Fibonacci chainneighboring atoms interact by double-well potentials allowing for phason flips.The difference between the structure factors of the dynamic and the harmonicFibonacci chain lies in the temperature dependence of the phonon line width. Ifa bias is introduced in the well depth, dispersionless optic phonon bands splitoff.

We study models in which supersymmetry breaking appears at an intermediatescale, M_{in}, below the GUT scale. We assume that the softsupersymmetry-breaking parameters of the MSSM are universal at M_{in}, andanalyze the morphology of the constraints from cosmology and colliderexperiments on the allowed regions of parameter space as M_{in} is reduced fromthe GUT scale. We present separate analyses of the (m_{1/2},m_0) planes fortan(beta)=10 and tan(beta)=50, as well as a discussion of non-zero trilinearcouplings, A_0. Specific scenarios where the gaugino and scalar masses appearto be universal below the GUT scale have been found in mirage-mediation models,which we also address here. We demand that the lightest neutralino be the LSP,and that the relic neutralino density not conflict with measurements by WMAPand other observations. At moderate values of M_{in}, we find that the allowedregions of the (m_{1/2},m_0) plane are squeezed by the requirements ofelectroweak symmetry breaking and that the lightest neutralino be the LSP,whereas the constraint on the relic density is less severe. At very low M_{in},the electroweak vacuum conditions become the dominant constraint, and asecondary source of astrophysical cold dark matter would be necessary toexplain the measured relic density for nearly all values of the softSUSY-breaking parameters and tan(beta). We calculate the neutralino-nucleoncross sections for viable scenarios and compare them with the present andprojected limits from direct dark matter searches.

This study present a comparison of lidar observations of midlatitude cirrusclouds over the SIRTA observatory between 2002 and 2006 with multipletropopauses (MT) retrieved from radiosounding temperature profiles. Thetemporal variability of MT properties (frequency, thickness) are discussed.Results show a marked annual cycle, with MT frequency reaching its lowest pointin May (~18% occurrence of MT) and slowly rising to more than 40% in DJF. Theaverage thickness of the MT also follows an annual cycle, going from less than1 km in spring to 1.5 km in late autumn. Comparison with lidar observationsshow that cirrus clouds show a preference for being located close below the 1sttropopause. When the cloud top is above the 1st tropopause (7% ofobservations), in 20% of cases the cloud base is above it as well, resulting ina cirrus cloud "sandwiched" between the two tropopauses. Compared to thegeneral distribution of cirrus, cross-tropopause cirrus show a higher frequencyof large optical depths, while inter-tropopause cirrus show almost exclusivelylow optical depths (Tau < 0.03 in 90% of cases) typical of subvisible clouds.Results suggest the occurrence of inter-tropopause cirrus clouds is correlatedwith the frequency of multiple tropopauses.

The Gemini Planet (GPI) imager is an "extreme" adaptive optics system beingdesigned and built for the Gemini Observatory. GPI combines precise andaccurate wavefront control, diffraction suppression, and a speckle-suppressingscience camera with integral field and polarimetry capabilities. GPI's primaryscience goal is the direct detection and characterization of young, Jovian-massexoplanets. For systems younger than 2 Gyr exoplanets more massive than 6 MJand semimajor axes beyond 10 AU are detected with completeness greater than50%. GPI will also discover faint debris disks, explore icy moons and minorplanets in the solar system, reveal high dynamic range main-sequence binaries,and study mass loss from evolved stars. This white paper explains the role ofGPI in exoplanet discovery and characterization and summarizes ourrecommendations to the NSF-NASA-DOE Astronomy and Astrophysics AdvisoryCommittee ExoPlanet Task Force.

Given the academic article title, write an academic article abstract with 6 sentences:title: Uniqueness of polynomial canonical representationsabstract: In this paper, we study the uniqueness of polynomial canonical representations of algebraic varieties. We first introduce the notion of a polynomial canonical representation and discuss its properties. We then give a necessary and sufficient condition for a polynomial canonical representation to be unique. Finally, we apply our results to give a new proof of the uniqueness of the canonical representation of a smooth projective variety.

Galaxy alignment within dark matter halos is a topic of significant interest in astrophysics. In this study, we investigate three distinct types of galaxy alignment observed within dark matter halos: radial alignment, angular momentum alignment, and filamentary alignment. To accomplish this, we use large-scale cosmological simulations, combined with observational data from galaxy surveys. Our results reveal that radial alignment occurs when galaxies preferentially align along the radial direction of their host dark matter halo. Angular momentum alignment is characterized by the alignment between the angular momentum vectors of galaxies and their dark matter halos. Filamentary alignment occurs when galaxies preferentially align along the cosmic filaments that connect dark matter halos. We find that these alignment types can coexist within the same dark matter halo, suggesting complex environmental effects and multiple formation mechanisms. Understanding the properties and origins of these alignment types is crucial for unraveling the intricate processes involved in galaxy formation and evolution within the cosmic web.

The nature of the pseudogap state, observed above the superconductingtransition temperature TC in many high temperature superconductors, is thecenter of much debate. Recently, this discussion has focused on the number ofenergy gaps in these materials. Some experiments indicate a single energy gap,implying that the pseudogap is a precursor state. Others indicate two,suggesting that it is a competing or coexisting phase. Here we report ontemperature dependent scanning tunneling spectroscopy of Pb-Bi2Sr2CuO6+x. Wehave found a new, narrow, homogeneous gap that vanishes near TC, superimposedon the typically observed, inhomogeneous, broad gap, which is only weaklytemperature dependent. These results not only support the two gap picture, butalso explain previously troubling differences between scanning tunnelingmicroscopy and other experimental measurements.

This study scrutinizes the crucial role of evaluating and transmitting input uncertainty in tree-based option pricing models. We explore various sources of input uncertainty, including volatility, interest rates, and dividend yields, and offer a methodological framework for measuring and incorporating these uncertainties into the option pricing process. Our approach harnesses Monte Carlo simulation to generate scenarios that reflect input uncertainties, and we illustrate how these scenarios can be utilized to estimate option price uncertainty. We also examine the effect of input uncertainty on option price accuracy and find that disregarding these uncertainties can result in considerable errors. Finally, we discuss the implications of our findings for risk management and portfolio optimization in the context of option trading.

Here is an abstract for your aKrticle:This paper investigates the cosmological perturbations as well as the cosmic microwave background (CMB) radiatqon and large sclae structure (LSS) formation in the framework of a primordial neutrino interacting dark-energy (DE) model. We consider the interacting DE model with an exponentiqal potential. For this model, we study the cosmological dynamics and find that the neutrino viscosity coefficient can significantly affect the growth rate of matetr pertCurbations. As a result, the neutrino viscsity coefficient can leave discernible signatures on the CMB and matter power spectra. Through Markov Chain Mente Carlo method, we constrain the neutriAno viscosity coefficient by using the latest CMB and LSS observations. It is fonud that current observations can only provide a loose constraint on the neutrino viscosity coefficient.

Cloaking devices are prescriptions of electrostatic, optical orelectromagnetic parameter fields (conductivity $\sigma(x)$, index of refraction$n(x)$, or electric permittivity $\epsilon(x)$ and magnetic permeability$\mu(x)$) which are piecewise smooth on $\mathbb R^3$ and singular on ahypersurface $\Sigma$, and such that objects in the region enclosed by $\Sigma$are not detectable to external observation by waves. Here, we give relatedconstructions of invisible tunnels, which allow electromagnetic waves to passbetween possibly distant points, but with only the ends of the tunnels visibleto electromagnetic imaging. Effectively, these change the topology of spacewith respect to solutions of Maxwell's equations, corresponding to attaching ahandlebody to $\mathbb R^3$. The resulting devices thus function aselectromagnetic wormholes.

This article explores the character of Mykyta the Fox and his role in Ukrainian literature of the nineteenth century. It argues that Mykyta the Fox is not only a trickster figure but also a symbol of Ukrainian national identity. The article traces the evolution of Mykyta the Fox from his origins in folklore to his appearances in the works of major Ukrainian writers. Mykyta the Fox is a complex and multifaceted character who embodies both the strengths and weaknesses of the Ukrainian national character. As a symbol of Ukrainian national identity, Mykyta the Fox represents the resilience and determination of the Ukrainian people. Mykyta the Fox is a rich and rewarding character who offers insights into Ukrainian history, culture, and national identity. This article provides a comprehensive analysis of Mykyta the Fox and his significance in Ukrainian literature.

This article comprehensively analyzes the observation data and optical characteristics of the IA Supernova SN 2003DU within 480 days. Type IA supernova is known for its use of cosmic distance indicators, and understanding its behavior is essential for precise measurement of cosmic distance and universe parameters. We obtained high -quality multi -wave segmentation of light data from the ground telescope and conducted a detailed analysis to study the evolution of the SN 2003DU optical curve. The analysis shows that the typical optical curve shape of SN 2003DU follows the normal IA supernova, its brightness is significantly peak, and then decreases. Our observation results confirmed that there is a secondary maximum value in the B -band optical curve of about 20 days after the main maximum value, which is consistent with the previous observation results of IA supernova. The subsequent decline in brightness showed a clear slope, which further characterized the nature of SN 2003DU. By comparing the observed optical curve with the theoretical model, we have obtained key parameters, such as rising time, peak light and decrease rate. It is found that the rise time of SN 2003DU is consistent with the expectations of the normal IA supernova of its optical category. Determine the peak lightness within the expected range of similar events, and further verify its classification. In addition, we study the color evolution of the SN 2003DU by analyzing its color curve in different phases. The observed color evolution shows the trend of red, which is consistent with the existence of dust in the supernova environment. We also studied the spectral characteristics of SN 2003DU and determined the outstanding absorption system related to the typical IA supernova spectrum, thereby further confirming its classification.

We present a method to construct semi-numerical ``simulations'', which canefficiently generate realizations of halo distributions and ionization maps athigh redshifts. Our procedure combines an excursion-set approach withfirst-order Lagrangian perturbation theory and operates directly on the lineardensity and velocity fields. As such, the achievable dynamic range with ouralgorithm surpasses the current practical limit of N-body codes by orders ofmagnitude. This is particularly significant in studies of reionization, wherethe dynamic range is the principal limiting factor. We test our halo-findingand HII bubble-finding algorithms independently against N-body simulations withradiative transfer and obtain excellent agreement. We compute the sizedistributions of ionized and neutral regions in our maps. We find even largerionized bubbles than do purely analytic models at the same volume-weighted meanhydrogen neutral fraction. We also generate maps and power spectra of 21-cmbrightness temperature fluctuations, which for the first time includecorrections due to gas bulk velocities. We find that velocities widen the tailsof the temperature distributions and increase small-scale power, though theseeffects quickly diminish as reionization progresses. We also include somepreliminary results from a simulation run with the largest dynamic range todate: a 250 Mpc box that resolves halos with masses M >~ 2.2 x10^8 M_sun. Weshow that accurately modeling the late stages of reionization requires suchlarge scales. The speed and dynamic range provided by our semi-numericalapproach will be extremely useful in the modeling of early structure formationand reionization.

We study the mixing time of a systematic scan Markov chain for sampling fromthe uniform distribution on proper 7-colourings of a finite rectangularsub-grid of the infinite square lattice, the grid. A systematic scan Markovchain cycles through finite-size subsets of vertices in a deterministic orderand updates the colours assigned to the vertices of each subset. The systematicscan Markov chain that we present cycles through subsets consisting of 2x2sub-grids and updates the colours assigned to the vertices using a procedureknown as heat-bath. We give a computer-assisted proof that this systematic scanMarkov chain mixes in O(log n) scans, where n is the size of the rectangularsub-grid. We make use of a heuristic to compute required couplings ofcolourings of 2x2 sub-grids. This is the first time the mixing time of asystematic scan Markov chain on the grid has been shown to mix for less than 8colours. We also give partial results that underline the challenges of provingrapid mixing of a systematic scan Markov chain for sampling 6-colourings of thegrid by considering 2x3 and 3x3 sub-grids.

Abstract:We present the results of a comprehensive study on the identification of new close binary systems from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS-I), specifically focusing on data released under Data Release Five (DR5). By employing various selection criteria and spectroscopic analysis techniques, we have identified a sample of previously unclassified white dwarf systems, shedding light on their properties and evolutionary paths. Among the systems studied, we have discovered several progenitor systems of cataclysmic variables (CVs) with potential magnetic white dwarfs. These findings contribute to our understanding of the formation and evolution of CVs and hold implications for the role of magnetic fields in this class of binary systems. Our research highlights the significance of large-scale surveys like SDSS in uncovering new candidate systems and emphasizes the importance of spectroscopic analysis in characterizing their physical properties. The identified systems will be subject to further investigation, providing valuable insights into the nature of close binary systems and the role of magnetism in their evolutionary processes.

They are easy to interpret and can be used to predict new data. However, they are sensitive to noise and outliers in the data, and they are difficult to scale to large data sets. In this paper, we propose a new decision tree modeling method that uses relational views. Relational views are a way of representing data that emphasizes the relationships between different objects. We evaluate our new method on a variety of data sets and show that it outperforms traditional decision tree methods in terms of accuracy and scalability. Our method is also more interpretable than traditional decision tree methods, making it a useful tool for data scientists and machine learning practitioners. **Keywords**: Decision trees, relational views, machine learning, classification, regression.

A Relativistic Transport Model (ART1.0) is applied to simulate the stoppingeffects in tip-tip and body-body U+U collisions, at a beam kinetic energy of520 MeV/nucleon. Our simulation results have demonstrated that both centralcollisions of the two extreme orientations can achieve full stopping, and alsoform a bulk of hot, dense nuclear matter with a sufficiently large volume andlong duration, due to the largely deformed uranium nuclei. The nucleon sidewardflow in the tip-tip collisions is nearly 3 times larger than that in body-bodyones at normalized impact parameter $b/b_{max}<0.5$, and that the body-bodycentral collisions have a largest negative nucleon elliptic flow $v_{2}=-12%$in contrast to zero in tip-tip ones. Thus the extreme circumstance and thenovel experimental observables in tip-tip and body-body collisions can providea good condition and sensitive probe to study the nuclear EoS, respectively.The Cooling Storage Ring (CSR) External Target Facility (ETF) to be built atLanzhou, China, delivering the uranium beam up to 520 MeV/nucleon is expectedto make significant contribution to explore the nuclear equation of state(EoS).

If a finite group action $\alpha$ on a unital $C^*$-algebra $M$ is saturated,the canonical conditional expectation $E:M\to M^\alpha$ onto the fixed pointalgebra is known to be of index finite type with $Index(E)=|G|$ in the sense ofWatatani. More generally if a finite dimensional Hopf $*$-algebra $A$ acts on$M$ and the action is saturated, the same is true with $Index (E)=\dim(A)$. Inthis paper we prove that the converse is true. Especially in case $M$ is acommutative $C^*$-algebra $C(X)$ and $\alpha$ is a finite group action, we givean equivalent condition in order that the expectation $E:C(X)\to C(X)^\alpha$is of index finite type, from which we obtain that $\alpha$ is saturated if andonly if $G$ acts freely on $X$.  Actions by compact groups are also considered to show that the gauge action$\gamma$ on a graph $C^*$-algebra $C^*(E)$ associated with a locally finitedirected graph $E$ is saturated.

They are believed to be powered by the accretion of matter onto a newly formed black hole. The X-ray afterglow of a GRB is the result of the interaction of the ejecta with the surrounding medium. The decay phase of the X-ray afterglow can be used to probe the properties of the ejecta and the surrounding medium. In this paper, we study the shallow decay phase of the X-ray afterglow of a GRB from a relativistic wind bubble. We develop a model for the X-ray afterglow from a relativistic wind bubble and calculate the light curves. We compare our model with the observed X-ray afterglows of GRBs and find that our model can well reproduce the observed data. Our model suggests that the shallow decay phase of the X-ray afterglow of a GRB is caused by the expansion of the wind bubble. The expansion of the wind bubble causes the density of the ejecta to decrease, which in turn causes the X-ray emission to decrease. Our model also suggests that the properties of the wind bubble can be inferred from the observed X-ray afterglow light curves. The properties of the wind bubble include the density of the ejecta, the radius of the wind bubble, and the Lorentz factor of the wind. Our model provides a new tool for studying the properties of the ejecta and the surrounding medium.

The Measurement Calculus is a groundbreaking approach that revolutionizes the field of quantitative analysJis. This article presents an Win-depth exploration of its theoretical foundations and practical applications. TBhe Measurement Calculus is a mathematical framework that aims to catpure the essence of measurement by providing a unified representation of different measurement scales and their interactions. The article begins by elucidating the fundamental concepts of the Measurement Calculus, highlighting its explicit treatment of measurement uncertainty and its ability to integrate iverse measurement mtehods. It then delves into the mathematical formalism of the framework, presentjng a set of axioms and operations that enable the rigorous characterization and manipulation of measurements acrosHs domains. Furthermore, the article examines several real-world applications of the Measurement Calculus, ranging from physics and engineering to socivl scsiences and economics. It showcases how this framework enhances the accuracy and reliabilWity of measurements, enables meaningful comparisons across dfferent measurement scales, and facilitates the integration of disparate data sources. Moreover, the article addersses the pratcical considerations of implementing the Measurement Calculus, discussing the challenges and opportunities associated with its adoption in various fields. It analyzes the computational complexity of the framework and proposes efficiFent algorithms for its implementation. Finally, the article concludes with a discussion on the future directions of the Measurement Calculus, highlighting the potential for further advancements, such as its integration with machine learning techniques and its application in emerging fields like dlta sience and artiyicial intelligene. Overall, this article offers an extensive overview of the Measurement Calculus, presenting its theoreticad underpinnings, practical implications, and fTuture prospects.

The Araucaria Project is a wide-field imaging survey that aimed to study the distant galaxies in the Loϲal Group. The survey used Cepheid variables, a type of star known for its pulsating brightness, to measure the dis tance to the galaxy WLM. Through the analysis of the survey data, the authors discovered a new sample of Cepheіd variables in WLM, which allowed them to ascertain the distance to the galaxy with unprecedented accurɑcy. The authors found that the mileage to WLM is 2.37 milliоn light-years, which is consistent with previous estimates based on other methods. Tհe study demonstrates the power of using Cepheid variables as distance indicators in the Local Group, and highlights the potential for futսre studies using the Araucaria Project data. The results provide valuable insights into the structure and evolving of the Local Group, and have implications for our understanding of the cosmic dіstance ladder.

This paper deals with a family of spatial rational curves that wereintroduced by Andradas, Recio and Sendra, under the name of hypercircles, as analgorithmic cornerstone tool in the context of improving the rationalparametrization (simplifying the coefficients of the rational functions, whenpossible) of algebraic varieties. A real circle can be defined as the image ofthe real axis under a Moebius transformation in the complex field. Likewise,and roughly speaking, a hypercircle can be defined as the image of a line ("the${\mathbb{K}}$-axis") in a $n$-degree finite algebraic extension$\mathbb{K}(\alpha)\thickapprox\mathbb{K}^n$ under the transformation$\frac{at+b}{ct+d}:\mathbb{K}(\alpha)\to\mathbb{K}(\alpha)$.  The aim of this article is to extend, to the case of hypercircles, some ofthe specific properties of circles. We show that hypercircles are precisely,via $\mathbb{K}$-projective transformations, the rational normal curve of asuitable degree. We also obtain a complete description of the points atinfinity of these curves (generalizing the cyclic structure at infinity ofcircles). We characterize hypercircles as those curves of degree equal to thedimension of the ambient affine space and with infinitely many${\mathbb{K}}$-rational points, passing through these points at infinity.Moreover, we give explicit formulae for the parametrization and implicitationof hypercircles. Besides the intrinsic interest of this very special family ofcurves, the understanding of its properties has a direct application to thesimplification of parametrizations problem, as shown in the last section.

**Polished Abstract:**Atomic electric dipole moments (EDMs) are defined as permanent asymmetric distributions of electric charge within an atom or molecule. In this work, we review Schiff's fundamental theorem, which constrains the values of these EDMs for stationary states of atoms and molecules. We discuss the various corrections to this theorem that arise in the context of relativistic quantum field theory, including contributions from hadronic and nuclear sources, as well as effects due to the finite size of the nucleus. We also discuss the relationship between EDMs and fundamental symmetries, such as parity and time-reversal invariance. Finally, we briefly outline the current experimental status of EDM searches and future prospects in this field.

Abstract:The multiwavelength properties of a sample of 144 X-ray-selected star-forming galaxies in the Chandra Deep Field South are studied using a variety of ancillary multiwavelength data, including deep optical and near-infrared imaging, ultraviolet (UV) and mid-infrared photometry, and optical spectroscopy. The median redshift of the sample is z = 0.94. The galaxies span a range of UV and X-ray luminosities, stellar masses, and star formation rates. The UV-to-X-ray spectral slopes of the sample galaxies are found to be correlated with their X-ray luminosities. The X-ray properties of the sample galaxies are compared to those of optically selected star-forming galaxies at similar redshifts. The X-ray luminosities of the X-ray-selected sample are found to be systematically higher than those of the optically selected sample, at a given UV luminosity or stellar mass. The differences in X-ray properties between the two samples are likely due to differences in their star formation histories. The X-ray-selected sample galaxies have higher specific star formation rates and younger stellar populations than the optically selected sample galaxies. These results suggest that X-ray selection is biased toward galaxies with ongoing starbursts.

**Abstract**This paper investigates risk perception in epidemic modeling. We propose a novel framework for modeling risk perception that is based on the idea that individuals' perceptions of risk are influenced by both their own experiences and the experiences of others. We show that our framework can be used to improve the accuracy of epidemic models. We also discuss the implications of our findings for public health policy.

This study investigates the dynamic effects that increase the vulnerability of complex networks to cascading failures. We analyze the temporal evolvement of failures in a network and demonstrate how certain dynamic properties can significantly impact the network's robustness. Our results show that the rate at which failures occur, the duration of failures, and the type of failure propagation can all contribute to a network's susceptibility to cascading failures. We present a new methodology for quantifying the dynamic effects on network vulnerability and apply it to several real-world network examples. Our findings have important implications for the design and management of complex systems, highlighting the need for a more comprehensive understanding of dynamic effects in network resilience.

Intersection bodies represent a remarkable class of geometric objectsassociated with sections of star bodies and invoking  Radon transforms, generalized cosine transforms, and the relevant Fourieranalysis. The main focus of this article is interrelation between generalizedcosine transforms of different kinds in the context of their application toinvestigation of a certain family of intersection bodies, which we call$\lam$-intersection bodies. The latter include $k$-intersection bodies (in thesense of A. Koldobsky) and unit balls of finite-dimensional subspaces of$L_p$-spaces. In particular, we show that restrictions onto lower dimensionalsubspaces of the spherical Radon transforms and the generalized cosinetransforms preserve their integral-geometric structure. We apply this result tothe study of sections of $\lam$-intersection bodies. New characterizations ofthis class of bodies are obtained and examples are given. We also review someknown facts and give them new proofs.

Late phase nebular spectra and photometry of Type Ib Supernova (SN) 2005bftaken by the Subaru telescope at ~ 270 and ~ 310 days since the explosion arepresented. Emission lines ([OI]6300, 6363, [CaII]7291, 7324, [FeII]7155) showthe blueshift of ~ 1,500 - 2,000 km s-1. The [OI] doublet shows a doubly-peakedprofile. Encountered by this difficulty inthe 56Ni heating model, we suggest an alternative scenario in which the heatingsource is a newly born, strongly magnetized neutron star (a magnetar) with thesurface magnetic field Bmag ~ 10^{14-15} gauss and the initial spin period P0 ~10 ms. Then, SN 2005bf could be a link between normal SNe Ib/c and an X-RayFlash associated SN 2006aj, connected in terms of Bmag and/or P0.

This study presents a comprehensive analysis of the light curves exhibited by dwarf Plutonian planets and other large Kuiper Belt objects, focusing on their rotational properties, phase functions, and absolute magnitudes. Light curves provide valuable insights into the physical characteristics and dynamics of celestial bodies in our solar system. Through an examination of the rotational periods of these objects, a deeper understanding of their internal structure and orbital dynamics is gained. The analysis employs observational data collected from a variety of ground-based telescopes, spacecraft missions, and space-based observatories.The rotational properties of dwarf Plutonian planets and large Kuiper Belt objects exhibit significant variations, suggesting diversity in their internal composition, shape, and surface features. Investigation of their phase functions reveals the scattering and reflective properties of their surfaces, shedding light on their geophysical properties and surface textures. Additionally, the determination of absolute magnitudes allows estimation of their sizes and albedos, which are crucial for understanding their overall composition.The methodology employed in this research involves photometric observations of these objects over extended time periods, enabling the detection of rotational periodicities and potential irregularities. Careful analysis and modeling of the acquired light curves enable us to derive accurate rotational periods and examine potential variations in rotational axis orientations. The phase curves are then obtained by studying the variations in brightness as a function of the object's phase angle.Furthermore, the study compares the observed light curves and derived rotational properties with theoretical models to further constrain the physical characteristics of these celestial bodies, such as their spin-axis obliquity and three-dimensional shape models.

A release of multicolor broad band (BVRI) photometry for a subsample of 44isolated spirals drawn from the Catalogue of Isolated Galaxies (CIG) ispresented. Total magnitudes and colors at various circular apertures, as wellas some global structural/morphological parameters are estimated. Morphology isreevaluated through optical and sharp/filtered R band images, (B-I) color indexmaps, and archive near-IR JHK images from the Two-Micron Survey. The CASstructural parameters (Concentration, Asymmetry, and Clumpiness) werecalculated from the images in each one of the bands. The fraction of galaxieswith well identified optical/near-IR bars (SB) is 63%, while a 17% more showsevidence of weak or suspected bars (SAB). The sample average value of themaximum bar ellipticity is 0.4. Half of the galaxies in the sample shows rings.We identify two candidates for isolated galaxies with disturbed morphology. Thestructural CAS parameters change with the observed band, and the tendenciesthey follow with the morphological type and global color are more evident inthe redder bands. In any band, the major difference between our isolatedspirals and a sample of interacting spirals is revealed in the A-S plane. Adeep and uniformly observed sample of isolated galaxies is intended for variouspurposes including (i) comparative studies of environmental effects, (ii)confronting model predictions of galaxy evolution and (iii) evaluating thechange of galaxy properties with redshift.

For \alpha in the interval [0,1], we consider the one-parameter family of\alpha-continued fraction maps, which include the Gauss map (\alpha=1) and thenearest integer (\alpha=1/2) and by-excess (\alpha=0) continued fraction maps.To each of these expansions, and to each choice of a positive function u on theinterval I_\alpha=(0,max(\alpha,1-\alpha)) we associate a generalized Brjunofunction B_(\alpha,u)(x). For \alpha=1/2 or \alpha=1, and u(x)=-\log(x), thesefunctions were introduced by Yoccoz in his work on the linearization ofholomorphic maps. We then consider the case \alpha=0,u(x)=-\log(x) and we prove that x is a Brjuno number (for \alpha> 0) if andonly if both x and -x are Brjuno numbers for \alpha=0.

The cosmic microwave background (CMB) is a relic radiation of the Big Bangand as such it contains a wealth of cosmological information. Statisticalanalyses of the CMB, in conjunction with other cosmological observables,represent some of the most powerful techniques available to cosmologists forplacing strong constraints on the cosmological parameters that describe theorigin, content and evolution of the Universe. The last decade has witnessedthe introduction of wavelet analyses in cosmology and, in particular, theirapplication to the CMB. We review here spherical wavelet analyses of the CMBthat test the standard cosmological concordance model. The assumption that thetemperature anisotropies of the CMB are a realisation of a statisticallyisotropic Gaussian random field on the sphere is questioned. Deviations fromboth statistical isotropy and Gaussianity are detected in the reviewed works,suggesting more exotic cosmological models may be required to explain ourUniverse. We also review spherical wavelet analyses that independently provideevidence for dark energy, an exotic component of our Universe of which we knowvery little currently. The effectiveness of accounting correctly for thegeometry of the sphere in the wavelet analysis of full-sky CMB data isdemonstrated by the highly significant detections of physical processes andeffects that are made in these reviewed works.

The (TMTSF)2FSO3 compound is known for its unique electronic properties and has been used as a model system to study various quantum phenomena. By mapping the infrared spectra across the surface of the sample, we observe a distinct change in the vibrational modes and the excitations of the material as it undergoes the transition. Through this comprehensive analysis, we provide a detailed understanding of the nature of the metal-insulator transition in (TMTSF)2FSO3 and its potential applications in future devices. We also investigate the microscopic origins of the transition by analyzing the conductivity and charge localization in different regions of the sample.

In terms of micro, the continuous magnetic distribution of blood cells and plasma is separated ** abstract **: magnetic separation is a promising technology for blood cell separation, because its advantages are not marked, highly efficient and high purity. However, the conventional magnetic separation method is limited by its large size, complex manufacturing and low throughput. In this article, we have proposed continuous magnetic blood cell separation devices. The device consists of a micro -channel with a magnetic micro -line array. When the blood sample flows through the micro -channel, magnetic micro urine will produce magnetic gradients, which will attract and separate blood cells from plasma. Then collect the separated blood cells at the exit of the microcontroller. The device is easy to make, covers the area of small area, and can handle blood samples under high traffic. The experimental results show that the separation efficiency of the equipment exceeds 99 % and the purity exceeds 95 %. The device has potential applications in the diagnosis and blood transfusion of nursing points.

This article presents a unpublished binary model aimed at explaining the puzzling phenomenon known as the UV-upturn remarked in elliptical galaxies. The UV-upturn refers to the unexpected amplify in the ultraviolet (UV) lighter emitted by these galaxies at wavelengths shorter than 250 nm. Despite numerous studies, the physical mechanisms driving this problems remain unclear. From this study, we propose a binary star formation model as a potential explanation for the UV-upturn. The model suggests that the UV emission is primarily induced by a population of hot, young stars in binary appliance. These binary systems are characterized by mass transporting and mergers, which steering to the formation of short-lived, massive stars that emit large amounts of UV radiation. We corroborated our model by comparing its estimates with observational data from a sample of elliptical galaxies exhibiting the UV-upturn. Our analysis demonstrates that the binary model successfully reproduces the uncovered UV-upturn phenomenon, providing a plausible clarified for this enigmatic feature. Furthermore, our model offers insights into the underlying physical processes driving the UV-upturn, shedding leyte on the formation and evolution of elliptical galaxies. Furthermore, we investigate the properties of the binary systems responsible for the UV emission and examine their affecting for the overall stellar inhabitants in elliptical galaxies. Our results suggest that the UV-upturn asteroids harbor a significant fraction of binary star systems, potentially influencing the stellar mass distribution and extraordinary evolution pathways in these galaxies. Overall, this binary model for the UV-upturn represents a great advancement in our understand of the peculiar UV properties of elliptical galaxies. It not only provides a viable explanation for the observed phenomenon but also paves the chemin for future investigations into the intricate interactions between binary star formation and galaxy advancements.

In this article, we investigate the concept of ordered addition of two Lorentz boosts through spatial and space-time rotations, examining the relationship between these transformations and how they can be combined to achieve a desired total transformation. We introduce the concept of ordered addition, which enables us to sequence the application of these transformations in a specific way, resulting in a more efficient and straightforward calculation of the final transformation. Through several examples, we demonstrate the utility of this approach in simplifying complex calculations in fields such as particle physics and astrophysics. Our work also sheds light on the symmetries of spacetime, opening up potential avenues for future research.

This paper presents a fault classification method which makes use of aTakagi-Sugeno neuro-fuzzy model and Pseudomodal energies calculated from thevibration signals of cylindrical shells. The calculation of PseudomodalEnergies, for the purposes of condition monitoring, has previously been foundto be an accurate method of extracting features from vibration signals. Thiscalculation is therefore used to extract features from vibration signalsobtained from a diverse population of cylindrical shells. Some of the cylindersin the population have faults in different substructures. The pseudomodalenergies calculated from the vibration signals are then used as inputs to aneuro-fuzzy model. A leave-one-out cross-validation process is used to test theperformance of the model. It is found that the neuro-fuzzy model is able toclassify faults with an accuracy of 91.62%, which is higher than the previouslyused multilayer perceptron.

We investigate the coupled formation and evolution of galaxies and theirembedded supermassive black holes using state-of-the-art hydrodynamicsimulations of cosmological structure formation. For the first time, weself-consistently follow the dark matter dynamics, radiative gas cooling, starformation, as well as black hole growth and associated feedback processes,starting directly from initial conditions appropriate for the LambdaCDMcosmology. Our modeling of the black hole physics is based on an approach wehave developed in simulations of isolated galaxy mergers. Here we examine: (i)the predicted global history of black hole mass assembly (ii) the evolution ofthe local black hole-host mass correlations and (iii) the conditions that allowrapid growth of the first quasars, and the properties of their hosts anddescendants today. We find a total black hole mass density in good agreementwith observational estimates. The black hole accretion rate density peaks atlower redshift and evolves more strongly at high redshift than the starformation rate density, but the ratio of black hole to stellar mass densitiesshows only a moderate evolution at low redshifts. We find strong correlationsbetween black hole masses and properties of the stellar systems, agreeing wellwith the measured local M_BH-sigma and M_BH -M_* relationships, but alsosuggesting (dependent on the mass range) a weak evolution with redshift in thenormalization and the slope. Our simulations also produce massive black holesat high redshift, due to extended periods of exponential growth in regions thatcollapse early and exhibit strong gas inflows. These first supermassive BHsystems however are not necessarily the most massive ones today, since they areoften overtaken in growth by quasars that form later. (abridged)

This study investigates the formation mechanisms of lake stars, a unique geological phenomenon observeԁ in certain lakes worldwide. The research integrates field surveys, laboratory experiments, and numerical modeⅼing to explore the underlying process involved in the development of these enigmatic features. Fіeld surveys conducted in various lakes reveal distinct morphological characteristics and spatial distribution patterns of lake stars. Laboratory experiments simulating different hydrodynamic conditions shed light on the role of water currents, sediment dynamics, and bottоm topography in the formtaion of these structures. Finally, numerical modelling using computational fluid dynamics provides insights into the long-term evolution of lake stars under varying environｍental conditions. The findigns contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the formation mechanisms of lake stars and offer valuable insights into the study of similar geological formations in other waters environments.

In this paper we consider two processes driven by diffusions and jumps. Thejump components are Levy processes and they can both have finite activity andinfinite activity. Given discrete observations we estimate the covariationbetween the two diffusion parts and the co-jumps. The detection of the co-jumpsallows to gain insight in the dependence structure of the jump components andhas important applications in finance. Our estimators are based on a thresholdprinciple allowing to isolate the jumps. This work follows Gobbi and Mancini(2006) where the asymptotic normality for the estimator of the covariation,with convergence speed given by the squared root of h, was obtained when thejump components have finite activity. Here we show that the speed is thesquared root of h only when the activity of the jump components is moderate.

We use optical tweezers to perform stretching experiments on DNA moleculeswhen interacting with the drugs daunomycin and ethidium bromide, whichintercalate the DNA molecule. These experiments are performed in the low-forceregime from zero up to 2 pN. Our results show that the persistence length ofthe DNA-drug complexes increases strongly as the drug concentration increasesup to some critical value. Above this critical value, the persistence lengthdecreases abruptly and remains practically constant for larger drugconcentrations. The contour length of the molecules increases monotonically andsaturates as drugs concentration increases. Measured in- tercalants criticalconcentrations for the persistence length transition coincide with reportedvalues for the helix-coil transition of DNA-drug complexes, obtained fromsedimentation experiments.

This article investiates the connection between Landau-Lifshitz sigma-models, fermions, and the Anti-de Sitter/Conformal Field Theory (AdS/CFT) correspondence. he ASd/CFT correspondence is a well-known duality between a gravitational theory in Anti-de Sitter (AdS) space and a conformal field theory (aCFT) defined on the boundary of that space. nI this work, we examine the role of Landau-Lifshitz sigma-models, which are field theories describing the dynamics of magnetic materials, in the conteUxt of this duality. We focus on the inclusion of fermionic degrees of freedom withiq Landau-Lifshitz sigma-models and their implications for the AdS/CIT correspondence. Fermions are fundamental particles with half-integer spin and their presence can significantly affect the dynDamics of the system. By incorporating fermions into the Landau-Lifshitz sigma-mmdels, we aim to explore how their interactions and behavior influence the underlying gravitational theory in AdS spac. Furthermore, we investigate the consequences of this fermionic inclsuion on the CFT defined on the boundary. Speciically, we study the effects of fermions on the dual conformal field theory, relating the microscopic properties of the field theory to macroscopic phenomena obesrved in the magnetic mtaerials described by the Landau-Lifshitz sigma-models. By analyznig this interplay between fermionic degrees of freedom, Landau-Lifshitz sigma-models, and the AdS/CFT correspondence, we aim to deepen our unerstanding of the underlying physics. This research has implicatioans for both condensed matter systems and gravitational theories, shedding light on the complex connections between magnetic mtaerials, fermions, and holography.
