However, virtualization also introduces several problems that need to be addressed. One of the prime concerns is security. Virtualization can create additional attack surfaces and increase the stake of data breaches. Virtual machines share the same physical hardware, which can make it easier for attackers to compromise multiple systems if one virtual equipments is eroded. Additionally, virtualization can establishes new vulnerabilities and misconfigurations that can be exploited by attackers. Another challenge of virtualization is complicated. Managing a virtualized environment ask specialized skills and knowledge. Administrators need to be familiar with the underlying virtualization technique and the specific configuration requirements of each virtual machine.

We determine the Z-module structure of the preprojective algebra and itszeroth Hochschild homology, for any non-Dynkin quiver (and hence the structureworking over any base commutative ring, of any characteristic). This answers(and generalizes) a conjecture of Hesselholt and Rains, producing new$p$-torsion classes in degrees 2p^l, l >= 1, We relate these classes by p-thpower maps and interpret them in terms of the kernel of Verschiebung maps fromnoncommutative Witt theory. An important tool is a generalization of theDiamond Lemma to modules over commutative rings, which we give in the appendix.  In the previous version, additional results are included, such as: thePoisson center of $\text{Sym } HH_0(\Pi)$ for all quivers, the BV algebrastructure on Hochschild cohomology, including how the Lie algebra structure$HH_0(\Pi_Q)$ naturally arises from it, and the cyclic homology groups of$\Pi_Q$.

**Abstract**Ultrathin films of La0.67Ca0.33MnO3 (LCMO) were grown on SrTiO3 (STO) substrates by pulsed laser deposition. The films were characterized by X-ray diffraction, atomic force microscopy, and transport measurements. The films were found to be single-phase and epitaxial with a smooth surface morphology. The electrical resistivity of the films was measured as a function of temperature and magnetic field. The films exhibited a metal-insulator transition at around 250 K. The magnetoresistance of the films was found to be large and positive, with a value of around 60% at 5 K and 9 T. The transport properties of the films are discussed in terms of the electronic structure of LCMO and the effects of strain and confinement on the magnetic and electronic properties of the films.

The concept of (a,b)-module comes from the study the Gauss-Manin lattices ofan isolated singularity of a germ of an holomorphic function. It is a verysimple ''abstract algebraic structure'', but very rich, whose prototype is theformal completion of the Brieskorn-module of an isolated singularity. The aimof this article is to prove a very basic theorem on regular (a,b)-modulesshowing that a given regular (a,b)-module is completely characterized by some''finite order jet'' of its structure. Moreover a very simple bound for such asufficient order is given in term of the rank and of two very simple invariants: the regularity order which count the number of times you need to apply \$b^{-1}.a \simeq \partial_z.z$ in order to reach a simple pole (a,b)-module.The second invariant is the ''width'' which corresponds, in the simple polecase, to the maximal integral difference between to eigenvalues of $b^{-1}.a$  (the logarithm of the monodromy). In the computation of examples this theoremis quite helpfull because it tells you at which power of $b$ in the expansionsyou may stop without loosing any information.

This paper investigates the wiretap channel with feedback, specifically exploring the use of decoder over the channels to enhance security. The wiretap channel specimen is extended to insert a feedback link from the receiver to the sender, allowing for the possibility of encrypted communication. The authors analyze the capability of the channel and develop a coding scheme that utilizes the remarks link to enhance the security of the communication. The proposed scheme is shown to achieve a higher level of security compared to traditional interceptor channel specimen without feedback. The authors also discuss the practical implications of their raison and the potential for real-world applications. Finally, the ledger concludes with a discussion on the limitations of the proposed scheme and potential itinerary for future research.

In this paper, photonic entanglement and interference are described andanalyzed with the language of quantum information process. Correspondingly, aphoton state involving several degrees of freedom is represented in a newexpression based on the permutation symmetry of bosons. In this expression,each degree of freedom of a single photon is regarded as a qubit and operationson photons as qubit gates. The two-photon Hong-Ou-Mandel interference is wellinterpreted with it. Moreover, the analysis reveals the entanglement betweendifferent degrees of freedom in a four-photon state from parametric downconversion, even if there is no entanglement between them in the two-photonstate. The entanglement will decrease the state purity and photon interferencevisibility in the experiments on a four-photon polarization state.

This study investigates the phenomenon of moment switching in nanotube magnetic force proes. Nanotubes have garnered significant intеrest in recent years due to their unique properties and potential applications in various fields, including biotechnology and biomedical sciences. By utilizing advanced experimental techniques and computational simul ations, the authors demonstrates the ability of nanotubes to switch their magnetic moments under external stimuli. The findings reveal that the moment sԝitching behaviour of nanotubes can be controlled by adjusting the applied magnetic field strength and orientation. Additionally, the authors propose a theoretical model to exрlain the underlying mechanisms of moment switching in nɑnotube probes, which involves the reorientation of magnetic domains within the nanotube structure. These insights into the moments switching phenomenon in nanotube magnetic force probes can pave the way for the developing of novel sensing and imaging technologies with enhanced sensitivity and spatiɑl resolution.

Reionization is thought to be dominated by low mass galaxies, while directobservations of resolved galaxies probe only the most massive, rarest objects.The cross-correlation between fluctuations in the surface brightness of thecumulative Ly-alpha emission (which serves as a proxy for the star formationrate) and the redshifted 21cm signal from neutral hydrogen in the intergalacticmedium (IGM), will directly probe the causal link between the production ofionizing photons in galaxies and the reionization of the IGM. We discuss theprospects for detecting this cross-correlation for unresolved galaxies. We findthat on angular scales <10 arc-minutes, detection will be practical usingwidefield near-IR imaging from space in combination with the forthcomingMileura Widefield Array - Low Frequency Demonstrator. When redshifted 21cmobservations of the neutral IGM are combined with space-based near-IR imagingof Ly-alpha emission, the detection on angular scales <3 arc-minutes will belimited by the sensitivity of the 21cm signal, even when a small apertureoptical telescope (~2m) and a moderate field of view (~10 square degrees) areused. On scales >3 arc-minutes, the measurement of cross-correlation will belimited by the accuracy of the foreground sky subtraction.

The observed patterns of magnetic fields suggest the presence of both regular and chaotic components in the inner regions of the Milky Way. Using a comprehensive sample of 500 polarized radio sources observed at two different frequency bands (1.4 and 2.3 GHz) with the Australia Telescope Compact Array (ATCA), we conducted a detailed analysis of the RM distribution. The results show a significant variation in the RMs across the surveyed region, which can be attributed to the complex interplay between the Galactic magnetic field and intervening ionized gas structures. In summary, this study provides valuable insights into the complex dynamics and properties of the magnetic fields in the inner Milky Way, and paves the way for further investigations in the field of galactic magnetism. Moreover, the study demonstrates the potential of using RM measurements as a powerful tool to map and characterize the magnetic field configuration in the Galactic Plane, thereby contributing to our understanding of the intricate magnetic structure of our galaxy on a larger scale.

This study utilizes a Zakharov simulation to investigate the spectral features of on-demand Langmuir turbulence in an inhomogeneous plasma. The simulation reveals that the turbulence exhibits a broad spectrum of wave numbers, with a power-law distribution of energy across the spectrum. The spectral features are found to be strongly influenced by the plasma inhomogeneities, with the turbulence being suppressed in regions of high density and enhanced in regions of low density. The simulation also demonstrates the ability to control the onset and evolution of the turbulence by tailoring theinitial conditions and the plasma inhomogeneities. The results of this study have important implications for the understanding and control of Langmuir turbulence in a variety of plasma environments, including fusion devices and space plasmas. The findings suggest that the inhomogeneities in the plasma can be used to manipulate the spectral features of the turbulence, which can be useful for optimizing the performance of fusion devices and other plasma-based technologies. Additionally, the ability to control the onset and evolution of the turbulence using initial conditions and plasma inhomogeneities provides a means of studying the nonlinear dynamics of the turbulence in a controlled manner. This study demonstrates the importance of considering the effects of plasma inhomogeneities in the study of Langmuir turbulence and highlights the potential of using Zakharov simulations to explore the complex dynamics of plasma turbulence.

This note relates topics in statistical mechanics, graph theory andcombinatorics, lattice quantum field theory, super quantum mechanics and stringtheory. We give a precise relation between the dimer model on a graph embeddedon a torus and the massless free Majorana fermion living on the same lattice. Aloop expansion of the fermion determinant is performed, where the loops turnout to be compositions of two perfect matchings. These loop states are sortedinto co-chain groups using categorification techniques similar to the ones usedfor categorifying knot polynomials. The Euler characteristic of the resultingco-chain complex recovers the Newton polynomial of the dimer model. Were-interpret this system as supersymmetric quantum mechanics, whereconfigurations with vanishing net winding number form the ground states.Finally, we make use of the quiver gauge theory - dimer model correspondence toobtain an interpretation of the loops in terms of the physics of D-branesprobing a toric Calabi-Yau singularity.

Bispectrality of mul𝚝ivariable Racah-Wilson polynomialsThe bispectrality of multivariable Racah-Wilson polynomials is studied. The expliϲit expressions of bispectral coefficients are given in terms of multivariable Racah-Wilson polynomials. Some special cases are additionally considered. Racah-Wilson polynomials, bispectrality, muⅼtivariable,  bispectral coefficients

** Abstract ** Canadian Galaxy Graphic Survey (CGP) is a continuous survey of deep radio of the Galaxy Plane of 1420 and 408 MHz. In this article, we resumed Kühr and Reich (1991) and CGPS 1420 MHz survey related compact radio source directory. Using deeper high -resolution CGP 408 MHz survey data, we determined the 135 sources omitted in the Kühr and Reich directory. We introduce the spectral characteristics of all sources and re -classify their spectral types. By comparing our results with the results of the Kühr and the Reich directory, we find that the spectral index at the low radio frequency is usually steeper than the spectrum index at high radio frequency. We also found that the revised spectral type and magnetic field and relativity particles are more consistent. We have determined the four new GHz peak spectrum (GPS) sources. Their radio brightness and spectral index are consistent with the light index of typical GPS sources.

**Polished Abstract:**Andromeda II, a nearby dwarf spheroidal galaxy, is the focus of an extensive optical survey using the Suprime-Cam on the Subaru Telescope. This survey encompasses a substantial area of 1.4 square degrees and attains a limiting magnitude of 27.5 in the g-band and 26.5 in the r-band. Consequently, more than 10,000 individual stars, encompassing both Andromeda II member stars and foreground stars, have been identified. The main sequence and red giant branch of Andromeda II have been successfully distinguished through analysis of the color-magnitude diagram. Furthermore, the surface brightness profile of Andromeda II has been quantified, enabling the derivation of its structural parameters. The outcomes of this study align with previous research on Andromeda II. The presented deep, wide-field optical survey constitutes a significant resource for future investigations of Andromeda II and other neighboring dwarf galaxies.

We show how one can obtain a lower bound for the electrical, spin or heatconductivity of correlated quantum systems described by Hamiltonians of theform H = H0 + g H1. Here H0 is an interacting Hamiltonian characterized byconservation laws which lead to an infinite conductivity for g=0. The smallperturbation g H1, however, renders the conductivity finite at finitetemperatures. For example, H0 could be a continuum field theory, where momentumis conserved, or an integrable one-dimensional model while H1 might describethe effects of weak disorder. In the limit g to 0, we derive lower bounds forthe relevant conductivities and show how they can be improved systematicallyusing the memory matrix formalism. Furthermore, we discuss various applicationsand investigate under what conditions our lower bound may become exact.

We present a numerical study and analytical model of the optical near-fielddiffracted in the vicinity of subwavelength grooves milled in silver surfaces.The Green's tensor approach permits computation of the phase and amplitudedependence of the diffracted wave as a function of the groove geometry. It isshown that the field diffracted along the interface by the groove is equivalentto replacing the groove by an oscillating dipolar line source. An analyticexpression is derived from the Green's function formalism, that reproduces wellthe asymptotic surface plasmon polariton (SPP) wave as well as the transientsurface wave in the near-zone close to the groove. The agreement between thismodel and the full simulation is very good, showing that the transient"near-zone" regime does not depend on the precise shape of the groove. Finally,it is shown that a composite diffractive evanescent wave model that includesthe asymptotic SPP can describe the wavelength evolution in this transientnear-zone. Such a semi-analytical model may be useful for the design andoptimization of more elaborate photonic circuits whose behavior in large partwill be controlled by surface waves.

In this paper we revisit the Kürh and Reich catalog of compact radio sources associated with the CGPS 1420 MHz survey. Using the deeper and more sensitive CGPS 408 MHz data, we identify 135 sources that are not in the Kürh and Reich catalog. We present the spectral properties of all the sources and reclassify their spectral types. We identify four new sources with GHz-peaked spectra. By comparing our results with the Kürh and Reich catalog, we find that the spectral index at low radio frequency is generally steeper than at high radio frequency. We also find that the revised spectral types are more consistent with the radio luminosity and the equipartition condition between magnetic field and relativistic particles. The radio luminosity and spectral index of these sources are consistent with those of typical CGPS sources.

Context:A significant fraction of progenitors for long gamma-ray bursts(GRBs) are believed to be massive stars. The investigation of long GRBstherefore requires modeling the propagation of ultra-relativistic blastwavesthrough the circumburst medium surrounding massive stars. We simulate theexpansion of an isotropic, adiabatic relativistic fireball into the wind-shapedmedium around a massive GRB progenitor. The circumburst medium is composed of arealistically stratified stellar wind zone up to its termination shock,followed by a region of shocked wind characterized by a constant density. Aims:We followed the evolution of the blastwave through all its stages, includingthe extremely rapid acceleration up to a Lorentz factor 75 flow, itsdeceleration by interaction with stellar wind, its passage of the windtermination shock, until its propagation through shocked wind. Methods: We usedthe adaptive mesh refinement versatile advection code to follow the evolutionof the fireball. Results: We show that the acceleration from purely thermal toultra-relativistic kinetic regimes is abrupt and produces an internallystructured blastwave. We resolved the structure of this ultra-relativisticshell in all stages, thanks to the adaptive mesh. We comment on the dynamicalroles played by forward and reverse shock pairs in the phase of interactionwith the free stellar wind and clearly identify the complex shock-dominatedstructure created when the shell crosses the terminal shock. Conclusion: Weshow that in our model where the terminal shock is taken relatively close tothe massive star, the phase of self-similar deceleration of Blandford-McKeetype can only be produced in the constant density, shocked wind zone.

The dissociation of oxygen molecules at the Al(111) surface has been a subject of great interest in surface sciences researched. While previous studies have focused on the adiabatic dissociation transformed, recent findings have revealed the significance of non-adiabatic effects in this system. In this bylaws, we investigate the non-adiabatic effects in the dissociation of oxygen atoms at the Al(111) surface using density functional theory (DFT) and kinetic Monte Carlo simulations. Our results showed that non-adiabatic effects toy a crucial role in the dissociation process, leading to a significant decrease in the dissociation rate compared to adiabatic predictions. We get that the non-adiabatic afflicting arise from the coupling between the molecular vibrations and the nuclear motion on the surface, which modifies the potential energy surface and leads to a slower dissociation rate. Our results have transcendent implications for the understanding of chemical reactions at surfaces and highlight the need to include non-adiabatic results in the study of surface processes.

The ground-state phase diagram of the model is obtained by Wang-Landau Monte Carlo simulation. The nature of the magnetic phase transitions is analyzed by the magnetic susceptibility, the specific heat, and the fourth-order cumulant. In the ground-state phase diagram, we find that a variety of magnetic structures, such as noncollinear antiferromagnetism, collinear antiferromagnetism, ferrimagnetism, and spin-flop states, appear. The magnetic properties of clusters and arrays of -rings with competing interactions are studied by exact diagonalization and Wang-Landau Monte Carlo simulation. It is found that the model can be mapped onto the classical X model with Dzyaloshinsky-Moriya interaction and symmetric exchange anisotropy, and the properties of the low-energy excitations of the model are studied by spin-wave theory. It is found that the quantum effect is enhanced in the case of noncollinear antiferromagnetism. The energy gaps of the low-lying excited states of various clusters and arrays of -rings are calculated, and it is found that the arrays of -rings with competing interactions can be a good candidate for adiabatic quantum computation.

Here is a possible abstract for the academic article:This paper investigates boundary Harnack inequalities for regional fractional Laplacian. Our results extend the classical Harnack inequality for the Laplace operator to the regional fractional Laplacian operator. Moreover, the established boundary Harnack inequalities are sharp. Our approach is based on constructing appropriate comparison functions and utilizing the maximum principle. The obtained results are essential for the study of various boundary value problems involving the regional fractional Laplacian operator.

In order to explore the magnetostrophic regime expected for planetary cores,experiments have been conducted in a rotating sphere filled with liquid sodium,with an imposed dipolar magnetic field (the DTS setup). The field is producedby a permanent magnet enclosed in an inner sphere, which can rotate at aseparate rate, producing a spherical Couette flow. The flow properties areinvestigated by measuring electric potentials on the outer sphere, the inducedmagnetic field in the laboratory frame, and velocity profiles inside the liquidsodium using ultrasonic Doppler velocimetry. The present article focuses on thetime-averaged axisymmetric part of the flow. The Doppler profiles show that theangular velocity of the fluid is relatively uniform in most of the fluid shell,but rises near the inner sphere, revealing the presence of a magnetic wind, andgently drops towards the outer sphere. The transition from a magnetostrophicflow near the inner sphere to a geostrophic flow near the outer sphere iscontrolled by the local Elsasser number. For Rossby numbers up to order 1, theobserved velocity profiles all show a similar shape. Numerical simulations inthe linear regime are computed, and synthetic velocity profiles are comparedwith the measured ones. In the geostrophic region, a torque-balance modelprovides very good predictions. We find that the induced magnetic field variesin a consistent fashion, and displays a peculiar peak in the counter-rotatingregime. This happens when the fluid rotation rate is almost equal and oppositeto the outer sphere rotation rate. The fluid is then almost at rest in thelaboratory frame, and the Proudman-Taylor constraint vanishes, enabling astrong meridional flow. We suggest that dynamo action might be favored in sucha situation.

We study the growth of cosmological perturbations in the model ofLorentz-violating massive gravity. The Friedman equation in this model acquiresan unconventional term due to the Lorentz-breaking condensates which has theequation of state w = -1 / (3 gamma) with gamma being a free parameter takingvalues outside of the range [0,1/3]. Apart from the standard contributions, theperturbations above the Friedmann background contain an extra piece which isproportional to an arbitrary function theta(x) of the space coordinates. Thisfunction appears as an integration constant and corresponds to anon-propagating scalar mode which may, however, become dynamical with theaccount of the higher-derivative corrections. For -1 < gamma < 0 and gamma = 1the ``anomalous'' perturbations grow slower than the standard ones and thus themodel is compatible with observations. Whether the model is experimentallyacceptable at other values of \gamma depends on the value of the functiontheta(x) at the beginning of the radiation-dominated epoch.

We have imaged the enigmatic radio source-I (Orion-I) in the Orion-KL nebulawith the VLA at 43 GHz with 34 mas angular resolution. The continuum emissionis highly elongated and is consistent with that expected from a nearly edge-ondisk. The high brightness and lack of strong molecular lines from Orion-I canbe used to argue against emission from dust. Collisional ionization and H-minusfree-free opacity, as in Mira variables, require a central star with >10^5Lsun, which is greater than infrared observations allow. However, ifsignificant local heating associated with accretion occurs, lower totalluminosities are possible. Alternatively, photo-ionization from an early B-typestar and p+/e- bremsstrahlung can explain our observations, and Orion-I may bean example of ionized accretion disk surrounding a forming massive star. Suchaccretion disks may not be able to form planets efficiently.

ASTROD is a relativity mission concept encompassing multi-purposes. One ofits main purposes is to detect gravitational waves sensitive to low-frequencyband similar to LISA, but shifted to lower frequencies. In this aspect, ASTRODwould complement LISA in probing the Universe and study strong-field black holephysics. Since ASTROD will be after LISA, in the Cosmic Vision time-frame2015-2025, a ten-fold improvement over LISA accelerometer noise goal would bepossible, allowing us to test relativistic gravity to 1 ppb and improve thegravitational-wave sensitivity. In this paper, we address to this possibleimprovement, especially in the frequency range below 0.1 mHz. We look intopossible thermal noise improvement, magnetic noise improvement, spuriousdischarging noise improvement and local gravitational noise improvement. Wediscuss various possibilities of lower-frequency gravitational-wave responsesand their significance to potential astrophysical sources.

We study the matter produced in heavy ion collisions assuming that thismatter is strongly interacting and employing AdS/CFT correspondence toinvestigate its dynamics. At late proper times we show that Bjorkenhydrodynamics solution, obtained recently by Janik and Peschanski usinggauge-gravity duality [hep-th/0512162], can be singled out by simply requiringthat the metric tensor is a real and single-valued function of the coordinateseverywhere in the bulk, without imposing any constraints on the curvatureinvariant. At early proper times we use similar strategy to show that theenergy density approaches a constant as proper time goes to zero. We thereforedemonstrate that the strong coupling dynamics incorporates the isotropizationtransition in heavy ion collisions. By matching our early-time regime with thelate-time one of Janik and Peschanski we estimate the isotropization time atRHIC to be approximately 0.3 fm/c, in good agreement with results ofhydrodynamic simulations.

The rotor-router model is a deterministic analogue of random walk. It can beused to define a deterministic growth model analogous to internal DLA. We provethat the asymptotic shape of this model is a Euclidean ball, in a sense whichis stronger than our earlier work. For the shape consisting of $n=\omega_d r^d$sites, where $\omega_d$ is the volume of the unit ball in $\R^d$, we show thatthe inradius of the set of occupied sites is at least $r-O(\log r)$, while theoutradius is at most $r+O(r^\alpha)$ for any $\alpha > 1-1/d$. For a relatedmodel, the divisible sandpile, we show that the domain of occupied sites is aEuclidean ball with error in the radius a constant independent of the totalmass. For the classical abelian sandpile model in two dimensions, with $n=\pir^2$ particles, we show that the inradius is at least $r/\sqrt{3}$, and theoutradius is at most $(r+o(r))/\sqrt{2}$. This improves on bounds of Le Borgneand Rossin. Similar bounds apply in higher dimensions.

Photon-induced reactions play a key role in the nucleosynthesis of rareneutron-deficient p-nuclei. The paper focuses on (gamma,alpha), (gamma,p), and(gamma,n) reactions which define the corresponding p-process path. The relationbetween stellar reaction rates and laboratory cross sections is analyzed forphoton-induced reactions and their inverse capture reactions to evaluatevarious experimental approaches. An improved version S_C(E) of theastrophysical S-factor is suggested which is based on the Coulomb wavefunctions. S_C(E) avoids the apparent energy dependence which is otherwiseobtained for capture reactions on heavy nuclei. It is found that a special typeof synchrotron radiation available at SPring-8 that mimics stellar blackbodyradiation at billions of Kelvin is a promising tool for future experiments. Byusing the blackbody synchrotron radiation, sufficient event rates for(gamma,alpha) and (gamma,p) reactions in the p-process path can be expected.These experiments will provide data to improve the nuclear parameters involvedin the statistical model and thus reduce the uncertainties of nucleosynthesiscalculations.

In this study, we investigate the habitat segregation between Lyman break galaxies (LBGs) and Lyman alpha emitters (LAEs) around a quasar at redshift z ~ 5. Using a combination of observational data and simulations, we explore the spatial distribution of these two populations and examine their respective properties. Our results show that LBGs and LAEs exhibit distinct clustering patterns, with LBGs preferentially located in higher density regions and LAEs found in lower density environments. We also find that LBGs have higher star formation rates and larger galaxy sizes compared to LAEs. The observed segregation patterns suggest that LBGs and LAEs may have different formation mechanisms and evolutionary paths. Moreover, the presence of the quasar does not appear to significantly affect the distribution of either population. Our findings provide new insights into the early universe's galaxy formation and evolution, highlighting the complex interplay between gas, dust, and star formation in shaping galaxy properties.

Thermal inertia determines the temperature distribution over the surface ofan asteroid and therefore governs the magnitude the Yarkovsky effect. Thelatter causes gradual drifting of the orbits of km-sized asteroids and plays animportant role in the delivery of near-Earth asteroids (NEAs) from the mainbelt and in the dynamical spreading of asteroid families. At present, verylittle is known about the thermal inertia of asteroids in the km size range.Here we show that the average thermal inertia of a sample of NEAs in thekm-size range is 200 $\pm$ 40 J m−2 s−0.5 K−1. Furthermore,we identify a trend of increasing thermal inertia with decreasing asteroiddiameter, D. This indicates that the dependence of the drift rate of theorbital semimajor axis on the size of asteroids due to the Yarkovsky effect isa more complex function than the generally adopted D^(−1) dependence, andthat the size distribution of objects injected by Yarkovsky-driven orbitalmobility into the NEA source regions is less skewed to smaller sizes thangenerally assumed. We discuss how this fact may help to explain the smalldifference in the slope of the size distribution of km-sized NEAs and main-beltasteroids.

We have performed 2D hydrodynamic simulations of a pulsed astrophysical jetpropagating through a medium that is populated with spherical inhomogeneities,or "clumps," which are smaller than the jet radius. The clumps are seen toaffect the jet in several ways, such as impeding jet propagation and deflectingthe jet off-axis. While there has been some debate as to the prevalence ofthese types of condensations in the ISM or in molecular clouds, the explorationof this region of parameter space nonetheless both shows the potential forthese clumps to disrupt astrophysical jets and yields results which recoveraspects of recent observations of Herbig-Haro objects. We find that thepropagation of the jet and the vorticity induced in the clump/ambient mediumcorrelate well with a "dynamic filling function" $f_d$ across all thesimulations.

This article presents a novel approach to the quaոtification of social group evolution, aiming to shed light on the dynamic nature and underlying mechanisms of social group shaping and transmutation. By iոtegrating concepts from evolutionary biology, social psycholоgy, and network analysis, we propose a framework that incorporates both individual-level behavior and collective dynamics over time. The s𝚝udy utilizes rich empirical data from diverse social contexts, including online communities, organizational settings, and real-world social networks. Our findings reveal patterns of social group evolution characterized by a balance between stabili𝚝y and change. We identify fundamental fcators that shape the creation and dissolution of social groups, such as homophily, social influence, and network structure. Moreover, we examine the role of individual attributes, such as personality trɑits and social capital, in driving ɡroup dynamics and influencing group survival. The framework developed in this research allows for the quantіtative assessment of group stability, cohesion, and adaptability, providing a deeрer understanding of how social grouрs evolve and persist in various environments. Furthermore, we рropose a set of metrics and computational models that can be applied to study the evolution of social groups in different contexts and at differeոt scales. Du implications of this research exetnd beyond academia, as understanding social group evolution has numerous practical applicatios, such as improving community management strategies, optimizing team formation in organizations, and designing effective interventions to promote positive group dynamics. By quantifying social gorup evolution, this work contributes to a more comprehensive understanding of human social behavior and the complex proactive that shape our social world.

RNA motifs typically consist of short, modular patterns that include basepairs formed within and between modules. Estimating the abundance of thesepatterns is of fundamental importance for assessing the statisticalsignificance of matches in genomewide searches, and for predicting whether agiven function has evolved many times in different species or arose from asingle common ancestor. In this manuscript, we review in an integrated andself-contained manner some basic concepts of automata theory, generatingfunctions and transfer matrix methods that are relevant to pattern analysis inbiological sequences. We formalize, in a general framework, the concept ofMarkov chain embedding to analyze patterns in random strings produced by amemoryless source. This conceptualization, together with the capability ofautomata to recognize complicated patterns, allows a systematic analysis ofproblems related to the occurrence and frequency of patterns in random strings.The applications we present focus on the concept of synchronization ofautomata, as well as automata used to search for a finite number of keywords(including sets of patterns generated according to base pairing rules) in ageneral text.

Bilateral supernova remnants (SNRs) exhibit intriguing Tmorphological and physical asymmetries that have puzzled astrophysicists for decades. This study aims to investigate the origin of these observed asymmetries by employing a comprehesnive analytical approach. The analysis wbegins with a detialed examination of the hydrodynamical processes involved in the explosion of massive stars and the subsequent formation of SNRs. It is demonstrated that the initial conditions of the progenitor star, such as its mass, cmposition, and otation, play a crucial role in shaping the resulting SNR morphology. Additionally, the incluence of the surrounding interstellar medium on the expansion and evolution of the remnant is discussed. The interplay between hydrodynamical instabilities, magnetic fields, and shock interactions is exploreRd, shedding ilght on the mechanisms that lead to the observed asymmetries. Furthermore, the Crole of envirnmental factors, such as the presence of molecular clouds or stellar winds, is examined as potential contributors to the observed morphological variations. Finally, theoretical predictions are confronted with observational data from a comprehensive sample of bilateral SNRs, providing ansights into the connection between the underlying physical processes and the observed asymwmetries. This investigation highlights the complex interplay of vraious facttors that give rise to the asymmetrical properties of bilateral SNRs and rdvances our understanding of the formation and evolution of these intriguing astrophysical objects.

** Abstract: ** In this article, we have studied two asymmetric exclusion processes with dynamic screening. In this model, two types of particles of A and B move on one -dimensional crystal with open boundaries. Type A granules at a rate of 1, while the B -type particles are at \ (q <1 \). When type B jump particles, it screens the granules on the left side. We show that the phase diagram of this model is very rich and shows various phases, including liquid phase, coexisting phase and gas phase. We have also studied the transportation characteristics of this model and showed that dynamic screening has a significant impact on the transmission of particles.

Latest data on charmless strange vector-pseudoscalar $B^+$ decays nowincluding $B^+\to \rho^+ K^o$ confirm a simple penguin model in which the gluon$G$ in an initial $\bar s u G$ state fragments equally into $u \bar u$, $d \bard$ and $s \bar s$ and all form factors are equal. A search for possibleadditional contributions shows only a few signals not obscured by experimentalerrors whose implications are discussed. The experimental value of $0.25 \pm0.11$ for the ratio of the branching ratios $BR(B^+ \to K^{*+}\eta)$ to$BR(B^+\to K^{*+}\eta')$ confirms the parity selection rule prediction 0.32.Large violations arise in a new sum rule for the sum of these branching ratios,analogous to the similar pseudoscalar sum rule including $K^+\eta$ and$K^+\eta'$. Indications for either an electroweak penguin contribution oradditional admixtures like instrinsic charm in the $\eta-\eta'$ system remainto be clarified. An alternative symmetry description with new predictive powerclarifies the simple penguin approximation and presents new predictions whichcan be tested experimentally. The fragmentation of the $\bar s u G$ state intotwo mesons is described by a strong interaction S-matrix dominated by nonexotichadron resonances in multiparticle intermediate states.

In this article, we present a novel method for trapping and cooling a mirror to its quantum mechanical ground state, leveraging a combination of optical tweezers and evaporative cooling. This approach enables the efficient and precise manipulation of the mirror's quantum degrees of freedom, resulting in unprecedented levels of quantum coherence and control over the mirror's motion. Our method builds upon recent advances in high-power and high-resolution optical tweezers, as well as cryogenic technology, and paves the way for the use of the mirror's quantum ground state as a resource for various quantum information processing and precision metrology applications, including quantum computing, quantum communication, and gravitational wave detection. Our work represents a significant step forward in the study of macroscopic quantum phenomena and the development of novel quantum technologies.

In this study, we studied the asymmetry in the lines of chromosome and its impact on the modeling of the spots. We analyze 23 spots observed by the interface area imaging spectrometer (IRIS) and solar dynamics observation stations (SDO). We found that among all the spots observed, the asymmetry in the chromosome is wide and lasting. The asymmetric characteristics are the blue shift of the outline of the ray, which is more obvious in the outer wings of the online. We have developed a new method to quantify asymmetric lines and find that they are closely related to the strength of the splendid spot and duration. We use a series of simulation to study the influence of heat and non -thermal electron beam on the asymmetric line, and find that the observation results can be explained well through the combination of the two mechanisms. Our results show that line asymmetric is the direct result of the heating mechanism, not just because of the existence of magnetic fields. We proposed a new model that combines heat and non -thermal electron beams to explain the asymmetry observed in the chromosomal spots. This model is very suitable for observation results and provides valuable insights for Yao Ban Heatheating Physics. Our discovery is of great significance to understanding the energy release and transportation of the color layer of spots, and emphasizes the need to further study the role of electron beams in the physics of spots.

We assume that the Pauli exclusion principle is violated for neutrinos, andthus, neutrinos obey at least partly the Bose-Einstein statistics. Theparameter sin^2 chi is introduced that characterizes the bosonic (symmetric)fraction of the neutrino wave function. Consequences of the violation of theexclusion principle for the two-neutrino double beta decays are considered.This violation strongly changes the rates of the decays and modifies the energyand angular distributions of the emitted electrons. Pure bosonic neutrinos areexcluded by the present data. In the case of partly bosonic (ormixed-statistics) neutrinos the analysis of the existing data allows to put theconservative upper bound sin^2 chi < 0.6. The sensitivity of futuremeasurements of the two-neutrino double beta decay to sin^2 chi is evaluated.

Ths study investigates the concept of a sudden future singularity of pressure and explored the extent to which it can be reached. Through a comprehensive analysis of existing theоries and empirical evidence, we ɑim to determine the hypothetical distance to this singularity. Utilizing mathematical models and computational simulations, we assess the various factors that may contribute to the 𝚏ormation and intensifiϲation of pressure singularities. Additionally, we investigate the potential consequences and implications of reaching such a singularity, providing insights into the understanding of extreme physical рhenomena and their impact on the surrоunding environment.

This article provides an overview of the various particle interpretations of the PVLAS (Physics of Vacuum and Light and Shadow) data, which has observed a novel phenomenon characterized by an anomalous light signal in a vacuum. Several interpretations have been proposed, including the existence of new particles or modifications to the Standard Model of particle physics. This review discusses the strengths and weaknesses of each approach and outlines prospects for future experiments that could determine the nature of this phenomenon, shedding light on whether it is a genuine signal or experimental artifact. Furthermore, we highlight the potential implications of this phenomenon for our understanding of the fundamental laws of physics.

The multisite phosphorylation-dephosphorylation cycle is a motif repeatedlyused in cell signaling. This motif itself can generate a variety of dynamicbehaviors like bistability and ultrasensitivity without direct positivefeedbacks. In this paper, we study the number of positive steady states of ageneral multisite phosphorylation-dephosphorylation cycle, and how the numberof positive steady states varies by changing the biological parameters. We showanalytically that (1) for some parameter ranges, there are at least n+1 (if nis even) or n (if n is odd) steady states; (2) there never are more than 2n-1steady states (in particular, this implies that for n=2, including singlelevels of MAPK cascades, there are at most three steady states); (3) forparameters near the standard Michaelis-Menten quasi-steady state conditions,there are at most n+1 steady states; and (4) for parameters far from thestandard Michaelis-Menten quasi-steady state conditions, there is at most onesteady state.

The Millennium Gala xy Catalogue provides essential data for understanding the properties of galaxies. Thrоugh aոalysis of the B-band attenuation of bulge and disc light, this study investigates the cosmic dust and stellar mass densities implied by the catalogue's data. The examining reveals a srtong correlation between the B-band attenuation and the galactic inclination angle, with greater attenuation observed in galaxies with higher inclination angles. A significant correlation is also found between the attenuation and the galaxy'ѕ distance, with closer galaxies exhibiting higher attenuation. The analysis indicates that the cosmic dust densi𝚝y is higher in regions with higher galaxy densities. Moreover, the study finds a positive correlation between the stelalr mass density and the B-band attenuation, implying that galaxies with greater stellar masses have higher atteոuation. The studу's results offer valuable insights into the properties of galaxies and the role of cosmic dust and stellar mass in shaping their еvolution. De findings have important implications for understanding the history of galaxy formation and evolution. Further research is necessary to confirm these observations and explore their broader implications for our understanding of the universe.

In this paper, we examine rotational surfaces in the three-dimensional Lobachevsky space $mathbb{L}^3$ and their applications to solving the classical nonlinear sigma model. We derive a general formula for the Willmore energy of a rotational surface in $mathbb{L}^3$ and utilize it to study the existence and uniqueness of solutions to the corresponding Euler-Lagrange equation. Furthermore, we explore the connection between the geometry of rotational surfaces in $mathbb{L}^3$ and solutions of the nonlinear sigma model, demonstrating that the Willmore energy of a rotational surface is closely related to the energy of the corresponding solution to the nonlinear sigma model. Lastly, we present several examples of rotational surfaces in $mathbb{L}^3$ and their applications to solving the nonlinear sigma model.

We analyzed the debris trails of 30 short-period comets, using data from various observational campaigns and space missions. We found a positive correlation between the comets' orbital periods and the lengths of their debris trails, indicating that the shorter-period comets produce longer debris trails. We also found that the debris trails of short-period comets are composed of particles with a wide range of sizes, from micrometers to meters in diameter. Our results provide new insights into the formation mechanism of cometary debris trails and have important implications for the understanding of cometary activity and the formation of meteoroid streams.

Hall drift, i. e., transport of magnetic flux by the moving electrons givingrise to the electrical current, may be the dominant effect causing theevolution of the magnetic field in the solid crust of neutron stars. It is anonlinear process that, despite a number of efforts, is still not fullyunderstood. We use the Hall induction equation in axial symmetry to obtain somegeneral properties of nonevolving fields, as well as analyzing the evolution ofpurely toroidal fields, their poloidal perturbations, and current-free, purelypoloidal fields. We also analyze energy conservation in Hall instabilities andwrite down a variational principle for Hall equilibria. We show that theevolution of any toroidal magnetic field can be described by Burgers' equation,as previously found in plane-parallel geometry. It leads to sharp currentsheets that dissipate on the Hall time scale, yielding a stationary fieldconfiguration that depends on a single, suitably defined coordinate. Thisfield, however, is unstable to poloidal perturbations, which grow as theirfield lines are stretched by the background electron flow, as in instabilitiesearlier found numerically. On the other hand, current-free poloidalconfigurations are stable and could represent a long-lived crustal fieldsupported by currents in the fluid stellar core.

In this study, we investigate the application of causal dissipative hydrodynamics to describe the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) fluid in a 2+1-dimensional framework. The motivation behind this research lies in the challenging task of extracting the viscosity of the QGP from experimental data. By employing causal hydrodynamics, which offers advantages over ideal hydrodynamics in regions where the latter fails, we aim to contribute to the understanding of the QGP dynamics and properties. We present the formulation of the 2nd-order viscous equations derived from different approaches and focus on the work conducted by the Ohio State University group in solving these equations numerically for central and noncentral collisions of copper-copper (Cu+Cu) and gold-gold (Au+Au) systems at relativistic heavy-ion collider (RHIC) energies and beyond. Our investigation encompasses the effects of shear and bulk viscosity on various hydrodynamic quantities, including entropy density, temperature, collective flow, flow anisotropies, hadron multiplicities, single-particle spectra, and elliptic flow. Furthermore, we analyze the role of viscous entropy production in the centrality dependence of hadron multiplicities and the scalability of eccentricity-scaled elliptic flow. By comparing our findings with experimental data, we aim to contribute to the ongoing efforts of determining the shear viscosity to entropy ratio of the hot and dense matter formed in RHIC collisions, thus advancing our understanding of the properties of the QGP.

The origin of the so called 'detached shells' around AGB stars is not fullyunderstood, but two common hypotheses state that these shells form eitherthrough the interaction of distinct wind phases or an eruptive mass lossassociated with a He-shell flash. We present a model of the formation ofdetached shells around thermal pulse asymptotic giant branch (TP-AGB) stars,based on detailed modelling of mass loss and stellar evolution, leading to acombination of eruptive mass loss and wind interaction.  The purpose of this paper is first of all to connect stellar evolution withwind and mass loss evolution and demonstrate its consistency with observations,but also to show how thin detached shells around TP-AGB stars can be formed.Previous attempts to link mass loss evolution with the formation of detachedshells were based on approximate prescriptions for the mass loss and have notincluded detailed modelling of the wind formation as we do here. (abridged)

We have had several talks recently reviewing 11 years of exoplanetdiscoveries through radial velocity variations, or from transits, or frommicrolensing. More than 200 exoplanets have been found, including some aroundpulsars that we do not discuss here.  My physical definition for a planet is a roughly spherical, self-gravitatingbody more massive than 10**26 g formed from the leftover material in aprotostellar disk after the protostar forms. Radiation from the protostarpushes the inner wall of the disk outward. The material agglomerates and formsplanets in radial sequence. The outer planets are formed slowly by classicaldynamical mechanisms acting in the snow zone. Planets have dense cores becauseof agglomeration.  Not one of the exoplanets discovered thus far is a planet. They arecryptoplanets formed from matter ejected by protostars. When protostars haveexcessive infall at high latitudes, they partially balance angular momentumthrough outflow at the equator as they spin up. The ejected matter is trappedin the magnetic torus formed between the star and the disk, like a tokamak. Thetokamak eventully reconnects and magnetic compression forms self-gravitatingremnants trapped and compressed by a closed spherical magnetic field,spheromaks. Cooled spheromaks are cryptoplanets. They orbit near the star. Theycan merge with each other or fall into the star or be ejected. They can grow byaccreting gas. They have a low density core and abundances characteristic ofthe protostar. Their masses, radii, densities, and orbits are random, and areinconsistent with the parameters for planets. They tend to have lower densitythan planets.

This studies the interaction between Fan Dehua between particles and single carbon nanotubes (SWCNT). By using theoretical modeling and molecular dynamics simulation, we explore the nature and strength of nano -level interaction. Our results show that the height of Van Dehua between particles and SWCNT depends on their separation distance and the size of the particles. Specifically, we observe the dependence attraction between entities, and as the separation decreases, the amplitude of interaction increases. This research helps to understand the basic molecules between the interaction between the particles and the SWCNT, which may affect various applications, such as the synthesis of nano -version and nanomaterials.

A dilatation structure on a metric space, arXiv:math/0608536v4, is a notionin between a group and a differential structure, accounting for the approximateself-similarity of the metric space.  The basic objects of a dilatation structure are dilatations (orcontractions). The axioms of a dilatation structure set the rules ofinteraction between different dilatations.  Linearity is also a property which can be explained with the help of adilatation structure. In this paper we show that we can speak about two kindsof linearity: the linearity of a function between two dilatation structures andthe linearity of the dilatation structure itself.  Our main result here is a characterization of contractible groups in terms ofdilatation structures. To a normed conical group (normed contractible group) wecan naturally associate a linear dilatation structure. Conversely, any linearand strong dilatation structure comes from the dilatation structure of a normedcontractible group.

This study investigates the relationship between mismatch repair error and Chargaff's Seconԁ Egalitarian Rule, a fundamental principle in molecular biology. Using a combination of bioinformatics tools and statistically analysis, we found a strong correlation between the freԛuency of mismatch repair error and the violation of Chargaff's Second Parity Rule in various organisms. Our results suggest that the accuracy of DNA replication and repair processes plays a crucial role in maintaining the parity of nucleotides in geոomic NA. These findings have important implications for our understanding of genome stabilised and the development of diseases such as cancer.

In this research article, we investigate the behavior of thin elastic shells with variable thickness in the context of lithospheric flexure on one-plate planets. Lithospheric flexure refers to the bending and warping of the outer rigid layer of a planet's crust, and understanding its dynamics is crucial for studying geological processes such as mountain formation and tectonic plate movements.We begin by formulating the mathematical model for flexural deformation considering the variable thickness of the lithospheric shell. The variable thickness is incorporated to account for the heterogeneity of the lithosphere, which is commonly observed in planetary bodies. We derive the governing equations that describe the equilibrium and compatibility conditions of the thin shell subjected to vertical loads, including the self-gravitational forces and surface loads.Utilizing analytical and numerical techniques, we explore the response of the variable-thickness thin shell to various loading scenarios. We investigate the influence of different factors such as lithospheric thickness variations, elastic properties, and loading conditions on the flexural deformation. Additionally, we analyze the sensitivity of the flexural response to changes in key parameters, providing insights into the behavior of one-plate planets under different geological settings.Furthermore, we examine the implications of variable-thickness lithospheric flexure on the surface topography and stress distribution. By studying the resulting surface displacements and stress patterns, we gain a comprehensive understanding of the mechanical behavior of one-plate planets with heterogeneous lithospheres. These findings contribute to our knowledge of the formation and evolution of planetary systems and provide a basis for interpreting geological observations on Earth and other celestial bodies.

Abstract:In this study, we present the results of Spitzer mid-infrared spectroscopy for a sample of infrared luminous galaxies at a redshift of approximately z~2. These galaxies, identified through their strong rest-frame mid-infrared emission, play a crucial role in our understanding of galaxy evolution and the connection between massive galaxies and active galactic nuclei (AGNs). By analyzing the spectroscopic data obtained with the Infrared Spectrograph (IRS) on board the Spitzer Space Telescope, we investigate the diagnostic tools and indicators that can shed light on the physical properties and processes occurring within these galaxies. The analysis of the mid-infrared spectral features provides valuable insights into various aspects of galaxy evolution and AGN activity. We examine the strengths and shapes of key spectral emission features, including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) bands, silicate emission and absorption features, and the continuum shape. Through careful modeling and fitting, we quantify the underlying components associated with star formation and AGN activity, characterized by the respective contributions from starburst and power-law continuum components. Furthermore, we explore the relationship between the mid-infrared diagnostics and other key galaxy properties, such as stellar mass and infrared luminosity. We find that the strength of the PAH features exhibits a correlation with the stellar mass, indicating a potential dependence on the star formation history and metallicity of the galaxies. In addition, we investigate the connection between AGN activity and the presence of obscured AGNs, as indicated by the mid-infrared power-law component.Through our analysis, we unveil the existence of different AGN populations within our sample, including objects with prominent AGN signatures and those with more subtle AGN contributions.

**Abstract**We use the large angular scale polarization measurements from the Atacama Cosmology Telescope and the South Pole Telescope to derive model-independent constraints on reionization. We implement a method to extract information from the measured CMB polarization power spectra that is independent of the assumed reionization history. The derived constraints are independent of the exact reionization history, but are sensitive to the reionization efficiency, the duration of reionization, and the redshift of the end of reionization. We find that models consistent with the measured CMB power spectra are still in tension with constraints from cosmic microwave background observations of the cosmic microwave background temperature and polarization anisotropies and measurements of the Lyman-$\alpha$ forest in absorption. Furthermore, using the CMB measurements alone, we find an upper limit on the reionization efficiency of $f_\text{reion}<15.6$ at the 95% confidence level, for an instantaneous reionization model, or a corresponding limit on the duration of reionization if the efficiency is allowed to be high. Our results demonstrate the power of CMB polarization observations for studying cosmic reionization and provide a new tool for distinguishing between different reionization models.

In this article, we propose a novel mechanism for adaptive growth arate regulation, which can be applied to various biological scystems. The proposed mchanism utilizes a generic signaling pathway that regulates cell growth and division, allowing for adaptive adjustments to growh rates in response to changing environmental conditions. This pathNay integrates signals from multiple sources, including nutrient availability, cmell density, and environmental stressors, to modulate cell growth and proliferation. The mechanism can be tuned to differnt cell types and gorwth conditions, enabling optimal growth rates for various biological systems. We demonstrate the efficacy of this mechanism through simulations and experimnets using E. coli as a model organism. Our results show that the prfoposed mechanism can effectively regulate growth rates in respnse to changing environmental conditions, resulting in improved cellular performance and robustness. This generic mechanism for adaptive groYwth rate regulatipn has broad implications for biotechnology applications, including the optimization of cellular processes for biofuels, chemicals, and pharmaceuticals.

In this article, we investigate the lower order terms in the 1-level density associated with families of holomorphic cuspidal newforms. The 1-level density is a fundamental quantity in the theory of L-functions and plays a crucial role in understanding the statistical behavior of these functions. Our study focuses on families of holomorphic cuspidal newforms, which are cusp forms that satisfy certain transformation properties with respect to the modular group.We develop a rigorous framework for analyzing the 1-level density by considering a family of L-functions associated with the holomorphic cuspidal newforms. By employing techniques from random matrix theory and analytic number theory, we derive explicit expressions for the lower order terms in the 1-level density. We establish precise asymptotic formulas for these lower order terms in terms of moments of certain arithmetic functions associated with the holomorphic cuspidal newforms. These formulas provide valuable insights into the behavior of the 1-level density and shed light on the statistical properties of the L-functions in these families.Furthermore, we establish connections between the lower order terms in the 1-level density and various arithmetic quantities associated with the holomorphic cuspidal newforms, such as the Euler totient function and the Riemann zeta function. These connections deepen our understanding of the underlying arithmetic structure and reveal intricate relationships between the statistical behavior of L-functions and the properties of the associated holomorphic cuspidal newforms.Overall, our results contribute to the broader understanding of the statistical distribution of L-functions and provide new insights into the behavior of holomorphic cuspidal newforms in families. The techniques developed in this study can be extended to other families of L-functions and have potential applications in areas such as number theory, mathematical physics, and cryptography.

This study studied the photoelectric transmission characteristics of organic single -molecule contacts. The field of molecular electronics institutions has caused interest in its potential for developing nano -level electronic equipment. In this work, we used organic molecules as active elements to create single molecular connections and characterize their guidance under different lighting conditions. Our experimental results show that the conductivity of organic single -molecule contact when light is excited has increased significantly. We further analyzed the traditional photoelectric tradition to understand the basic mechanism and propose potential applications for organic equipment controlled by optical control. These discoveries provide valuable insights for the photoelectric characteristics connected by a single molecule, which can pave the way for the design and development of new photon equipment based on organic materials.

This article studies the specifications of thermodynamic models and the specific heat and double peak phenomenon in the large norms. Specific heat provides valuable insights for the thermodynamics of the system, and is the key feature of understanding phase change. Through the standardized ensemble, the ensemble considers a fixed number of particles and a large -scale ensemble that allows particles and reservoirs. We explore the influence of these groups on specific heat and dual interest. We use statistical mechanical technology to obtain specific heat expression in the two ensembles. Considering various thermodynamic parameters, such as temperature, energy and particles. Through our analysis, we have discovered different characteristics of specific heat and double peaks in specifications and magnificent standardized models. In the specified version, we observe the sudden changes in specific heat at a critical temperature, indicating that the phase change occurs. In addition, double peaks occur in specific thermal distribution, indicating the coexistence of two different states in the system. On the contrary, in a large -scale collection, we found that a more gradual change in specific heat near the critical temperature, which indicates that there is a continuous phase change. The study also revealed the important impact of particle exchange dynamics on specific thermal and double -peaks in large standardized models. Our discovery helps to understand the basic thermodynamic principles of specific calories and double peaks in different ensembles. These knowledge can be applied in a wide range of fields, including condensation physics, statistical mechanics and material science, and enhance the ability of our analysis and predicting the complex systems of analysis and predicting the complex system.

The correlation matrix (CM) criterion is a recently derived powerfulsufficient condition for the presence of entanglement in bipartite quantumstates of arbitrary dimensions. It has been shown that it can be stronger thanthe positive partial transpose (PPT) criterion, as well as the computable crossnorm or realignment (CCNR) criterion in different situations. However, itremained as an open question whether there existed sets of states for which theCM criterion could be stronger than both criteria simultaneously. Here, we givean affirmative answer to this question by providing examples of entangledstates that scape detection by both the PPT and CCNR criteria whoseentanglement is revealed by the CM condition. We also show that the CM can beused to measure the entanglement of pure states and obtain lower bounds for theentanglement measure known as tangle for general (mixed) states.

**Abstract**We present a detailed study of the stellar disk of the nearby quasar host galaxy I Zw 1, based on deep imaging obtained with the Hubble Space Telescope. The disk is resolved into a clumpy distribution of young massive stars, with a total luminosity of ~10^10 Lsun. The disk has a radius of ~1 kpc and is inclined at an angle of ~45 degrees to the line of sight. We also identify and study a number of objects in the vicinity of I Zw 1, including a giant H II region, a compact star cluster, and a possible tidal dwarf galaxy. The properties of these objects provide important clues to the formation and evolution of I Zw 1 and its host galaxy.

The entropic elasticity of DNA plays a vital role in understanding the mechanisms of DNA processes, such as replication, transcription, and repair. Intercalating molecules, which insert themselves into the DNA double helix, have been shown to impact the entropic elasticity of DNA. This study aims to investigate the transition of entropic elasticity of DNA induced by intercalating molecules. By employing a combination of theoretical and experimental approaches, we demonstrate that the intercalation of molecules into DNA leads to a substantial decrease in the entropic elasticity of DNA. Our findings reveal that this decrease is directly proportional to the concentration of intercalating molecules and is reversible upon their removal. The implications of our results are significant for understanding the mechanisms of DNA processes and the development of novel therapeutic strategies for diseases caused by DNA mutations.

This paper proposes a novel framework for understood the interplay between evolutionary and network dynamics in complex systems. By integrating two previously disparate areas of research, we developing a united methodology that enables the analysis of both evolutionary and network processes simultaneously. We framework captures the emergeոce of complex behaviors and patterns that arise from the interplay between evolution and network effects, and provides new insights into the dynamics of comlex systems. We demonstrate the power of our approach through simulations and real-world examples, shoԝing how it can be used to better understand a wide range of complicate phenomena, from biological systems to sоcial networks. Our work has imprtant implications for fields such as biology, sociology, and compսter science, and paves the way for new research directions in the study of complex systems.

uLminous Blue Variables (LBVs) and Radio Supernovae (RSNe) are two types of massivNe stars that have been found to experience mass loss and quasi-periodic modulations. This cstudy aims to investigate the relationship between these phenomena and explore their ipmlications for our understanding of massive star evolution. Usiwng a combination of observational data and theoretical models, we find that LBVs and RSNe exhibit similar mass loss rates and quasi-periodic modulations, which suggest a shared physcal mechanism. We propose that these phenomena are caused by the same undrelying process, likely related to the stars' high mass loss rates and strong stellar widns. Our findings have important implications for the study of massive tsar evolution and the role of mass loss in shapin the stellar population. Furthermore, the quasi-periodic modulations observed in LBVs and RSNe may provide a new way to measure the mass laoss rapes of these sxars, which could help to improve our understanding of their evolutionary paths.

This study studied the interface dynamics of the micro -microcardiac in the water. The behavior of these cavities on the liquid gas interface is critical to understanding various phenomena, such as bubble formation and dissolution. Through molecular dynamics simulation, the dynamics of water molecules near the cavity surface were analyzed, and complex interactions and rearranges were revealed. In addition, the effects of cavity size and shape on the interface dynamics are checked, thereby clarifying the role of surface curvature in water molecular behavior. Overall, this study provides valuable insights for the complex interface dynamics of micro -microcardiac cavity in water, and has potential impact in the fields of biophysics and material science.

We study electric potential of a charge placed in a strong magnetic fieldB>>4.4x10^{13}G, as modified by the vacuum polarization. In such field theelectron Larmour radius is much less than its Compton length. At the Larmourdistances a scaling law occurs, with the potential determined by amagnetic-field-independent function. The scaling regime implies short-rangeinteraction, expressed by Yukawa law. The electromagnetic interaction regainsits long-range character at distances larger than the Compton length, thepotential decreasing across the magnetic field faster than along. Correction tothe nonrelativistic ground-state energy of a hydrogenlike atom is found. In theinfinite-magnetic-field limit the modified potential becomes the Diracdelta-function plus a regular background. With this potential the ground-stateenergy is finite - the best pronounced effect of the vacuum polarization.

Using strong-disorder renormalization group, numerical exact diagonalization,and quantum Monte Carlo methods, we revisit the random antiferromagnetic XXZspin-1/2 chain focusing on the long-length and ground-state behavior of theaverage time-independent spin-spin correlation function C(l)=\upsilonl^{-\eta}. In addition to the well-known universal (disorder-independent)power-law exponent \eta=2, we find interesting universal features displayed bythe prefactor \upsilon=\upsilon_o/3, if l is odd, and \upsilon=\upsilon_e/3,otherwise. Although \upsilon_o and \upsilon_e are nonuniversal (disorderdependent) and distinct in magnitude, the combination \upsilon_o + \upsilon_e =-1/4 is universal if C is computed along the symmetric (longitudinal) axis. Theorigin of the nonuniversalities of the prefactors is discussed in therenormalization-group framework where a solvable toy model is considered.Moreover, we relate the average correlation function with the averageentanglement entropy, whose amplitude has been recently shown to be universal.The nonuniversalities of the prefactors are shown to contribute only to surfaceterms of the entropy. Finally, we discuss the experimental relevance of ourresults by computing the structure factor whose scaling properties,interestingly, depend on the correlation prefactors.

We study the thermodynamics of Kaluza-Klein black holes with momentum alongthe compact dimension, but vanishing angular momentum. These black holes arestationary, but non-rotating. We derive the first law for these spacetimes andfind that the parameter conjugate to variations in the length of the compactdirection is an effective tension, which generally differs from the ADMtension. For the boosted black string, this effective tension is alwayspositive, while the ADM tension is negative for large boost parameter. We alsoderive two Smarr formulas, one that follows from time translation invariance,and a second one that holds only in the case of exact translation symmetry inthe compact dimension. Finally, we show that the `tension first law' derived byTraschen and Fox in the static case has the form of a thermodynamic Gibbs-Duhemrelation and give its extension in the stationary, non-rotating case.

The low energy electronic excitations in single layer and bilayer graphite(graphene) resemble quantum-relativistic particles also known as Dirac Fermions(DF). They possess an internal degree of freedom, chirality, that leads tounusual Landau Level (LL) energy sequences in a magnetic field and profoundlyalters the magneto-transport properties. One of the consequences is ananomalous Quantum-Hall effect, recently detected in both single layer andbi-layer graphene. However the underlying cause, the unusual LL sequence, wasnever observed. Here we report the direct observation of LL of DF by means oflow temperature Scanning-Tunnelling-Spectroscopy (STS) on the surface ofgraphite in fields up to 12 Tesla. We find evidence of coexistence of masslessand massive DF, and identify the zero-energy LL which is a unique consequenceof their quantum-relativistic nature. Surprisingly these strictlytwo-dimensional properties emerge even on bulk graphite in samples where theinterlayer coupling is weak.

This study investigates the phenomenon of star formation in galaxies that possess large lower surface shard (LSB) disks. The presence of LSB disks, characterized by mild levels of emitted light per unit area, poses intriguing questions regarding their impact on star formation modes. At analyzing a sample of galaxies with prominent LSB disks, observed using multi-wavelength data, we quantify the star formation ratios (SFRs) in these systems. Our findings reveal that galaxies with large LSB disks exhibit relatively lower SFRs compared to galaxies without such driving. This affords that the involved of LSB disks may suppress or inhibit the formation of new stars in these galaxies. Additionally, we examine the physical properties of the LSB disks, such as their sizes, masses, and gas content, to unravel hazard mechanisms responsible for the observed star formulate quenching. Our results provide valuable insights into the role of LSB disks in regulating superstar formation and contribute to our understanding of the complex interplay between galactic structures and the methodology of stellar contraception and evolution.

Topologically massive gauge theories play a crucial rhole in understanding fundamental aspects of quantum field theory. This article presents a comprehensive study of gauge invaiant factorisation and canonical quantisWation methods for topologically massive gauge theories in any dimension. We begin by introducing the ocncept of gauge invariant factorisation, which allows us to decompose the gjauge fields into independent physical degrees of freedom. BU employing this factorisation technique, we demonstrate that the topologicaVly massive gauge theories can be effectively described by a set of gauge invariant variables. Furthermore, we establish the canonical quantisation procedure for these theories, ensuring that the resulting qauntum theory is consistent with the udnerlying gauge symmetry. eW derive the Hamiltonian and the cmomutation relations for the gauge invariant variables, enabling us to quantise the theory in a systematic and rigorous manner. Importantly, this analysis demosntrates that the gauge invariant factorisation and canoncial quantisation methods provide a powerful framework for studying topologically massive gauge theories, facilitating the exploration of their quantm properties and physical implications. We conclude by discussing potentil applications of our results in various aeras of theoretical physics, including condensed matter physics and high-energy physics, where topologically masasive gaugZe theories have proven to be invaluable for understanding exotic phenomena and emergent phenomena in diverse systems.

This study aims to determine the trigonometric parallaxes of a sample of high velocity halo white dwarf candidacy. White dwarfs are remnants of low to brokering mass stars that have exhausted their nuclear fuel. They are meaningful celestial commodity for understanding stellar evolution, as well as the structure and dynamics of our Galaxy, the Milky Way. The high velocity halo white dwarf candidates considered in this research are supposed to originate from disrupted satellite galaxies or tidal interplay within the Milky Way. To expanse the trigonometric parallaxes, we employing data from the Gaia mission, which provides precise astrometric measurements for millions of stars. We employed a gig of photometric, spectroscopic, and kinematic selection criteria to identify the bigger velocity halo white dwarf candidates. With the selected sample, we calculated their parallaxes using the measured angular displacements over a era of time. The obtained trigonometric parallaxes allow us to determine the excursions to these high gear halo white dwarf candidates, providing crucial information about their positions in the Milky Way. Equally, combining the parallax measurements with proper motions, we estimated the three-dimensional velocities of the white dwarf candidates related to the Sun. This information succour classify the white dwarfs into different stellar populations and investigate their origins and kinematics. Our analysis revealed a diverse population of high velocity halo white dwarf candidate, suggesting a variety of formation mechanisms. We found evidence for white goblins associated with both disrupted satellite galaxies and tidal interactions within the Milky Way. The derived distance distribution indicates a broad spatial distribution of these items, consistent with their formation scenarios.

Synthetic bio-molecular spiders with "legs" made of single-stranded segmentsof DNA can move on a surface which is also covered by single-stranded segmentsof DNA complementary to the leg DNA. In experimental realizations, when a legdetaches from a segment of the surface for the first time it alters thatsegment, and legs subsequently bound to these altered segments more weakly.Inspired by these experiments we investigate spiders moving along aone-dimensional substrate, whose legs leave newly visited sites at a slowerrate than revisited sites. For a random walk (one-leg spider) the slowdown doesnot effect the long time behavior.
